abstract: Some Bravais lattices have a particular geometry that can slow down the motion of Bloch electrons by pre-localization due to the band-structure properties. Another known source of electronic localization in solids is the Coulomb repulsion in partially filled d or f orbitals, which leads to the formation of local magnetic moments. The combination of these two effects is usually considered of little relevance to strongly correlated materials. Here we show that it represents, instead, the underlying physical mechanism in two of the most important ferromagnets: nickel and iron. In nickel, the van Hove singularity has an unexpected impact on the magnetism. As a result, the electron–electron scattering rate is linear in temperature, in violation of the conventional Landau theory of metals. This is true even at Earth’s core pressures, at which iron is instead a good Fermi liquid. The importance of nickel in models of geomagnetism may have therefore to be reconsidered.
news_body: Without a magnetic field life on Earth would be rather uncomfortable: Cosmic particles would pass through our atmosphere in large quantities and damage the cells of all living beings. Technical systems would malfunction frequently and electronic components could be destroyed completely in some cases. Despite its huge significance for life on our planet, it is still not fully known what creates the Earth's magnetic field. There are various theories regarding its origin, but a lot of experts consider them to be insufficient or flawed. A discovery made by scientists from Würzburg might provide a new explanatory angle. Their findings were published in the current issue of the journal Nature Communications. Accordingly, the key to the effect could be hidden in the special structure of the element nickel. Contradiction between theory and reality "The standard models for Earth's magnetic field use values for the electric and thermal conductivity of the metals inside our planet's core that cannot square with reality," Giorgio Sangiovanni says; he is a professor at the Institute for Theoretical Physics and Astrophysics at the University of Würzburg. Together with PhD student Andreas Hausoel and postdoc Michael Karolak, he is in charge of the international collaboration that was published recently. Among the participants are Alessandro Toschi and Karsten Held of TU Wien, who are long-term cooperation partners of Giorgio Sangiovanni, and scientists from Hamburg, Halle (Saale) and Yekaterinburg in Russia. At Earth's centre at a depth of about 6,400 km, there is a temperature of 6,300 degrees Celsius and a pressure of about 3.5 million bars. The predominant elements, iron and nickel, form a solid metal ball under these conditions which makes up the inner core of the Earth. This inner core is surrounded by the outer core, a fluid layer composed mostly of iron and nickel. Flowing of liquid metal in the outer core can intensify electric currents and create Earth's magnetic field – at least according to the common geodynamo theory. "But the theory is somewhat contradictory," Giorgio Sangiovanni says. Band-structure induced correlation effects "This is because at room temperature iron differs significantly from common metals such as copper or gold due to its strong effective electron-electron interaction. It is strongly correlated," he declares. But the effects of electron correlation are attenuated considerably at the extreme temperatures prevailing in Earth's core so that conventional theories are applicable. These theories then predict a much too high thermal conductivity for iron which is at odds with the geodynamo theory. With nickel things are different. "We found nickel to exhibit a distinct anomaly at very high temperatures," the physicist explains. "Nickel is also a strongly correlated metal. Unlike iron, this is not due to the electron-electron interaction alone, but is mainly caused by the special band structure of nickel. We baptised the effect 'band-structure induced correlation'." The band structure of a solid is only determined by the geometric layout of the atoms in the lattice and by the atom type. Iron and nickel in Earth's core "At room temperature, iron atoms will arrange in a way that the corresponding atoms are located at the corners of an imaginary cube with one central atom at the centre of the cube, forming a so-called bcc lattice structure," Andreas Hausoel adds. But as temperature and pressure increase, this structure changes: The atoms move together more closely and form a hexagonal lattice, which physicists refer to as an hcp lattice. As a result, iron looses most of its correlated properties. But not so with nickel: "In this metal, the atoms are as densely packed as possible in the cube structure already in the normal state. They keep this layout even when temperature and pressure become very large," Hausoel explains. The unusual physical behaviour of nickel under extreme conditions can only be explained by the interaction of this geometric stability and the electron correlations originating from this geometry. Despite the fact that scientists have neglected nickel so far, it seems to play a major role in Earth's magnetic field. Decisive hint from geophysics The goings-on inside Earth's core are not the actual focus of research at the Departments of Theoretical Solid-state Physics of the University of Würzburg. Rather Sangiovanni, Hausoel and their colleagues concentrate on the properties of strongly correlated electrons at low temperatures. They study quantum effects and so-called multi-particle effects which are interesting for the next generation of data processing and energy storage devices. Superconductors and quantum computers are the keywords in this context. Data from experiments are not used in this kind of research. "We take the known properties of atoms as input, include the insights from quantum mechanics and try to calculate the behaviour of large clusters of atoms with this," Hausoel says. Because such calculations are highly complex, the scientists have to rely on external support such as the SUPERMUC supercomputer at the Leibniz Supercomputing Centre (LRZ) in Garching. And what's the Earth's core got to do with this? "We wanted to see how stable the novel magnetic properties of nickel are and found them to survive even very high temperatures," Hausoel says. Discussions with geophysicists and further studies of iron-nickel alloys have shown that these discoveries could be relevant for what is happening inside Earth's core.
generated_news_body:
**Title: New Insights into Electron Behavior in Nickel and Iron Challenge Traditional Theories**

Recent research unveils surprising new insights into two of the most significant ferromagnetic materials, nickel and iron, potentially reshaping our understanding of their roles in both basic science and Earth's geophysics.

Scientists have discovered that certain properties of nickel's atomic structure can significantly slow down the motion of electrons. This unexpected behavior is linked to a geometric feature known as the "van Hove singularity," which influences how electrons distribute and interact at the atomic level. As a result, electrons in nickel experience a unique scattering process that defies long-standing theoretical expectations. Instead of following the conventional Landau theory of metals, which predicts electron scattering rates as quadratic in temperature, nickel exhibits a linear relationship between electron scattering and temperature.

Iron, another cornerstone element known to generate Earth's magnetic field, does not display these unusual characteristics under similar conditions. Even when subjected to the intense pressures found at the Earth's core, iron's electrons behave according to the well-understood Fermi liquid theory, making iron a stable and predictable player in current models. 

The findings suggest that the interplay between lattice structures and electron interactions in nickel may also need a reevaluation in its contribution to geomagnetism. Previously underestimated or considered irrelevant, this physical mechanism involving both lattice-induced slowing of electrons and electron-electron interactions might indeed be crucial to how these metals operate under extreme conditions, such as those found within the Earth.

This groundbreaking discovery could lead to revisions in the scientific models that describe geomagnetic phenomena and other applications involving these ferromagnetic materials. Understanding these nuances may refine predictions about Earth's magnetic behavior and the application of these metals in technology.

As researchers delve deeper, the study raises intriguing questions about the fundamental physical characteristics of familiar elements like nickel and iron, emphasizing the complexity and nuances of their behaviors. This could not only reshape academic perspectives but also practical approaches in industries relying on ferromagnetic materials.

paper_body: Abstract This study examines the radicalization of Islamic thought from Ibn Taymiyyah in the thirteenth century, to the emergence of Wahhabism in the eighteenth century, followed by twentieth century radical ideologues such as Mawdudi, Qutb, and Azzam, to explain how terrorist organizations like Al Qaeda, Hamas, and Palestinian Islamic Jihad have rejected mainstream Islamic jurisprudence and crafted a complete reinterpretation of religious doctrine in an attempt to legitimate female suicide bombings. This examination of women suicide bombers provides a clear example of how what is often called Islamic radicalism or political Islam has far less connection with religion than it does with utilitarian and pragmatic concerns such as strategic and military tactics. Access provided by DEAL DE / Springer Compact Clearingstelle Uni Freiburg _ Working on a manuscript? Avoid the common mistakes Introduction The use of female suicide bombers in Palestine and Iraq is a phenomenon that has spurred considerable academic inquiry, but little effort has been made to trace the doctrinal evolution of female suicide bombers in Islam. This study follows the development of radical Islamic interpretation, examining how it deviates from mainstream Islamic jurisprudence, and how it has come to be reinterpreted, and even misinterpreted, as a tool to legitimate female suicide bombers. Mainstream scholars of Islam have consistently maintained for centuries that the Qur’an , the Hadith (traditions and practices of the Prophet Muhammad), and other principles of Shari’a (Islamic law) clearly condemn terrorist acts and oppose suicide operations, yet leaders of Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad (PIJ) and Al Qaeda have formulated their own radical interpretations of Islam that are based more on pragmatic military strategy than on any theological foundation. This is nowhere more apparent than in their attempts to portray female suicide bombing as consistent with Islamic doctrine. While the media may publicize the use of female suicide bombers as a sacrament of self-proclaimed “religious” terrorists, or “fundamentalists,” this study argues that the religion of Islam does not provide any basis for suicide bombing. Rather, terrorist groups have drawn on radical and aberrant reinterpretations of religious doctrine to justify political violence that is prohibited by Islam. Furthermore, there is no precedent for religiously motivated suicide in Islam. Thus, this study eschews popular terms like “Fundamentalist Islam,” “Radical Islam,” or even “Political Islam” in reference to groups using female suicide bombers, because these terms imply a religious basis for suicide bombing. This study employs the term “Jihadism,” due to its adherents’ emphasis on a radical reinterpretation of the concept of jihad and the pragmatic manipulation of religious doctrine to conform to military and strategic needs—as opposed to a political movement based on a religious foundation. Indeed, the Jihadi version of Islam has been altered to the point that is no longer recognizable by the vast majority of the world’s mainstream Muslims. Shari’a , or Islamic law, consists of four elements: the Qur’an as revealed to the Prophet Muhammad by God, the Hadith or collected sayings and actions of Muhammad, the ijmaa or Muslim consensus, and ijtjihad or interpretation by religious scholars [ 36 : 17]. Scholars of fiqh , or Islamic jurisprudence, spend years studying the Qur’an and Hadith , often earning PhDs from established Islamic universities [ 70 : 81]. These scholars are responsible for establishing the mainstream interpretations of the Qur’an and Islamic law [ 37 : 122] and adhere to precedent, much like jurists of Western case law. Despite this, the global Muslim community, or umma , is far from monolithic. Unlike the Roman Catholic Church, which is organized within a hierarchical structure of religious leaders from the Pope to local clergy, Islam is highly decentralized and has no universal Imam or supreme religious leader, and Jihadist groups like Hamas, the PIJ, and Al Qaeda have taken advantage of this power vacuum to reinterpret Islamic doctrine to meet their own needs. Some of the issues in dispute include the right to wage war, the legitimacy of civilians as targets, the definition of jihad, and the concept of martyrdom. Within Islam, the rules of engagement and conduct of war have been established by over one thousand years of scholarly interpretation and precedent. According to the Qur’an and Islamic tradition, warfare is the domain of the state and can only be authorized by the executive of an established government [ 37 : 124]. This well-established doctrine negates any authority by non-state actors to engage in war, even in defense of Islam. According to traditional interpretation, the need for social order and political stability supersedes the desire of any rebel group or individual to mount a campaign of violence [ 5 ]. Mainstream scholars are unequivocal, “The rules of Islam are clear. Individuals cannot declare war. A group or organization cannot declare war. War is declared by the state. War cannot be declared without a president or an army…. Otherwise, it is an act of terror” [ 22 : 2]. The late Shaykh Muhammad Nasiruddin al-Albani (1914–1999), known as the Muhaddith - al - asr or Hadith and fiqh specialist of the era, agreed, stating that living well, even under non-Islamic law, is better than rising against the government [ 18 : 32]. The Qur’an also prohibits targeting civilians (9:91; 51:17). Muslims believe that, “According to the… Holy Qur’an , the murder of a single innocent person is a murder that is as heinous as the murder of all humanity” [ 21 : 11] and “killing an innocent human being is equivalent to assuming the role of God, which is a great sin” [ 22 : 105]. The Qur’an strictly regulates the conduct of warfare, requiring that the enemy be treated with mercy, prohibiting torture, respecting the dead, and not targeting Muslims or civilians. Those who violate these rules of engagement are not mujahid or warriors of God, and their violence is not true jihad [ 2 : 27–39]. Repeatedly and consistently, “The scholars of Islam have unanimously agreed that the killing of non-combatant women, children, elderly people, monks,… the blind and the handicapped is forbidden in Islam,” [ 22 : 107] and “tying bombs to one’s own body for the sole purpose of killing and detonating these explosives in a crowd of innocent people and children cannot be considered Islamic behavior” [ 22 : 117]. The term “jihad” literally means “to strive” and can take the form of Greater Jihad, the internal struggle against sin, or Lesser Jihad, the violent jihad that has become more well-known in recent years. Although modern media typically discuss only violent jihad, the Qur’an emphasizes the Greater Jihad of psychological or spiritual jihad within oneself, the intellectual jihad of interpreting religious doctrine, and the social dimension of jihad that battles injustice and seeks to help others [ 35 : 51–52]. Under Islamic doctrine, all forms of Lesser Jihad must be defensive in nature. Only defensive jihad is permitted [ 51 : 384] [ 40 : 69–71], for the Qur’an states, “You may fight in the cause of God against those who attack you, but do not aggress. God does not love the aggressors” (2:190).This command and its orthodox interpretation are supported by centuries of Islamic jurisprudence. Closely related to the issue of jihad is that of martyrdom. Muslim scholars define religious martyrdom as bearing witness to the truth of God and risking one’s life for it [ 73 : 87]; however, suicide is expressly forbidden throughout the Qur’an (2:195). As one scholar of Islam explains, “In Islam, desiring to die is forbidden; believers must struggle to survive and win a war, rather than choosing to die” [ 73 : 87]. According to the Hadith , the Prophet Muhammad stated, “Whoever purposely throws himself from a mountain and kills himself, will in the Fire falling down into it and abiding therein perpetually forever; and whoever drinks poison and kills himself with it, he will be carrying his poison in his hand and drinking it in the Fire wherein he will abide eternally forever; and whoever kills himself with an iron weapon, will be carrying that weapon in his hand and stabbing his abdomen with it in the Fire wherein he will abide eternally forever” ( Hadith — Bukhari 7:670). Shaykh Muhammad Afifi al-Akiti of Oxford University details the authoritative religious objections to political violence in the name of Islam, and particularly the use of suicide attacks, in his fatwa, Defending the Transgressed by Censuring the Reckless against the Killing of Civilians . He contends that Islamic jurists and scholars from the Far East to the Middle East reject the attempt to justify terrorism through Islam not only as “an anomaly,” “a misguided innovation,” and “completely wrong,” but also as “an effort to hijack our fiqh [Islamic law]” [ 3 : 18–19]. Al-Akiti bases his argument against Islamic terrorism on three points: First, there is no legal precedent in Islam for attacking civilians, women, or children unless they are in direct combat, and even then they may be killed only in self-defense; the Qur’an and Hadith are very clear on this issue. According to al-Akiti, “A valid military target is limited to either a battlefield… or a military base… and certainly NEVER can anything else such as a restaurant, a hotel, a public bus, the area around a traffic light, or any other public place be considered a valid military target” [ 3 : 34]. Second, the only authority that may legitimately engage in war is the executive authority of the state; individuals, rebels, and other non-state actors are prohibited from waging war [ 3 : 19, 21, 36]. Third, suicide terrorism is suicide, not war. As al-Akiti plainly states, those who engage in terrorism in the name of Islam are “hijacking rules from our Sacred Law which are meant for the conventional (or authorized) army of a Muslim state and addressed to those with authority over it (such as the executive leader(s), the military commanders and so forth), but not to individuals who are not connected to the military or those without the political authority of the state” [ 3 : 19]. M. Fethullah Gülen, founder of the Gülen Movement for interfaith dialogue and “Turkey’s most influential religious leader,” [ 45 ] explains that “suicide is forbidden in Islam” and “official Islamic bodies… have denounced violence and terrorism, including use of suicide weapons” [ 29 ]. According to Gülen’s website, he “was the first Muslim scholar to publicly condemn the attacks of 9/11 (in an advertisement in the Washington Post)” and consistently condemns acts of terror and suicide terrorism in Western media as well as in sermons to thousands of Muslim congregants [ 31 ]. Gülen also helped publish a collection of essays by Muslim scholars detailing religious and humanitarian objections to terrorism in the name of Islam [ 22 ]. Mohammed Sayed Tantawi, Grand Shaykh of Egypt’s premier Sunni Muslim institution, Al Azhar University, from 1996 until his death in 2010, denounced terrorism, specifically the use of suicide bombings, calling suicide bombers “extremists and enemies of Islam” [ 46 ]. Following the terrorist attacks of 9/11, Tantawi explained, “It’s not courage in any way to kill an innocent person, or to kill thousands of people, including men and women and children” [ 11 ]. Although he was more ambivalent about the Palestinian situation, Tantawi consistently opposed Osama bin Laden’s call for jihad against the West, declaring it “invalid and not binding on Muslims,” adding that “killing innocent civilians is a horrific, hideous act that no religion can approve…. [The Qur’an ] specifically forbids the kinds of things the Taliban and Al Qaeda are guilty of” [ 34 ]. The Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia, Shaykh Abd al-Aziz bin Abdallah Aal al-Shaykh, is his nation’s supreme authority on Islam, as well as the leader of the Saudi commission of senior Islamic scholars. He concurs with mainstream views, stating, “What you call suicide bombings in my view are illegitimate and have nothing to do with jihad in the cause of God” [ 55 ]. The Palestinian Authority’s Mufti of Jericho, Sheik Muhammad Isma-il al-Jamail, published a fatwa against suicide bombing, explaining that it is not martyrdom [ 50 ], and the Islamic Commission of Spain went so far as to issue a fatwa against Osama bin Laden, calling him an apostate and urging other Muslims to denounce him [ 34 ]. Moreover, approximately 6,000 Muslim clerics from India, the world’s second largest Muslim population, approved a fatwa against terrorism in November 2008 [ 66 ]. Other examples of mainstream Islamic views on martyrdom are too numerous to include. The Origins of Radical Interpretations of Islam Although radical interpretations of Islam began to develop as early as the twelfth century, it was not until the twentieth century that they began to have a global impact. The modern era saw the decline of Muslim power begin with the fall of the Ottoman Empire followed by the creation of the state of Israel from 1917 to 1948, further contributing to the sense of loss in the Muslim world. The Arab defeat in the 1967 Six-Day War and the subsequent loss of Jerusalem, Islam’s holiest city after Mecca and Medina, brought the crisis to a head. Some Muslims believed that the Muslim world had fallen because it had strayed from the Qur’an and traditional religious values. They began to question the incursion of Western models of development, secularism, modernization, and nationalism, or “Westoxification” [ 27 : 11–17]. Radical elements went even further, drawing on the writings of Taymiyyah in the thirteenth century, and Mawdudi and Qutb in the twentieth century to develop a new interpretation of Islam whose doctrines harkened to a past golden age, but pragmatically incorporated modern technology and institutions into a simplistic battle of good versus evil, pitting Islam against the West and its Muslim allies. Ibn Taymiyyah and the Neglected Duty Taqi al-Din Ibn Taymiyyah (1263–1328) was a respected mujtihad , or one competent to interpret religious doctrine, but the political turmoil of relentless attacks from Eastern Mongols and Western Crusaders under fragmented political control and weak rulers led him to reevaluate traditional Islamic views. Ibn Taymiyyah countered orthodox Islamic doctrine by maintaining that jihad as a holy war, rather than an internal moral struggle, is the greatest form of jihad, and he criticized mainstream Islam for neglecting this duty far too often [ 64 : 154]. He further concluded that jihad was not only the duty of the community or state ( fard ‘ayn ), but of the individual ( fard kafiyah ), an interpretation of war powers that deviated dramatically from traditional jurisprudence [ 65 ] [ 48 : 11]. According to Taymiyyah, jihad becomes an individual duty when any Muslim community is attacked [ 56 ] and he later prioritized violent jihad as one of the three most important practices for Muslims [ 64 : 154]. Taymiyyah took on the role of reformer and purifier, believing that the Muslim community’s tolerance of other religions and intermingling of ethnicities led to a lack of solidarity and played a large part in the empire’s disorder and vulnerability to foreign invaders [ 4 : 17] [ 48 : 14, 113]. Taymiyyah strongly opposed Sufi Islam as well, arguing that Sufism’s focus on an internal, personal knowledge of God limited a necessary political and social activism [ 48 : 18]. Contrary to the Qur’an ’s repeated policy of Muslim tolerance toward Jews and Christians as People of the Book, Taymiyyah adopted the view that only Muslims, and only those who followed his views, were “friends of God,” while all others were “friends of Satan” and objects of jihad [ 64 : 54, 174]. Despite his declaration of a lifelong goal to purify Islam, his rejection of orthodox views and his defiance of political and religious elites led to several imprisonments by Muslim regimes [ 41 : 106]. Salafi and Wahhabis Another powerful early influence on Jihadism came in the forms of Salafism and Wahhabism. The term “Salafi” refers to those who hold the belief that the companions ( salaf ) of the Prophet Muhammad gained a purer understanding of Islam from his direct teachings, but that subsequent generations have defiled the faith with regional and cultural innovations. The Salafis seek to purify Islam by adhering to their own strict interpretation of the Qur’an , which often conflicts with that of other Muslims [ 32 : 16] [ 70 : 75]. Salafis are divided, however, on the means to achieve their goal. Non-violent Salafis do not use force to impose Shari’a and they abide by the Qur’anic prohibition against rebellion (4:92). Salafis who seek to purify Islam by force, through violent jihad, are often known as “Wahhabis” in reference to Muhammad bin Abdul al-Wahhab (1703–1792), a warrior who envisioned himself as a religious purist and joined forces with Muhammad Ibn Saud to establish political control over the Arabian Peninsula. Wahhabi was not a scholar, but a warrior who forged an alliance with Ibn Saud to establish political dominance over the Arabian peninsula. In doing so, Wahhabi also sought to eliminate what he considered heretical and irreverent practices that had evolved in Islam. Because he particularly feared Shi’a beliefs in intercession and veneration of saints, his forces plundered the honored tomb of the Prophet Muhammad’s grandson, Hussein, and destroyed the domes over the tombs of the Prophet Muhammad and other religious Imams [ 6 : 27–29]. Wahhabis would later storm Saudi Arabia’s Grand Mosque in 1979 for similar purposes, fearing the spread of Shi’ism through Iran’s Islamic revolution [ 26 : 72]. Modern Wahhabis mobilized against the invasion of Muslim lands during the Soviet-Afghan war (1979–1989), uniting Jihadis worldwide and providing them a universal cause. The Saudi government was more than generous in providing money, weapons, and young fighters to help combat the ailing Soviet empire in what Jihadis came to perceive as a mujahadin , or holy warrior, victory over a superpower [ 18 : 1] [ 6 : 57]. When the Saudi government permitted US troops in Saudi Arabia to fight Iraq during the first Gulf War (1990–1991), the Wahhabis were particularly incensed. Supported by Saudi petrodollars and fortified by the victorious jihad against the Soviets, the Wahhabis grew even more convinced of their radical religious views and were soon extending the Jihadi network to regions like the Balkan nations, Central Asia and other opportune locations where they painted over “idolatrous” murals and rebuilt mosques that were deemed too Westernized. Former communist regimes were particularly susceptible to Wahhabism as there was little extant religious tradition to displace and Saudi funding facilitated the expansion [ 26 : 113]. Mawdudi, Ijtihad, and Jahiliyya Like the radical ideologues before him, Mawlana Abul Mawdudi (1903–1979) of Pakistan also sought to restore Islam to a former purity. His beliefs emerged in an environment of heated conflict between Muslims and Hindus under British colonial rule in the 1920s and 1930s and he feared that converts to Islam were contaminating the religion with “pagan” practices [ 53 ]. Mawdudi accepted Taymiyyah’s argument for individual duty to jihad, adding that the authoritative interpretation of Islam, ijtihad, should not be limited to religious authorities and scholars but should be open to individuals [ 53 ], paving the way for increasingly radical interpretations to come. Mawdudi further argued that man-made law and governance placed man on a level with God, a sin known as shirk . Mawdudi likened positive law to the pre-Islamic era of ignorance and barbarism, or jahiliyya, and considered Muslims who supported the rule of man or non-Islamic laws to be apostates [ 70 : 78], thus justifying, and even requiring, the overthrow of non-theocratic Muslim regimes [ 49 : 97]. In response to the dangers he perceived threatening Islam, Mawdudi called for a universal jihad to resist Western jahiliyyah [ 7 : 168]. Qutb and the Expansion of Jihad Much like his ideological predecessors, Sayyid Qutb (1906–1966) of Egypt was born into an era of political chaos. President Abdel Nasser’s secular government had replaced Egypt’s corrupt monarchy, but was equally intolerant of political opposition and broke its promises to install an Islamic state [ 70 : 79] [ 49 : 94–96] [ 7 : 169–170]. In response, Qutb crafted a revolutionary ideology based upon Taymiyyah’s argument for jihad and Mawdudi’s rejection of man-made law, arguing for violent jihad and Islamic law not only in Muslim lands, but in the non-Muslim world as well [ 43 , 57 , 58 ]. Qutb wrote prolifically, but his work was denounced by orthodox clergy as heretical and his political activities led to his imprisonment in 1956 and execution 10 years later [ 49 : 95]. His writings lived on to inspire the 1981 assassination of Egyptian President Anwar Sadat as well as the activities of radical Jihadis throughout the world, such as Omar Abdul Rahman, of the Egyptian Islamic Jihad, Al Qaeda’s Osama bin Laden and Ayman Zawahiri, and the Taliban of Afghanistan. Qutb’s brother, Mohammed, even taught the young Bin Laden at King Abdul-Aziz University in Saudi Arabia [ 70 : 80–81] [ 7 : 170]. Azzam and Universal Jihad Abdullah Azzam (1941–1989) also played a major role in developing the modern Jihadi interpretation of Islam. As a Palestinian, he was deeply affected by the 1967 Arab defeat and fought with the Palestinian resistance, but was disillusioned with the secular nature of the PLO. Azzam accepted the unorthodox interpretations of earlier radical Muslims and proposed an international jihad to export Islamic revolutions to other nations [ 49 : 93]. Like Qutb’s brother, Azzam also taught at King Abdul-Aziz University where his radical religious interpretations influenced the student Bin Laden [ 49 : 100] [ 9 ]. In pursuit of his international jihad, Azzam joined the Afghan resistance in 1979, promoting the virtues of martyrdom [ 49 : 93, 104]. It was there that Azzam founded Al Qaeda al - Sulba , the “Firm Base” of support and services to facilitate global jihad, and precursor to today’s Al Qaeda [ 1 ]. Under Azzam’s guidance, the Sunni Jihadis adopted the Shi’a Hezbollah’s suicide bomber tactics [ 49 : 99]. Azzam is perhaps best known for his admonition to fight the West with “Jihad and the rifle alone; no negotiations, no conferences, and no dialogues” [ 49 : 92]. Khomeini and Martyrdom Clearly, these radical ideologues profoundly influenced global jihad, but the role of suicide bombing emerged as a form of Islamic martyrdom from the interpretation of Iran’s Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini (1902–1989). It was not until the Iraqi invasion of Iran in 1980 and a desperate lack of military troops that the Ayatollah Khomeini altered traditional Shi’a religious interpretation to endorse suicide bombing and became “the first to offer a religious justification for self-sacrifice in the name of the Islamic cause” [ 59 : 49]. Generally, Sunni Muslims and Shi’a Muslims are not influenced by each other’s interpretations, as these two sects of Islam have developed very different interpretations of various concepts over the centuries; however, as stated previously, suicide bombing is a political tactic, rather than a religious act. Thus, it is not surprising that the practice of suicide bombing crossed over from a Shi’a military context to be adopted by Sunni terrorist groups, for in neither sect was suicide bombing motivated by religion. Moreover, it is important to emphasize that mainstream Muslim scholars of both Sunni and Shi’a sects uniformly condemn suicide and suicide bombing. Khomeini’s interpretation was based upon the Shi’a belief in redemptive suffering, a concept that is absent from Sunni doctrine [ 14 : 65]. The Shi’a acceptance of atoning for sin through suffering has been reinforced by heroic Muslim deaths of Muslims throughout history, like that of the Prophet Muhammad’s grandson, Hussein, at the Battle of Karbala in 680 AD. For Shi’i, the murder of Hussein is comparable in importance to the Crucifixion for Christians, and Hussein’s death provides the basis for the Shi’a tradition of martyrdom [ 14 : 67]. Khomeini’s writings explicitly support suicide bombings [ 42 ] and he actively encouraged Hezbollah in Lebanon to adopt the “Karbala option” of martyrdom [ 1 ]. The Ayatollah stopped short of utilizing female suicide bombers, however, telling them to support jihad in other ways, “bake bread… and send money, provisions, and so many other things” [ 42 ]. Considering the longstanding schism between Shi’a and Sunni religious sects, the fact that Sunni terrorist organizations later adopted suicide bombing is further evidence that suicide bombing is a political and military tactic, not a religious act. Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad and Female Suicide Bombers In the late 1970s, Palestinian students formed the Palestinian Islamic Jihad (PIJ) as a Jihadist alternative to the PLO [ 25 ] [ 26 : 95]. The Islamic Resistance Movement, known by the acronym HAMAS , or zeal, emerged with the first Intifada (uprising) in December 1987. Although Azzam’s radicalism was shaped by the Palestinian crisis, he focused more on a global jihad, while Hamas’ founder and spiritual leader, Shaykh Ahmad Yassin, envisioned Taymiyyah’s violent jihad as a nationalist tool to liberate occupied Palestine [ 26 : 127]. Hamas and PIJ suicide operations in Israel-Palestine began in 1993 with a series of car bombings [ 12 , 39 , 69 ] and the support of Hezbollah, focused on undermining the Oslo Peace Accords. Despite the fact that Palestinians are predominantly Sunni, PIJ founder, Fathi Shiqaqui, borrowed Hezbollah’s Shi’a guidelines for differentiating martyrdom and suicide [ 8 : 1535]. After the second Intifada began in September 2000, a number of young Palestinian women reportedly volunteered for suicide missions, but Shaykh Yassin rejected them [ 61 ]—until Wafa Idris conducted a suicide operation on behalf of the PLO’s Al Fatah on January 27, 2002, and subsequently gained enormous notoriety [ 52 : 933] [ 67 : 4, 64]. Yassir Arafat publicly honored Idris and referred to Palestinian women as his “army of roses” [ 67 ]. Within the month, Yassin stated that Hamas would allow women to engage in suicide missions if no men were available and if the women were accompanied by a male chaperone—a restriction he later rescinded. Shaykh al-Qardawi, dean of Islamic studies at the University of Qatar, was even more enthusiastic, stating that, “women’s participation in the martyrdom operations… is one of the most praised acts of worship…. [She may] go out for jihad even without the permission of her husband,” and even travel without male chaperones or veils [ 61 ]. In 2003, the PIJ announced that it too would accept female suicide bombers, explaining that the change would improve the ability of the organization to carry out its operations [ 16 ]. Although the group provided no theological reasoning, it claimed that Islam does not preclude women’s participation in suicide bombings and while the PIJ claimed not to actively recruit women for these operations, it admitted that it would permit them [ 24 : 48]. The PIJ did, however, begin recruiting women for martyrdom operations and two female PIJ operatives carried out their missions that same year. In January of 2004, Hamas launched its first female suicide bomber, Reem Raiyshi [ 52 : 933]. In August of that year, the online magazine Al Khansaa called for women to join jihad as a mother, wife, sister, daughter, educator, preacher of Islam—and female Jihadi warrior [ 17 ]. Hamas was responsible for at least three more of the nine Palestinian women bombers that followed and paid survivors’ benefits to the bombers’ families, albeit half the amount paid to male bombers’ families [ 67 : 95]. The late Muhammad Sayyid Tantawi, Grand Shaykh of Al-Azhar University in Egypt, issued a number of conflicting statements on suicide bombings, originally arguing against them as a violation of Shari’a [ 11 , 34 , 46 ], but altering his position in 2002, when he proclaimed that suicide bombings were valid under Shari’a in the case of occupied Muslim lands like Palestine [ 30 ]. He continued to maintain that noncombatants should not be targeted, but did not address the contradictory fact that suicide bombings usually kill civilians. The inconsistency of these interpretations suggests that the desire to support female suicide bombing stems more from strategic and tactical arguments than any theological premise. Tantawi seemed to reject terrorist tactics in general, but condone them in the specific case of Palestine due to its special significance in the Muslim world. The progression of radical interpretation is clearly visible in the efforts to legitimate female suicide bombing in Palestine. Radical clerics accept Taymiyyah’s thirteenth century interpretation of violent jihad as an individual duty, an operation that can be waged by non-state actors or individuals, and like Mawdudi, reject the man-made laws that prohibit these activities as shirk , or ascribing to man the law-making rights exclusive to God. Shaykh Ali Abu al-Hassan, former head of the Fatwa Council at the Religious Studies Institute at Az-Azhar University in Egypt, declared his support for the use of female suicide bombers or Jihadis in 2002, explaining that all Islamic wars are, by law, defensive wars, and that jihad is an individual duty that applies to all Muslims, male and female. When Muslim lands are invaded by non-Muslims, a woman is obligated to join jihad, even without permission from her husband. Abu al-Hassan supports his views with the example of the Prophet Muhammad’s wife, Aisha, who led troops in the Battle of Camel, and other women from the early Islamic era [ 72 : 53]; however, the Qur’an does not include women as soldiers and explicitly categorizes women and children as noncombatants [ 38 : 97–124] [ 2 : 27–39]. Thus, modern Jihadists have further radicalized the work of earlier extremist thinkers to justify female suicide bombing. Al Qaeda and Female Suicide Bombers Women have been largely absent from Al Qaeda’s operations. A recent exception is the recruitment of women as suicide bombers in Iraq, where they became a routine tactic during the US occupation [ 10 ]. Just as in the Palestinian terrorist movements, the women of Al Qaeda are used as expendable tools and economical weapons and little effort has been made to theologically justify their role. Suicide bombers were active in Iraq from the beginning of the US invasion in April 2003; however, women suicide bombers were relatively scarce at first. In 2003, only two female bombers successfully carried out operations and there were none the following year. Similarly, 2005 saw only three female bombers and a slight increase to four in 2006. That number doubled to eight in 2007 and more than tripled to thirty-five in 2008, with half that many female bombers apprehended before completing their missions [ 20 ]. Although the rapid increase was undeniably cause for concern, it must be noted that women still comprise only a small percentage of the total suicide bombings conducted by Al Qaeda in Iraq. Close to 1000 suicide bombings occurred in Iraq between April 2003 and the end of 2008, yet only 52 of those operations were conducted by women. The years 2009 and 2010 saw a marked decline in all suicide bombings after the announcement of US plans for military withdrawal; however, two Sunni female suicide bomber attacks killed over 55 Shi’a in 2009. Similar attacks killed 54 in February 2010 and five in July 2010 [ 19 , 28 ]. When we examine the circumstances and motivations of these women, the common profile that emerges is that of women trying to survive in the aftermath of a war with no political, economic or social collateral. As investigators point out, most of the female suicide bombers in Iraq are deemed unsuccessful in their own society, and are therefore more susceptible to recruitment for these missions [ 62 ]. Approximately half of Iraq’s female suicide bombers are from the wartorn and poverty-stricken Diyala province and according to Saja Quadouri, Diyala’s only female council member, “The women forced into these marriages are often passed around,…. Eventually, due to despair, hopelessness and fear, they get exploited to commit such crimes, as they become unwanted by society” [ 74 ]. Major General Abdul Karim al-Rubaie, head of Iraqi Army operations in Diyala, adds that the traditional gender roles and lack of rights in rural villages often leave women vulnerable to Al Qaeda recruiters [ 60 ]. It is not surprising that such desperation drives some women to these acts, but according to Shari’a , martyrs must be of sound mental health [ 73 : 89]. Muslims who engage in suicide bombings out of despair are committing suicide, not sacrificing themselves for Islam as martyrs. Martyrs must also be aware of their actions and capable of making their own decisions [ 73 : 89], yet two mentally handicapped women in Baghdad killed almost 100 victims in a February 2008 suicide bombing. Investigators reported that one woman may have had Down syndrome and the other had been treated for schizophrenia and depression at Ibn Rushd Psychiatric Hospital [ 54 ]. Their explosive vests were detonated by remote control and officials suspect that the women may have been unaware of the nature of their mission. Subsequent inquiries by the US military revealed that Al Qaeda agents had been bribing staff at Baghdad’s two psychiatric hospitals, Ibn Rushd and Rashad Hospital, to deliver medical files and contact information of patients vulnerable to recruitment [ 23 ]. In 2009, Samira Ahmed Jassim, age 51, admitted to recruiting over 80 women, 28 of whom successfully completed their missions, to become suicide bombers. Known as the “mother of believers,” she also confessed to helping organize the rape of young Iraqi women and then counseling the distraught victims that their only remaining option was to become a suicide bomber. Al Qaeda also recruited Belgian Muriel Degauque, an unemployed former drug addict, to travel to Iraq where she conducted a suicide bombing in 2005 [ 13 , 15 ]. Martyrs are also required to be at the age of puberty or beyond to ensure that they are at an age of accountability for their actions [ 73 : 89]. Al Qaeda’s female suicide bombers in Iraq are typically between the ages of 15 and 25, but bombers as young as 13 have been apprehended [ 63 ]. These women are often recruited by their own husbands or in-laws as Al Qaeda leaders routinely infiltrate Iraqi families through marriage to gain members. Rania Ibrahim, age 15, reported that her husband of 5 months had allowed his female relatives to strap an explosives vest to the girl and send her on a suicide mission. Afraid for her life, she surrendered to police of the Baqouba province in August 2008 [ 44 ]. Earlier that year, a 14-year-old girl’s suicide vest was detonated by remote control in Baghdad and another 14-year-old girl was apprehended prior to completing her mission [ 47 ]. Clearly, Al Qaeda’s recruitment of women in desperate situations, some of whom are unable to clearly evaluate their actions, constitutes not only an egregious violation of these women’s rights, but also a serious breach of the precepts of Islam. Despite its professed commitment to return to a golden age of Islam and re-establish the Islamic caliphate, Al Qaeda has eagerly embraced new technologies to recruit female suicide bombers as well as facilitate its operations in other ways. As terrorism experts note, “suicide bombers are quintessentially modern, in that they have left behind the traditional interpretations of religion in order to exploit only selected aspects of religion. They are a mixture of the Battle of Karbala and cable television—old myths and new media” [ 59 : 16]. The global nature of the Internet makes terrorist communication easily accessible to a vast population, which is particularly important for building and maintaining an international terrorist network, such as Al Qaeda. Thousands of Al Qaeda’s authors and postings appeal specifically to women, disseminating propaganda, gathering strategic intelligence, and mobilizing female operatives. In 2004, the online periodical Al - Khansaa , published by the Women’s Information Bureau of al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula, contributed to the Al Qaeda recruitment effort, espousing that a virtuous woman should seek to become a martyr, or female suicide bomber [ 68 : 143]. One aspect of recruitment and mobilization of women is providing a theological foundation for Jihadi operations, but Al Qaeda did not even attempt to cobble together a religious justification for its actions until it was besieged by criticism from Muslim religious leaders after the attacks of 9/11. On April 24, 2002, Al Qaeda issued “A Statement from Qaidat al-Jihad Regarding the Mandates of the Heroes and the Legality of the Operations in New York and Washington” [ 71 : 77]. Mawdudi’s concept of ijtihad is an important basis of Jihadi logic because the majority of trained Muslim scholars reject Jihadi radicalism. Formally trained Islamic scholars are imbued with the authority to interpret the Qur’an , Hadith , and religious doctrine on behalf of the Muslim community, but engineers like Bin Laden and orthopedic surgeons like Al-Zawahiri do not possess that credibility. They and other “Jihadi scholars who nurture Al Qaeda and provide religious cover for acts of violence suffer from a ‘reputation deficit.’ Many are self-taught, new Islamist intellectuals with little formal religious training… in contrast to the non-violent Salafi scholars, many who hold PhDs from established Islamic universities” [ 71 : 81]. Although loosely based on earlier radical interpretations of Shari’a , Jihadist writings actually focus less on religious doctrine and more on Western military concepts and strategic calculations. They are more pragmatic than theological and “although each of them is without question a true believer in the global Jihadist ideology, none allows his religious or ideological beliefs to hamstring his military thinking. Winning comes first, so the ideology can flex to the needs of the situation on the ground” [ 18 : 82]. Among the Jihadi ideologues, Bin Laden and Al-Zawahiri have almost mythic status, but they are not scholars of Islam. Their purpose is primarily to mobilize believers for jihad and “they rarely provide specific strategic or tactical advice” [ 32 : 15]. The leaders of Al Qaeda “intentionally utilize Islam as a tool to rally popular support and legitimize terrorism in the pursuit of their purely political goals” [ 33 : 40]. Conclusion In tracing the development of radical Islamic doctrine, we can clearly see the building blocks that have culminated in Jihadi female suicide bombers. Ibn Taymiyyah’s emphasis on violent jihad and his declaration of jihad as an individual duty began the radicalization, followed by Mawdudi’s redefining of jahiliyyah and his assertion of ijtihad to freely reinterpret Islamic jurisprudence. Qutb built upon their arguments, applying the concept of jahiliyya to the West, and Azzam founded The Base for the universal jihad. Khomeini provided the next phase in the process, drawing on the Shi’a concept of redemptive suffering to rationalize suicide bombing as martyrdom. It was but a short step to female suicide bombers with Hamas, the PIJ, and Al Qaeda. The decidedly un-Islamic introduction of female suicide bombers is hardly surprising in these terrorist groups, for they simply reinterpet and manipulate religious doctrine to legitimize acts that are strategically and militarily utilitarian. Female suicide bombers are merely a phase in the evolution of terrorist tactics, much as were hijackings in the 1970s and discotheque bombings in the 1980s. The use of female suicide bombers is a tool to gain media attention and emphasize the underdog nature of terrorist groups in what we now call “asymmetrical warfare.” But it is not Islam. The female suicide bombers of nominally Muslim groups like Hamas, the PIJ, and Al Qaeda are no more Islamic than the Hindu Tamil women bombers of Sri Lanka or the communist female suicide bombers of the Kurdish Workers’ Party in Turkey. They are not Islamic martyrs nor any other manifestation of orthodox religious faith. From a policy perspective, an in-depth understanding of female suicide bombers, their motivations, and their uses can be very helpful in developing counter-terrorism and anti-terrorism strategy. Efforts to prevent suicide operations or apprehend female suicide bombers can be tailored to accommodate the methods utilized by various Jihadi organizations. Just as war, fragmented political control, and weak rulers spawned the radical views of earlier Jihadis like Ibn Taymiyyah, these factors have also contributed to unstable political and economic environments that make women more vulnerable to recruitment for suicide bombing operations. Possible responses include foreign assistance with police training and the institutionalization of law and order, along with humanitarian aid and micro-credit operations similar to those that have been so successful in Bangladesh, India, and parts of Africa. Of course, political and economic development do not necessarily preclude terrorist activities, but desperate situations of political and economic instability certainly create a more hospitable environment for recruiters of suicide bombers.
abstract: This study examines the radicalization of Islamic thought from Ibn Taymiyyah in the thirteenth century, to the emergence of Wahhabism in the eighteenth century, followed by twentieth century radical ideologues such as Mawdudi, Qutb, and Azzam, to explain how terrorist organizations like Al Qaeda, Hamas, and Palestinian Islamic Jihad have rejected mainstream Islamic jurisprudence and crafted a complete reinterpretation of religious doctrine in an attempt to legitimate female suicide bombings. This examination of women suicide bombers provides a clear example of how what is often called Islamic radicalism or political Islam has far less connection with religion than it does with utilitarian and pragmatic concerns such as strategic and military tactics. news_body: Terrorist groups bend the rules of 'true' Islam to justify the use of female suicide bombers, according to Margaret Gonzalez-Perez from Southeastern Louisiana University in the US. Her paper traces the development of radical Islamic doctrine over time, highlights how it deviates from mainstream Islam, and identifies the building blocks that have culminated in Jihadi female suicide bombers. According to Gonzalez-Perez, "Radical Islam reinterprets, and even misinterprets Islamic jurisprudence, as a tool to legitimize female suicide bombers." Her findings are published online in Springer's journal, Gender Issues. According to mainstream scholars of Islam, the Qur'an, the Hadith (traditions and practices of the Prophet Muhammad), and other principles of Shari'a (Islamic law) clearly condemn terrorist acts and oppose suicide operations. In order to justify suicide bombings, leaders of Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad (PIJ) and Al Qaeda have formulated their own interpretations of Islam, that are based more on military strategy than theology. This is particularly apparent in their attempts to portray female suicide bombing as consistent with Islamic doctrine, according to Gonzalez-Perez. She identifies a two-stage radicalization process, the origins of which she traces back to the thirteenth century. The gradual radicalization of Islam began in the Middle Ages with some individuals claiming the right to interpret religious law and decide for themselves when war was justified. These ideas led to the justification of suicide bombers as a form of Islamic martyrdom under the interpretation of Iran's Ayatollah Khomeini (1902-1989). Further radicalization enabled the justification of female suicide bombing. However, the Qur'an does not include women as soldiers and explicitly categorizes women and children as non-combatants. According to Gonzalez-Perez's analysis of the emergence in 2000, and rise since, of female suicide bombers, they are merely a military tactic, rather than a religious act. In the author's view, terrorist groups recruit women by exploiting vulnerable females, including some with mental health issues and girls as young as 14. The common profile of female suicide bombers is that of a woman trying to survive in the aftermath of a war with no political, economic or social security. Understanding female suicide bombers, their motivations and their uses can be helpful in developing counter-terrorism strategies. Gonzalez-Perez concludes: "The decidedly un-Islamic introduction of female suicide bombers is hardly surprising in current terrorist groups, for they simply reinterpret and manipulate religious doctrine to legitimize acts that are strategically and militarily utilitarian. Female suicide bombers are not Islamic martyrs nor any other manifestation of orthodox religious faith."

**New Study Sheds Light on the Influences Behind Female Suicide Bombings in Radical Islamist Movements**

A recent study has delved deep into the historical evolution of radical Islamic thought, tracing its roots from the 13th century to contemporary times. This study provides a fresh perspective on how certain extremist groups have strategically reinterpreted religious doctrines to justify actions such as female suicide bombings—actions that contradict traditional Islamic teachings.

The research journey begins with the teachings of Ibn Taymiyyah in the 13th century, traveling through the emergence of Wahhabism in the 18th century, to the thoughts of influential 20th century ideologues like Mawdudi, Qutb, and Azzam. These figures have significantly influenced groups like Al Qaeda, Hamas, and Palestinian Islamic Jihad, which have deviated from mainstream Islamic jurisprudence.

The study suggests that what is often labeled as "Islamic radicalism" might be less about religious fervor and more about pragmatic strategies. By examining the role of female suicide bombers, this research highlights how these radical factions have repurposed religious narratives to serve their military and strategic goals rather than adhering to traditional religious practices.

This reinterpretation of religious doctrine illustrates that the motivations behind radical actions are often utilitarian, focusing on achieving specific objectives rather than purely spiritual ones. This perspective challenges the common assumptions about the nature of political Islam and suggests that the issues at hand are more complex than simply a clash of religious ideologies.

The findings underline the importance of understanding the historical and strategic contexts in which these radical ideologies develop, offering a nuanced view that could inform policies and strategies aimed at countering extremism.

paper_body: Abstract The intimate link between location of fat accumulation and metabolic disease risk and depot-specific differences is well established, but how these differences between depots are regulated at the molecular level remains largely unclear. Here we show that TRIP-Br2 mediates endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress-induced inflammatory responses in visceral fat. Using in vitro , ex vivo and in vivo approaches, we demonstrate that obesity-induced circulating factors upregulate TRIP-Br2 specifically in visceral fat via the ER stress pathway. We find that ablation of TRIP-Br2 ameliorates both chemical and physiological ER stress-induced inflammatory and acute phase response in adipocytes, leading to lower circulating levels of inflammatory cytokines. Using promoter assays, as well as molecular and pharmacological experiments, we show that the transcription factor GATA3 is responsible for the ER stress-induced TRIP-Br2 expression in visceral fat. Taken together, our study identifies molecular regulators of inflammatory response in visceral fat that—given that these pathways are conserved in humans—might serve as potential therapeutic targets in obesity. Introduction Obesity is a worldwide health problem that impacts the risk and progression of many diseases including diabetes, cardiovascular disease, hyperlipidaemia and certain types of cancer 1 . In humans, the anatomical patterns of fat accumulation during obesity have gained considerable interest because of their association with differential metabolic risks 2 . Accumulation of visceral fat is associated with high risk of metabolic disease and increased mortality, even in those with a normal body mass index. In contrast, increase in subcutaneous (SQ) fat, especially in the gluteofemoral region, confers little or no risk and may even be protective 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , 7 . Depot-specific differences, including insulin sensitivity, adipogenesis, adipokines secretion and beige adipocyte induction potential, are well characterized, but the mechanisms responsible for these differences are not well understood 8 , 9 . Recent studies have demonstrated intrinsic gene expression differences between visceral and SQ fat depots 10 , 11 , 12 , 13 . Change in expression of Shox2, a gene expressed in SQ but not visceral fat, affects SQ adipose tissue functions 14 , confirming the importance of differentially expressed genes in different fat depots. However, visceral fat-specific regulators are not well studied. Considering the metabolic risks associated with visceral fat, identification of key visceral fat-specific regulators are likely to have high therapeutic potential to combat obesity-induced metabolic diseases. Previously, we showed that a transcriptional regulator, TRIP-Br2 (also known as SERTAD2), is specifically upregulated in visceral but not SQ fat in obese men and women. Further, we have demonstrated that ablation of TRIP-Br2 protects mice from obesity and associated metabolic dysfunction 15 . Our previous study demonstrated a role for TRIP-Br2 in adipocyte biology and energy metabolism. Owing to the striking conservation of visceral fat-specific regulation of TRIP-Br2 expression in human and mouse white adipose tissues as well as the metabolic protective phenotype of the knockout (KO) mouse, we hypothesized that TRIP-Br2 plays a role in the regulation of differential metabolic risks between different white fat depots during obesity, especially in visceral adipocytes. Therefore, understanding the upstream regulatory pathway of TRIP-Br2 in visceral fat and its visceral fat-specific functions could yield critical information on the mechanisms leading to visceral fat-induced metabolic risks. In our present study, we found that expression of TRIP-Br2 in visceral fat is specifically regulated by obesity-induced inflammatory cytokines and fatty acids via the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress pathway. We found that modulation of TRIP-Br2 expression significantly altered the expression of visceral adipocyte inflammatory adipokines and acute phase response gene. In addition, we observed that transcription factor GATA3 is similarly regulated in visceral fat upon physiological or pharmacological ER stress induction and inhibition of GATA3 abolished visceral adipocyte ER stress-induced TRIP-Br2 expression. Results Adipose and macrophages secreted factors modulate TRIP-Br2 Our previous study demonstrated that TRIP-Br2 is specifically upregulated in visceral but not SQ fat in obese humans and ablation of TRIP-Br2 protects mice from diet-induced obesity and metabolic dysfunction 15 . However, we also observed that TRIP-Br2 expression was similarly regulated in both gonadal (gWAT) and inguinal (iWAT) white adipose tissues in obese mice 15 . This observation limits the use of the mouse model to examine visceral fat-specific TRIP-Br2 regulation. More recently, we discovered that, unlike in humans, there are three TRIP-Br2 isoforms (encoded by a different Exon 1 but coding for the same protein) in mice ( Fig. 1a ). We noted that transcript 3 is structurally similar to the human transcript ( Fig. 1a ) and has highest relative expression in both iWAT and gWAT ( Supplementary Fig. 1a ) compared with transcripts 1 and 2. Interestingly, we observed that transcript 3 is specifically upregulated in gWAT in the high-fat diet (HFD) fed mice ( Fig. 1b ). These results recapitulate our observation in our human cohorts. Therefore, understanding the visceral fat-specific regulation of the mouse Trip-br2 transcript 3 might provide mechanistic insight on how TRIP-Br2 is specifically regulated in visceral fat in humans. Figure 1: Adipocyte TRIP-Br2 expression is regulated by adipose- and macrophage-secreted factors. ( a ) Schematic depicting exon-intron structure of TRIP-Br2 isoforms in mouse and human genomes. qPCR analysis of TRIP-Br2 gene expression in ( b ) gonadal (gWAT) or inguinal (iWAT) adipose tissues after 12 weeks (wk) of low-fat diet (LFD) or high-fat diet (HFD; n =6 per group replicated twice); ( c ) 3T3-L1 adipocytes treated with serum from either chow diet (CD) or HFD mice for 24 or 48 h ( n =4 per group replicated thrice); ( d ) 3T3-L1adipocytes treated with conditioned media from adipose tissues harvested from mice fed with 12 wk of CD or HFD ( n =5 per group replicated twice); ( e ) 3T3-L1 adipocytes treated with RAW-conditioned media with or without LPS (1, 10 μg ml −1 ) stimulation for 24 h ( n =5 per group replicated twice). ANOVA analysis. ( f ) 3T3-L1 adipocytes treated with conditioned media of isolated stromal-vascular-fraction (SVF) or mature adipocytes from CD or HFD-fed mice ( n =5 per group). All qPCR data are normalized to TATA box-binding protein (TBP) and presented as mean±s.e.m. Two-tailed Student’s t -test or ANOVA, * P <0.05; ** P <0.01; *** P <0.001. Full size image To identify upstream factors leading to the increased expression of TRIP-Br2 in visceral fat during obesity, we incubated differentiated 3T3-L1 adipocytes with serum obtained from mice fed either chow diet (CD) or 12 weeks of HFD and observed that HFD serum significantly upregulated Trip-br2 expression in adipocytes compared with CD serum ( Fig. 1c ). To determine the source of the upstream factor, we incubated differentiated adipocytes with conditioned media from adipose tissues harvested from CD- or HFD-fed mice. Interestingly, we observed that conditioned media from HFD adipose tissues also significantly upregulated Trip-br2 expression in 3T3-L1 adipocytes ( Fig. 1d ). As HFD induces accumulation and activation of immune cells, most prominently the macrophages 16 , 17 , in the adipose tissues, to determine whether macrophages play a role in TRIP-Br2 regulation, we treated differentiated adipocytes with RAW (macrophage cell line)-conditioned media with or without lipopolysaccharides (LPS) stimulation. Similarly, we observed that RAW-conditioned media, especially RAW cells stimulated with LPS but not LPS alone, significantly upregulated Trip-br2 expression in adipocytes ( Fig. 1e and Supplementary Fig. 1b ). To confirm our findings and determine the contribution of mature adipocytes and stromal-vascular fraction (SVF) cells on Trip-br2 induction, we isolated mature adipocytes and SVF from either CD- or HFD-fed mice to obtain respective conditioned media. Interestingly, we observed that the conditioned media from mature adipocytes, but not the SVF isolated from the HFD mice, stimulated Trip-br2 expression in 3T3-L1 adipocytes ( Fig. 1f ). These data at first glance seem contradictory to our earlier experiment ( Fig. 1e ) but SVF cells (that is, macrophages) isolated from their in vivo environment might have lost their stimuli resulting in decreased proinflammatory cytokines secretion. If this is the case, these data are in line with our RAW cells study as unstimulated RAW cells did not significantly upregulate Trip-br2 expression in differentiated adipocytes ( Fig. 1e ). In addition, we also observed that during the process of SVF isolation, compared with the intact adipose tissues, significant amount of macrophages, as examined by the F4/80 expression, were lost ( Supplementary Fig. 1c ), potentially causing low level of cytokines in the SVF-conditioned media. Taken together, our results suggest that TRIP-Br2 is regulated by circulating factors secreted by HFD-fed adipose tissues and activated macrophages. Adipocyte TRIP-Br2 is regulated by the ER stress pathway To identify the potential TRIP-Br2 inducer, we treated differentiated 3T3-L1 adipocytes with factors known to be elevated during obesity, including cytokines, adipokines and fatty acids, as well as the ER stress-inducing agent thapsigargin ( Supplementary Table 1 ). We observed that the cytokines interleukin (IL) 6, IL15 and tumour necrosis factor α (TNFα) and the fatty acid palmitate could significantly increase Trip-br2 expression individually, although their individual effects are relatively small, whereas thapsigargin significantly upregulated Trip-br2 expression to the level observed in adipose tissues from HFD-fed mice 15 , a physiological ER stress model ( Fig. 2a ). To confirm the role of ER stress on TRIP-Br2 expression in adipocytes, we first treated the 3T3-L1 adipocytes with thapsigargin or tunicamycin, another commonly used ER stress chemical inducer. Our results showed that ER stress regulates Trip-br2 in 3T3-L1 adipocytes in a time-dependent manner in line with the upregulation of ER stress markers ( Fig. 2b ). To further confirm the role of ER stress, we treated adipocytes differentiated from other preadipocyte cell lines and observed consistent results ( Fig. 2c ). Further, we showed that ER stress-induced Trip-br2 expression is absent in undifferentiated preadipocytes, confirming its specificity ( Fig. 2d ). Figure 2: Obesity-induced circulating factor converged on ER stress pathway to regulate TRIP-Br2 expression in vitro . qPCR analysis of TRIP-Br2 or ER stress marker (BiP) gene expression in ( a ) 3T3-L1 adipocytes after 24 h treatment with control (Ctrl) or compound indicated ( n =4 per group replicated twice); ( b ) 3T3-L1 adipocytes after 6, 24 or 48 h treatment with vehicle, thapsigargin (Thap; 0.1 μM) or tunicamycin (Tuni; 1 μg ml −1 ; n =3 per group replicated twice); ( c ) WT1 or C3H10T1/2 adipocytes after treatment with vehicle or Thap (0.1 μM) for 6, 24 or 48 h ( n =3 per group replicated twice); ( d ) 3T3-L1 preadipocytes treated with vehicle or Tuni (1 μg ml −1 ) for 24 h ( n =3 per group replicated twice); ( e ) human SGBS adipocytes treated with vehicle or Tuni (1 μg ml −1 ) for indicated time points ( n =3 per group replicated twice). ( f ) Western blot analysis for TRIP-Br2, BiP or β-actin (loading Ctrl) protein in 3T3-L1 adipocytes treated with vehicle (control, C) or Tuni (1 μg ml −1 , T) for indicated time points. ( g ) qPCR analysis of BiP gene expression in 3T3-L1 adipocytes treated with RAW- or HFD-fed gWAT-conditioned media for 24 h ( n =5 per group replicated twice) or 3T3-L1 adipocytes after 24 h treatment with Ctrl or compound indicated ( n =4 per group replicated twice). All qPCR data are normalized to TATA box-binding protein (TBP) and presented as mean±s.e.m. Two-tailed Student’s t -test or ANOVA, * P <0.05; ** P <0.01; *** P <0.001. C, control; T, Tunicamycin. Full size image Consistently, we also observed that TRIP-BR2 is similarly regulated in human adipocytes differentiated from the preadipocyte cell line (SGBS) in a time-dependent manner 18 upon ER stress induction ( Fig. 2e ). Using western blotting analysis, we confirmed that the transcriptional regulation of Trip-br2 was accompanied by TRIP-Br2 protein upregulation ( Fig. 2f ). Finally, to determine the role of ER stress on Trip-br2 expression in adipocytes treated with adipose- or macrophage-conditioned media ( Fig. 1 ) as well as in cells treated with individual adipokines or fatty acid ( Fig. 2a ), we examined the expression of ER stress markers. In line with our data obtained so far, we observed that Trip-br2 upregulation is concomitant with the upregulation of ER stress in the conditioned media-treated adipocytes as well as adipocytes treated with IL6, IL15 and palmitate ( Fig. 2g ). Taken together, our current data suggest that secreted factors from HFD-fed adipose tissues and activated macrophages such as inflammatory cytokines and fatty acids are potential upstream regulators, but ER stress might be the converging pathway for TRIP-Br2 regulation during obesity. ER stress regulates human and mouse visceral fat TRIP-Br2 To determine whether ER stress induction also induces TRIP-Br2 expression in vivo , we injected C57BL/6 (wild-type, WT) mice with vehicle or tunicamycin. Tissues were harvested for analysis 18 h after treatment. Interestingly, we observed that tunicamycin significantly and specifically upregulated Trip-br2 in gWAT but not in other metabolic organs including iWAT, liver and heart ( Fig. 3a ). Failure of Trip-br2 regulation in the liver and the heart despite successful induction of ER stress, as shown by ER stress markers ( Fig. 3b , Supplementary Fig. 2a ), suggests that Trip-br2 is not regulated by ER stress in the liver and the heart, consistent with our previous observation in the HFD-fed mice 15 . Figure 3: Both chemically- and HFD-induced ER stress promote TRIP-Br2 expression specifically in visceral fat. qPCR analysis of TRIP-Br2 or ER stress marker (XBP1s) gene expression in ( a , b ) tissues harvested from mice 18 h after intraperitoneal (i.p.) injection with vehicle (Ctrl, control) or tunicamycin (Tuni; 2.5 mg kg −1 ; n =5 per group replicated thrice); ( c , d ) iWAT or gWAT from mice 6, 12 or 18 h after IP injection with vehicle or Tuni (2.5 mg kg −1 ; n =5 per group replicated twice). ( e ) Western blot analysis of TRIP-Br2, BiP or β-actin (loading control (Ctrl)) in iWAT or gWAT from mice treated for 6, 12 or 18 h with vehicle or Tuni (2.5 mg kg −1 , i.p.). qPCR analysis of TRIP-Br2 or ER stress marker gene expression in ( f ) retroperitoneal (rWAT), perirenal (pWAT) or mesenteric (mWAT) adipose tissues after IP injection with vehicle or Tuni (2.5 mg kg −1 ; n =5 per group replicated thrice); ( g ) gWAT from mice after 12 weeks of CD, HFD or HFD with TUDCA (250 mg kg −1 at 0800 hours and 2000 hours i.p., total 500 mg kg −1 for 15 days; n =5 per group); ( h ) gWAT from mice after 4 or 8 wk of CD or HFD ( n =5 per group); ( i ) SVF or mature adipocytes from mice after 12 wk of CD or HFD ( n =5 per group replicated twice); ( j ) gWAT or iWAT adipocytes differentiated from immortalized gWAT or iWAT preadipocytes after 24 h of vehicle or Tuni (1 μg ml −1 ) treatment ( n =3 replicated twice); ( k ) human adipocytes differentiated from immortalized preadipocytes from lean or obese patients treated with vehicle or Tuni (1 μg ml −1 ) for 24 h ( n =4 replicated twice). All qPCR data are normalized to TATA box-binding protein (TBP) and presented as mean±s.e.m. Two-tailed Student’s t -test or ANOVA, * P <0.05; ** P <0.01; *** P <0.001 (versus Ctrl or CD); # P <0.05 (versus HFD). Full size image As iWAT failed to show any ER stress response after 18 h of tunicamycin treatment, to find out whether SQ fat is resistant to ER stress induction by tunicamycin or has a different response kinetic, we treated WT mice for different time points. Our experiments confirmed that ER stress indeed plays a specific regulatory role on Trip-br2 regulation in gWAT, because despite successful ER stress induction, we still failed to detect changes in Trip-br2 expression in the iWAT ( Fig. 3c,d and Supplementary Fig. 2b ). Increased TRIP-Br2 protein level was later confirmed in gWAT using western blot analysis ( Fig. 3e ). In addition to gWAT, we observed that Trip-br2 expression was also upregulated in other visceral fat depots, for example, retroperitoneal, perirenal and mesenteric fat ( Fig. 3f ), confirming its visceral fat specificity. As tunicamycin is a chemical ER stress inducer, to determine whether TRIP-Br2 is regulated by physiological ER stress and whether gWAT TRIP-Br2 induction by HFD feeding is actually mediated by ER stress, we treated HFD-fed mice with the ER chaperone tauroursodeoxycholic acid (TUDCA) 19 . Our data showed that TUDCA treatment ameliorated HFD-induced ER stress and at the same time prevented the induction of gWAT Trip-br2 expression ( Fig. 3g and Supplementary Fig. 2c ). Further, we also found that even a shorter period (4 or 8 weeks) of HFD was enough to induce ER stress and Trip-br2 expression in gWAT ( Fig. 3h and Supplementary Fig. 2d ), confirming the link between gWAT ER stress and TRIP-Br2 regulation. In addition, we also determined that the upregulated Trip-br2 expression by ER stress in gWAT was confined to mature adipocytes but not the preadipocytes ( Fig. 3i ), consistent with our in vitro study ( Fig. 2d ). To facilitate subsequent mechanistic studies (see below), we established immortalized gWAT and iWAT preadipocyte cell lines from C57BL/6 mice and confirmed their differentiation capacity with differentiation markers ( Supplementary Fig. 2e ). Consistent with our in vivo data, upon tunicamycin treatment, Trip-br2 was specifically upregulated in gWAT but not iWAT adipocytes ( Fig. 3j ). As we have previously observed that TRIP-Br2 is specifically regulated in visceral fat in overweight and obese patients, to determine the role of ER stress in human visceral adipocytes, we treated differentiated visceral or SQ adipocytes derived from lean or obese patients 20 with tunicamycin ( Supplementary Fig. 2f ). Consistently, we observed that TRIP-BR2 was specifically induced by ER stress in visceral adipocytes but not SQ adipocytes despite robust induction of ER stress markers in both adipocytes ( Fig. 3k ). Taken together, our in vivo and in vitro data showed that ER stress (both physiologically and chemically induced) activation plays a selective role in the induction of TRIP-Br2 expression in both human and mouse visceral adipocytes. TRIP-Br2 mediates gWAT ER stress-induced inflammation Our previous study demonstrated that TRIP-Br2 plays an important role in lipolysis and thermogenesis in visceral fat 15 . Visceral and SQ fats are known to have differential inflammatory adipokines synthesis and secretion during obesity 8 , 9 , and obesity has been shown to induce ER stress in adipose tissues 21 ; however, to the best of our knowledge, it has not been carefully examined whether different white fat depots respond differentially to obesity-induced ER stress and whether the potential differences in ER stress response contributes to the differential metabolic risks observed in visceral and SQ fat during obesity. Therefore, to gain insight, we examined expression of inflammatory and acute phase response markers in gWAT and iWAT from WT mice after tunicamycin treatment. Interestingly, we observed that ER stress differentially regulated expression of inflammatory and acute phase response markers in gWAT and iWAT ( Fig. 4a ). Unlike in the gWAT, these markers are either not induced or induced to a lower level in the iWAT. As we know that TRIP-Br2 is selectively regulated by ER stress in gWAT, to determine whether TRIP-Br2 plays a role in the regulation of the ER stress-induced differential inflammatory and acute phase responses between gWAT and iWAT, we examined the levels of inflammatory and acute phase response markers in gWAT from the TRIP-Br2 KO and WT mice after tunicamycin treatment. Interestingly, we observed that ablation of TRIP-Br2 significantly reduced or abolished expression of ER stress-induced inflammatory and acute phase response markers including Il-6 , Ifnγ , Tnfa , Mcp-1 , F4/80 , Crp and Saa in the KO gWAT but not as effectively in the iWAT ( Fig. 4b and Supplementary Fig. 3a ), which is not surprising as TRIP-Br2 is not regulated in the iWAT. Consistent with the gene expression data, we also observed that circulating levels of IL6 and MCP-1 were upregulated in WT mice treated with tunicamycin but their levels in KO mice were either attenuated or even lowered ( Fig. 4c ).
