There are some data to suggest that these genes may cooperatively contribute to the increased proliferative capacity of ER + tumors 20 , and it is tempting to speculate that these may be additional target genes for the candidate causal variants at a subset of the five signals identified here and perhaps responsible for their differential phenotype associations. A greater understanding of these genes may also provide novel targets for breast cancer prevention or therapies. Methods Study populations and genotyping. Epidemiological data were obtained from three separate consortia that had all conducted genotyping using the iCOGS array, a custom array comprising approximately 200,000 SNPs. (i) Data on overall breast cancer risk, tumor subtypes and grade came from 50 breast cancer case-control studies participating in BCAC; these comprised 41 studies from populations of European ancestry and 9 studies from populations of East Asian ancestry 3 . Details of the participating studies, genotype calling and quality control are given elsewhere 3 . After quality control exclusions, we analyzed data from 46,451 cases and 42,599 controls of European ancestry and 6,269 cases and 6,624 controls of Asian ancestry. A further 23 SNPs were directly genotyped in two case-control studies (CCHS and SEARCH). The ER status of the primary tumor was available for 34,539 European and 4,972 Asian cases; of these, the tumor was ER − for 7,465 (22%) European and 1,610 (32%) Asian cases 3 . (ii) Data on BRCA1 mutation carriers were obtained through CIMBA. Eligibility is restricted to females 18 years or older with pathogenic mutations in BRCA1 or BRCA2 . The majority of the participants were sampled through cancer genetics clinics 37 , including some related participants. Fifty-one studies from 25 countries contributed data on BRCA1 mutation carriers who were genotyped using the iCOGS array 38 . After quality control of the phenotypes and genotypes, data were available on 15,252 BRCA1 mutation carriers, of whom 7,797 had been diagnosed with breast cancer, all of European ancestry. Analyses in BRCA1 mutation carriers assessed associations with breast cancer risk. (iii) Mammographic density information was available for 7,025 women from ten studies in BCAC and, in addition, 1,621 women from the Mayo Mammographic Health Study (MMHS). All were additionally participants in the MODE Consortium. Forty-six women were excluded because of missing BMI information, leaving 8,600 women with mammographic density information, relevant covariates and iCOGS genotyping (2,955 breast cancer cases and 5,645 controls). Study details are given in Supplementary Table 14 and in Lindstrom et al . 26 . Mammographic density measurements were performed on digitized analog mammographic films using 'Cumulus' software 39 . This applies a thresholding technique to measure the total area of the breast and the absolute dense area, from which the absolute non-dense area and percent dense area are derived. Dense areas and non-dense areas were converted to cm 2 according to the pixel size used in the digitization. Readers blinded to genotype, case status and risk factor data conducted all measures. For cases, mammograms before the diagnosis of breast cancer were used or, where this was not possible, measures from the contralateral breast were used. SNP selection, genotyping and imputation. We first defined a mapping interval of ∼ 1 Mb (chr. 6: 151,600,000–152,650,000; NCBI Build 37 assembly). We catalogued 2,821 variants with a MAF >2% using the 1000 Genomes Project (March 2010 Pilot version 60 CEU project data); of these variants, we selected 277 SNPs correlated with the 3 previously reported associated SNPs (rs2046210 (ref. 1 ), rs3757318 (ref. 2 ) and rs3020314 (ref. 40 )) at r 2 >0.1, plus a set of 698 SNPs designed to tag all remaining SNPs with r 2 >0.9. Of the SNPs, 902 that passed quality control were included in this analysis. After completion of iCOGS genotyping, this initial set was supplemented with a further 23 SNPs selected from the October 2010 (Build 37) release of the 1000 Genomes Project, to improve coverage. These SNPs were genotyped in two large BCAC studies (CCHS and SEARCH) comprising 12,273 cases and controls, using a Fluidigm array according to the manufacturer's instructions. Using the above data, results for all the additional known common variants (MAF >0.02 in Europeans) on the January 2012 release of the 1000 Genomes Project were imputed using IMPUTE version 2.0. Quality control and imputation steps were carried out separately in the different consortia, leading to slight differences in the numbers of SNPs with available data. In addition to the 902 successfully genotyped SNPs, genotypes at 2,972 SNPs were imputed in BCAC and 2,907 SNPs were imputed in CIMBA (imputation r 2 score >0.3 in each case). In total, 3,872 genotyped or imputed SNPs were available for the combined BCAC ER − and CIMBA BRCA1 mutation carrier meta-analysis Statistical analysis. Case-control analysis, logistic regression and retrospective cohort analyses. For the case-control analysis in BCAC, per-allele odds ratios and standard errors were estimated for each SNP using logistic regression, separately for subjects of European and Asian ancestry and for each tested phenotype. Principal components were included as covariates as previously described 21 . The statistical significance of each SNP was derived using a Wald test. To evaluate evidence for multiple association signals, we performed conditional analyses in which the association for each SNP was reevaluated after including other associated SNPs in the model. SNPs with a P value <1 × 10 −4 and MAF >2% in the single-SNP analysis were included in this analysis 21 . Haplotype-specific odds ratios and confidence limits were estimated using haplo.stats 22 . Associations between genotypes and breast cancer risk in BRCA1 mutation carriers in CIMBA were evaluated using a per-allele trend test with 1 degree of freedom ( P trend ), based on modeling the retrospective likelihood of the observed genotypes conditional on breast cancer phenotypes 41 . To allow for non-independence among related individuals, an adjusted test statistic was used that took into account the correlation in genotypes 21 . Per-allele hazard ratio estimates were obtained by maximizing the retrospective likelihood. All analyses were stratified by country of residence. Conditional analyses were performed to identify SNPs independently associated with each phenotype. To identify the most parsimonious model, all SNPs with a marginal P value <1 × 10 −4 were included in forward selection regression analyses with a threshold for inclusion of P < 1 × 10 −4 and including terms for principal components and study. Similarly, forward selection Cox regression analysis was performed for BRCA1 mutation carriers, stratified by country of residence, using the same P -value thresholds. This approach provides valid significance tests of the associations, although the estimates quantifying the association can be biased 41 , 42 . Parameter estimates for the most parsimonious model were obtained using the retrospective likelihood approach. Within MODE, mammographic dense area, non-dense area and percent dense area were each square-root transformed to fit a normal distribution. For the ten MODE and BCAC studies, a linear regression assuming a multiplicative per-allele model adjusting for study, age at mammogram, BMI, menopausal status (pre or post) and the first six principal components was carried out for each trait and for each SNP. The MMHS participants were analyzed separately in the same way but without the principal-components covariates, and the results were combined with those from BCAC using a standard inverse variance–weighted fixed-effects meta-analysis. Expression analysis. eQTL analyses were conducted in 57 normal breast samples from the GTEx Project 43 and 135 adjacent normal breast samples from women of European origin in the METABRIC study 44 . For the METABRIC analyses, matched gene expression (Illumina HT-12 v3 microarray) and germline SNP data from either genotyping (Affymetrix SNP 6.0) or imputation (1000 Genomes Project, March 2012 data using IMPUTE version 2.0) were used. Correlations between the five signal-representative SNPs and expression levels of nearby genes (500 kb upstream and downstream of the SNPs) were assessed using a linear regression model in which an additive effect on expression level was assumed for each copy of the rare allele. Calculations were carried out using the eMAP library in R. Allele-specific expression analysis. ASE analysis has been described previously 11 . Three SNPs for signal 1, two SNPs for signal 3 and a proxy SNP for signal 2 ( r 2 = 0.85) were on Affymetrix SNP Array 6.0. TCGA genotype calls and corresponding confidence scores were retrieved using level 2 TCGA SNP array Birdseed data downloaded from the TCGA portal. Genotyping data with a confidence score of 0.1 were excluded. We selected 742 breast cancer samples with European ancestry. The corresponding RNA-seq BAM files and metadata are available from the Cancer Genomics Hub (CGHub). Marker SNPs, the exonic SNPs of the target genes, were extracted from dbSNP human Build 142 (collectively ∼ 800 SNPs for ESR1 , RMND1 , ARMT1 and CCDC170 ), and RNA-seq read counts on SNP sites for reference and alternative alleles were computed. Homozygote marker SNPs and those with low coverage (less than 15×) were excluded. Major allele fraction ( μ ) representing allelic imbalance for each marker SNP was computed, and an average of allelic imbalances for each gene was calculated for individual tumor samples. Marker SNPs with extreme μ values ( μ >0.75) were not included in the analysis. Level 3 SNP array data were downloaded from the TCGA portal, and GISTIC version 2.0.16 was used to identify copy number variations (CNVs) for each sample. Samples with low or high CNV levels, as presented in the gene-based GISTIC module report, were excluded from the analysis of the corresponding gene. For each risk SNP, allelic imbalance for the target transcripts was compared between heterozygote (AB) and homozygote (AA and BB) samples. For a given risk SNP and target gene, we used Levene's test, a more robust test than the F test, for equality of variances when the risk SNP was not in linkage disequilibrium with any of the marker SNPs on that gene ( r 2 <0.5). Otherwise, a two-tailed t test was used for equality of means 45 . Estrogen receptor protein expression. Normal breast samples derived from 150 postmenopausal donors (non-Hispanic, mean age of 62 years) and identified through the Susan G. Komen for the Cure Tissue Bank at the Indiana University Simon Cancer Center were used in this study 46 . DNA was extracted from blood cells at the Indiana CTSI Specimen Storage Facility using an AutogenFlex Star instrument (Autogen) and the FlexiGene AGF3000 blood kit for DNA extractions (Qiagen). SNP analysis was performed with 1 ng of DNA using TaqMan genotyping assays for rs2046210 (C_12034236_10), rs3757322 (C_27475059_10), rs9397437 (C_11556300_10), rs851984 (C_2496819_10), rs9918437 (C_29496189_10) and rs2747652 (C_2823750_10) from Life Technologies, following the manufacturer's protocol. ER protein abundance was measured by immunohistochemical semiquantitation using an antibody to ERα (clone 6F11; 1:40 dilution; Leica Microsystems) and quantified with (i) an H score consisting of the sum of the percent of tumor cells staining, multiplied by an ordinal value corresponding to the intensity level (0, none; 1, weak; 2, moderate; 3, strong; Supplementary Fig. 2 ), and (ii) the percentage of positive cells. Correlations between the H scores and ER immunohistochemistry values were calculated using Spearman's rank correlation analysis. All P values reported are two-sided, and values <0.05 were considered statistically significant. Cell lines. Breast cancer cell lines MCF-7 (ER + ; American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) HTB22), T-47D (ER + ; ATCC HTB133) and BT-474 (ER + ; ATCC HTB20) were grown in RPMI medium with 10% FCS and antibiotics. Normal breast epithelial cell lines MCF10A (ATCC CRL 10317) and Bre-80 (provided as a gift from R. Reddel, Children's Medical Research Institute, Sydney) were grown in DMEM/F12 medium with 5% horse serum (HS), 10 μg/ml insulin, 0.5 μg/ml hydrocortisone, 20 ng/ml epidermal growth factor, 100 ng/ml cholera toxin and antibiotics. Cell lines were maintained under standard conditions, routinely tested for mycoplasma and short tandem repeat (STR) profiled. Chromatin conformation capture. 3C libraries were generated using EcoRI, HindIII or BglII as described previously 15 . 3C interactions were quantified by RT-PCR (qPCR) using primers designed within restriction fragments ( Supplementary Table 15 ). qPCR was performed on a RotorGene 6000 instrument using MyTaq HS DNA polymerase (Bioline) with the addition of 5 mM Syto9, an annealing temperature of 66 °C and an extension time of 30 s. 3C analyses were performed in three independent 3C libraries from each cell line, with each experiment quantified in duplicate. BAC clones (RP11-108N8, RP11-713G5, RP11-450E24 and RP11-55K19) covering the 6q25 region were used to create artificial libraries of ligation products to normalize for PCR efficiency. Data were normalized to the signal from the BAC clone library and, between cell lines, by reference to a region within GAPDH . All qPCR products were electrophoresed on 2% agarose gels, gel purified and sequenced to verify the 3C product. Electromobility shift assays. Gel shift assays were performed with ER + MCF-7 or ER − Bre-80 nuclear lysates and biotinylated oligonucleotide duplexes ( Supplementary Table 16 ). Nuclear lysates were prepared using NE-PER nuclear and cytoplasmic extraction reagents (Thermo Fisher Scientific) according to the manufacturer's instructions. Total protein concentrations in nuclear lysates were determined by Bradford's method. Duplexes were prepared by combining sense and antisense oligonucleotides in NEBuffer2 (New England BioLabs) and heat annealing at 80 °C for 10 min followed by slow cooling to 25 °C for 1 h. Binding reactions were performed in binding buffer (10% glycerol, 20 mM HEPES (pH 7.4), 1 mM DTT, protease inhibitor cocktail (Roche), 0.75 μg poly(dI:dC) (Sigma-Aldrich)) with 7.5 μg of nuclear lysate. For competition assays, binding reactions were preincubated with 1 pmol of competitor duplex ( Supplementary Table 17 ) at 25 °C for 10 min before the addition of 10 fmol of biotinylated oligonucleotide duplex and a further incubation at 25 °C for 15 min. Reactions were separated on 10% Tris-borate-EDTA (TBE) polyacrylamide gels (Bio-Rad) in TBE buffer at 160 V for 40 min. Duplex-bound complexes were transferred onto Zeta-Probe positively charged nylon membranes (Bio-Rad) by semidry transfer at 25 V for 20 min and then cross-linked onto the membranes under 254-nm ultraviolet light for 10 min. Membranes were processed with the LightShift Chemiluminescent EMSA kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific) according to the manufacturer's instructions. Chemiluminescent signals were visualized with the C-DiGit blot scanner (LI-COR). Plasmid construction and reporter assays. Promoter-driven luciferase reporter constructs were generated by the insertion of PCR-amplified fragments containing ESR1 , ARMT1 , RMND1 and CCDC170 promoters into the KpnI and MluI sites of pGL3-Basic. To assist in cloning, AgeI and SbfI sites were inserted into the BamHI and SalI sites downstream of the luciferase gene. A 1,496-bp signal 1 PRE fragment, a 997-bp signal 2 PRE fragment, a 1,566-bp signal 3 PRE fragment, a 1,463-bp signal 4 PRE fragment and a 1,349-bp signal 5 PRE fragment were generated by PCR or gBlocks (Integrated DNA Technologies) and cloned into the AgeI and SbfI sites of the modified pGL3-Promoter constructs. The minor alleles of individual SNPs were introduced into the PRE sequences by overlap extension PCR or gBlocks. Sequencing of all constructs confirmed variant incorporation (AGRF). ER + MCF-7 and BT-474 or ER − Bre-80 cells were transfected with equimolar amounts of luciferase reporter plasmids and 50 ng of pRL-TK transfection control plasmid with Lipofectamine 3000. The total amount of transfected DNA was kept constant at 600 ng for each construct by the addition of pUC19 as a carrier plasmid. Luciferase activity was measured 24 h after transfection by the Dual-Glo Luciferase Assay System. To correct for any differences in transfection efficiency or cell lysate preparation, firefly luciferase activity was normalized to Renilla luciferase activity, and the activity of each construct was measured relative to the promoter-only construct, which had a defined activity of 1. Statistical significance was tested by log transforming the data and performing two-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett's multiple-comparisons test in GraphPad Prism. Chromatin immunoprecipitation. ER + MCF-7 and BT-474 breast cancer cells were cross-linked with 1% formaldehyde at 37 °C for 10 min, rinsed once with ice-cold PBS containing 5% BSA and once with PBS, and collected in PBS containing 1× protease inhibitor cocktail (Roche). The cells were centrifuged for 2 min at 900 g . Cell pellets were resuspended in 0.35 ml of lysis buffer (1% SDS, 10 mM EDTA, 50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.1, 1× protease inhibitor cocktail) and sonicated three times for 15 s each with a 70% duty cycle (Branson SLPt) followed by centrifugation at 15,000 g for 15 min. Supernatants were collected and diluted in dilution buffer (1% Triton X-100, 2 mM EDTA, 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.1). Two micrograms of antibody was prebound for 6 h to Protein G Dynabeads (Life Technologies) and then added to the diluted chromatin for overnight immunoprecipitation. The magnetic bead–chromatin complexes were collected and washed six times in RIPA buffer (50 mM HEPES, pH 7.6, 1 mM EDTA, 0.7% sodium deoxycholate, 1% NP-40, 0.5 M LiCl) and then twice with TE buffer. To reverse the cross-linking, the magnetic bead complexes were incubated overnight at 65 °C in elution buffer (1% SDS, 0.1 M NaHCO 3 ). DNA fragments were purified using a QIAquick Spin kit (Qiagen). For qPCR, 2.0 μl from a 100-μl immunoprecipitated chromatin extraction was subjected to 40 cycles of amplification. All PCR products were sequenced by Sanger sequencing (AGRF). The antibodies used were to CTCF (C-20; sc-15914) and GATA3 (HG3-31; sc268) or control IgG (sc-2027) (all from Santa Cruz Biotechnology). ChIP primers are listed in Supplementary Table 18 . URLs. 1000 Genomes Project,  ; Breast Cancer Association Consortium (BCAC),  ; Consortium of Investigators of Modifiers of BRCA1 and BRCA2 (CIMBA),  ; Collaborative Oncological Gene-environment Study (COGS),  ; iCOGS,  ; SNAP,  ; The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA),  ; Cancer Genomics Hub (CGHub),  ; eMAP,  . Accession codes. The relevant SNP genotype data underpinning these analyses can be accessed by applying to the BCAC and CIMBA consortia (see URLs ).
abstract: We analyzed 3,872 common genetic variants across the ESR1 locus (encoding estrogen receptor α) in 118,816 subjects from three international consortia. We found evidence for at least five independent causal variants, each associated with different phenotype sets, including estrogen receptor (ER + or ER − ) and human ERBB2 (HER2 + or HER2 − ) tumor subtypes, mammographic density and tumor grade. The best candidate causal variants for ER − tumors lie in four separate enhancer elements, and their risk alleles reduce expression of ESR1 , RMND1 and CCDC170 , whereas the risk alleles of the strongest candidates for the remaining independent causal variant disrupt a silencer element and putatively increase ESR1 and RMND1 expression.
news_body: An international study of almost 120,000 women has newly identified five genetic variants affecting risk of breast cancer, all of which are believed to influence how breast cells respond to the female sex hormone oestrogen. Breast cancer is the most common type of cancer among women. Around one in eight women in the general population is expected to develop the disease at some point in her life. The majority of cases occur in women aged 50 and over. The female sex hormone oestrogen acts as a trigger, binding to a molecule known as an oestrogen receptor in most breast cells and triggering a cascade of signals that cause the cell to behave normally. However, the oestrogen receptor is switched off in some cells and these do not respond to the hormone. An international collaboration, led by researchers at the University of Cambridge and the QIMR Berghofer Medical Research Institute, examined the DNA surrounding the gene for the oestrogen receptor—known as ESR1—in women with different types of breast cancer against those of healthy controls to identify genetic variants responsible for an increased risk of breast cancer. The results are published today in the journal Nature Genetics. Among their findings, the researchers discovered five cancer-risk variants based within or around the ESR1 gene. This gene has long been known to be related to the risk and progress of breast cancer, but little is understood of how it works and why it should affect breast cancer. Of the five variants discovered by the team, four were more strongly associated with tumours where the ESR1 gene is switched off, so the tumour cells have no oestrogen receptors. These represent around one fifth of breast cancers. One of these four variants was of particular interest as it was associated with a rarer type of breast tumour that contain active receptors for the protein known as 'human epidermal growth factor 2' (HER2). Such tumours can be treated by the drug trastuzumab (marketed as Herceptin). This is believed to be the first time a specific genetic risk factor for HER2 positive breast tumours has been found. Dr Stacey Edwards' team from QIMR Berghofer, Brisbane, had been searching for gene regulatory elements around the ESR1 gene, which act like the volume controls on a radio or TV, turning the activity of the nearby genes up or down. There are two major types of gene regulators: 'enhancers', which increase activity of the genes express such that they make more protein, and 'silencers', which have the opposite effect. When the Cambridge and Brisbane teams compared notes, they spotted that four of the breast cancer risk variants coincided with 'volume-up' enhancers. These particular regulators did not just affect the ESR1 gene but also other nearby genes. The variants that increased risk of breast cancer directly reduced the effectiveness of each enhancer, hence turning down the volume of ESR1 and the other nearby genes. This reduced the amount of oestrogen receptor produced by breast cells. The researchers say that their results suggest the ESR1 gene works with other nearby genes to affect breast cancer development. The fifth genetic variant was found to be more strongly associated with tumours where the oestrogen receptor is switched on. This variant coincides with and alters the effectiveness of 'volume-down' silencer, which means that it increases the amount of oestrogen receptor protein produced by breast cells. "It's interesting that all five of the genetic variants that we have found affect levels of oestrogen receptors in breast cells," says Dr Alison Dunning from the Department of Oncology at the University of Cambridge, one of the lead authors on the study. "This suggests that there may be a 'Goldilocks' level of these receptors in breast cells: too few or too many and the breast cells are more likely to become cancerous." "As our research looks at how tumours with and without the oestrogen receptor are regulated, it's possible it could help make sense of the enduring mystery of how tamoxifen works and why tumours develop in these two divergent ways," says Dr Edwards, one of the study's senior authors. "Our findings could open the way to developing new, more specific breast cancer preventions." The genetic variants are all very common, each one carried by around one in three women. Each variant only increases the risk of developing breast cancer by a small amount. Professor Doug Easton, another senior author from the University of Cambridge, adds: "breast cancer is a very complex disease, with many genes, and other factors, contributing to an overall increased risk of developing the disease. These five common variants that we have identified will contribute to an eventual predictive test for breast cancer risk, and for determining the risk of the particular subtype of breast cancer, that will include hundreds of similar variants."
generated_news_body:
**Title: New Genetic Insights Unveil the Complexity of Breast Cancer Risk Factors**

**Subtitle: Groundbreaking study identifies multiple genetic variants affecting breast cancer subtypes and mammographic density.**

In a significant leap forward for breast cancer research, scientists have uncovered key genetic details that shed light on the complexities of what influences this disease. The extensive study, conducted by an international team of researchers, analyzed nearly 4,000 genetic variants in the estrogen receptor gene region, using a massive dataset of over 118,000 individuals. Their findings reveal not only the multiple layers of genetic influence but also potential pathways for targeted therapies.

The research focused on genetic variations in the ESR1 gene, which is known to encode the estrogen receptor α, a crucial component in many breast cancers. Estrogen receptors are proteins activated by the hormone estrogen, influencing the growth and development of some breast cancer cells. Importantly, this study identified at least five independent genetic variants that play different roles in various breast cancer traits, including certain tumor subtypes and mammographic density.

One of the standout findings is the association of certain genetic variants with estrogen receptor-negative (ER-) tumors. These variants seem to lie in parts of the DNA known as enhancer elements, which can significantly impact gene expression. In particular, the risk alleles—the versions of these genetic variants associated with disease—were shown to reduce the expression of several key genes: ESR1 itself, RMND1, and CCDC170. This diminished gene activity could influence how these tumors develop and progress.

Conversely, the study found that other genetic variants disrupt a silencer element, leading to increased expression of ESR1 and RMND1. This discovery hints at a complex interplay where both increased and decreased expressions of these genes have roles in different breast cancer subtypes.

This cutting-edge research not only advances our understanding of the genetic underpinnings of breast cancer but also opens the door for more personalized treatment strategies. By pinpointing these genetic factors, scientists hope to develop therapies that specifically target the molecular characteristics of different tumor types, potentially improving outcomes for countless patients worldwide.

As researchers continue to explore these genetic pathways, there is hope for more refined risk assessments and prevention strategies. This study emphasizes the importance of genetic research in unraveling the intricate details of cancer and ultimately fostering innovative solutions for disease management and treatment.

paper_body: Abstract Conventional electron linear accelerators are essential research tools but limited in providing high beam currents. Energy recovery technology enables high beam currents with reasonable and sustainable power supply requirements by recycling the electrons’ kinetic energy. Independently, higher beam energies can be achieved if electrons are accelerated multiple times in a linear accelerator. The combination of both techniques results in a multi-turn energy recovery linear accelerator, which is capable of providing high beam power. Here we report the demonstration of efficient energy recycling in multi-turn operation where we saved up to 87% of the consumed beam power in the main linear accelerator of the superconducting Darmstadt electron linear accelerator (S-DALINAC). In this setting, the cumulative phase slippage effect, caused by the different speeds of the electrons per main linear accelerator pass and the resulting different interactions with the alternating electric field, cannot be neglected and was compensated. Our proof-of-principle demonstration shows how multi-turn energy recovery linear accelerators can outperform conventional machines due to the potential for considerable power saving while providing higher beam power.     Main Electron linear accelerators (LINACs) are indispensable for applications in fundamental research 1 , industry 2 and medicine 3 . Future research projects 4 , 5 , 6 require extraordinary beam power, which cannot be realized sustainably using conventional LINACs. If conventional LINACs, with their potential for low beam emittances, were used for a desired electron beam power of 1 GW (ref. 5 ), this power would have to be provided externally. Use of an energy recovery LINAC (ERL) can greatly reduce the external radio-frequency (RF) power required 7 , 8 , 9 . Depending on the energy recycling efficiency, orders of magnitude in RF power can be saved, so that deployment of this type of particle accelerator can considerably enhance research sustainability. In an ERL, energy consumed to accelerate an electron bunch is recovered during deceleration and recycled through the acceleration of a subsequent electron bunch. In the same way, energy consumed to accelerate this subsequent electron bunch is recovered, which is why the energy is continuously recycled. Thus, the acceleration power for a certain beam current required by an ERL is only a small fraction of that which a conventional LINAC would require. Consequently, for a certain available power supply, more electrons can be accelerated at the same time using an ERL instead of a conventional LINAC, so the beam current and thus the beam power can be increased considerably. The idea of energy recovery using an electron LINAC was proposed in 1965 by M. Tigner 7 and has become an extensive field of research 8 , 9 . Energy recovery was already achieved 1975 by passing a LINAC twice in opposite directions 10 . In the technology used today, accelerated and decelerated beams pass the LINAC in the same direction 11 , 12 , 13 . To date, some ERLs have been realized 14 , 15 , 16 , 17 , 18 , 19 , 20 , 21 or are in progress 22 using the concept of single-turn acceleration and deceleration. To achieve higher beam energies, LINACs should be passed several times for acceleration, and subsequently as often for deceleration to ensure maximum energy recovery. Such multi-turn ERL operations were realized using normal-conducting cavities 23 , 24 , whereby the recovered energy is mostly transformed into heat loss and can therefore no longer be used to accelerate subsequent electrons. This is therefore better termed recuperation rather than recovery. To store the recovered energy efficiently, cavities in a superconducting state are necessary 8 . Multi-turn acceleration and deceleration using superconducting cavities has been realized 25 , but substantial saving of RF power consumption has not been measured. To prove that multi-turn ERLs are capable of providing higher beam currents than conventional multi-turn LINACs for the same RF power, a quantitative demonstration of substantial energy recycling during multi-turn ERL operation is required. Here, we show a substantial saving in beam power consumption via recycling of the recovered energy during superconducting multi-turn ERL operation. Our proof-of-principle demonstration at the superconducting Darmstadt electron linear accelerator (S-DALINAC, Fig. 1 ) clearly evidences that superconducting multi-turn ERLs can provide efficient energy recycling, paving the way for future high-beam-power applications. Fig. 1: Layout of the S-DALINAC. The electron beam is generated at the gun and boosted in the injector LINAC before bending into the main LINAC for multi-turn acceleration and deceleration. Due to its recirculation beamlines, the machine provides several acceleration modes. In particular, it supports multi-turn energy recovery operation. In the presented experiment, a two-fold energy recovery mode was realized. Here, the beam was guided back to the main LINAC using the first and second recirculation beamline. The third recirculation beamline was not used in this experiment but offers the possibility for a future three-fold energy recovery mode. The splitter magnet defines the design orbits and the related momenta to be reached. Depending on the mode, the beam can be stopped either at the high-energy dump (pure acceleration modes) or at the low-energy dump (energy recovery modes). The presented experiment is a pure demonstration of multi-turn energy recovery operation, without experiment. In the future, the electron beam can be used for an experiment at the intended interaction point. The recirculation beamlines as well as the injector arc offer the possibility to set an individual longitudinal dispersion, necessary to influence the longitudinal phase space. The injector RF monitor and the injector Faraday cup are used to measure the initial beam current. The injector Faraday cup is also used to block the electron beam during mode changes. One of the modes studied requires that the once-decelerated beam is stopped destructively. Since the once-decelerated and the once-accelerated beam share the same recirculation beamline, a screen with a hole is required, which allows the once-accelerated beam to pass undisturbed while the once-decelerated beam is stopped. Full size image Multiple passing of a LINAC provides the accumulation of the electrons’ energy gain per pass, enabling higher beam energies compared with single-pass operation. To pass a LINAC several times, the electrons are guided back to the LINAC using recirculation beamlines. The length of the recirculation beamlines must be matched to the RF oscillation period of the alternating electric fields inside the cavities. In the following, an ultra-relativistic case (where each electron’s speed is considered to be the speed of light, c ) is described to approach the multi-turn ERL concept. During the multi-pass acceleration process, the length of each recirculation beamline must be chosen such that the design particle’s time of flight, t f, a , through a recirculation beamline, a , is an integer multiple, n a , of the RF oscillation period, t RF , that is, t f, a = n a × t RF . After the last LINAC pass for acceleration, a phase shift must be induced so that the electrons arriving at the cavities will be decelerated for the first time. Therefore, the time of flight, t f, b , through the recirculation beamline between the last acceleration and the first deceleration, b , must satisfy the relation t f, b = ( n b + 1/2) × t RF . After the first deceleration, the time of flight, t f, d , in each downstream recirculation beamline, d , must satisfy t f, d = n d × t RF for a continuous multi-pass deceleration. The recirculation beamlines a and d can be identical or different, as discussed in more detail below. After the last deceleration, the beam leaves the LINAC with an energy equal to its original injection energy, provided that no energy is lost due to collective or interaction effects in the system. It should be emphasized that alternating electric fields are the prerequisites for being able to accelerate and decelerate superimposed electron beams in the same LINAC if the electrons pass the LINAC in the same direction. Exploitation of alternating fields, in turn, requires that the electrons are in a bunched structure. While travelling through the LINAC, the centres of those bunches that are accelerated are separated by a distance of c × t RF and those bunches that are decelerated are in between the accelerated bunches, that is, their centres are about c × t RF /2 ahead or behind the closest accelerated bunch centre, respectively. Further details are given below. The ultra-relativistic approximation is insufficient at low energies of the electrons as their speed still changes appreciably with momentum change. Due to the different speeds of the electrons at given locations in a LINAC during the various acceleration and deceleration passes, the electrons interact differently with the alternating electric fields at these locations per LINAC pass. This cumulative phase slippage effect is stronger for lower energies and cannot be neglected in the lower megaelectronvolt range. There are different possible multi-turn ERL schemes 26 . In the sharing model, the electron bunches leave a LINAC after each deceleration with the same energy that they had before when they left the very LINAC after a given stage of intermediate acceleration. In this case, accelerated and decelerated beams of the same energy are guided in the same beamlines. Consequently, the beam-guiding elements of these beamlines must be optimized for two beams. In the individual model, which requires at least two spatially separated LINAC sections, the electron bunches leave each of the LINACs after each deceleration with intermediate energies that were not reached after acceleration at those particular sections before. In this case, accelerated and decelerated beams leaving a given LINAC section always have different energies and, hence, can be guided in individual beamlines due to different beam rigidities, allowing for individual optimization and higher flexibility for beam tuning. While manufacturing and maintenance costs can be reduced using the sharing model, the individual model allows for better compensation of phase slippage and facilitates robust operation. Since the beams recirculating in the same beamline are not individually tunable in the sharing model, the use of that model entails the particular challenge of including the effect of phase slippage in advance via beam dynamics simulations due to a lack of degrees of freedom to reach the correct target momenta after each LINAC pass 27 , 28 . Degrees of freedom are the amplitudes and phases of the alternating electric fields inside the cavities of a LINAC, the path length adjustments of the recirculation beamlines and the longitudinal dispersion in the arc sections (Extended Data Table 1 ). The first-order longitudinal dispersion, R 56 , is defined for a section from s 1 to s 2 via \(R_{56,\overline {s_1s_2} } = {\int}_{s_1}^{s_2} {R_{16}(s)/\rho \left( s \right){{{\mathrm{d}}}}s}\) , where R 16 is the first-order horizontal dispersion and ρ is the bending radius. Higher-order longitudinal dispersion was not considered in our study due to a lack of degrees of freedom. As can be seen from the simulated longitudinal phase space (Extended Data Fig. 1 ), such a consideration was not necessary for the tuning beam current of 1 nA due to sufficient small longitudinal bunch parameters (Extended Data Table 2 ). However, a sophisticated choice of higher-order longitudinal dispersion can beneficially influence the longitudinal phase space 26 . The associated optimization problem to find suitable values for the mentioned degrees of freedom is described in Methods . Results of particle tracking along the accelerator using optimum degrees of freedom are visualized in Extended Data Figs. 1 and 2 . Our experiment took place at the S-DALINAC, where a single-turn ERL had been realized as a first step previously 20 . A substantial energy recovery during multi-turn ERL operation was measured in August 2021. The sections of the S-DALINAC relevant for this experiment are shown in Fig. 1 . Details on beam preparation up to the actual injection process relevant for energy recovery are given in Methods . It is desirable to realize ERLs with low injection energies to increase the efficiency. To verify the feasibility of multi-turn ERLs with low injection energies or momenta, respectively, a centroid momentum of 5 MeV/ c was chosen for injection, implying large phase slippage through the system. The choice of that injection momentum defines all further momenta (Extended Data Table 3 ). To evaluate the multi-turn ERL performance, we compare a conventional two-fold acceleration (CTA) mode with a two-fold energy recovery (TER) mode, as shown schematically in Fig. 2 . The accelerator sections relevant for the realization of the multi-turn ERL mode are located downstream of the injector LINAC. Here, lattice parameters were set according to the results of the optimized beam dynamics simulations ( Methods ). First, the beam was guided through the injector arc where R 56 = −0.01 m was set to create a low initial correlation between momentum and longitudinal position within the bunch. It was then accelerated to the target centroid momentum of 23.66 MeV/ c during the first main-LINAC pass. The amplitudes and phases of the alternating electric fields inside the cavities of the main LINAC were set based on the values listed in Table 1 , which correspond to the optimized setting under consideration of the present phase slippage. After the first acceleration, the beam was guided back to the main LINAC using the first recirculation beamline where R 56 = +0.33 m was set. After the second LINAC pass, the beam reached the target centroid momentum of 41.61 MeV/ c to be properly bent downstream into the second recirculation beamline due to the increased beam rigidity. Here, an interaction point for future experiments is intended (Fig. 1 ). Afterwards, the beam could either be stopped in the high-energy dump while losing the entire kinetic energy via heat loss (CTA mode) or be guided back to the main LINAC for energy recovery (TER mode). Therefore, the path length of the second recirculation beamline 29 was adjusted for deceleration in the main LINAC. To ensure a sufficiently small momentum spread to keep the beam within the acceptance until dumping, R 56 = +0.18 m was set in the second recirculation beamline. The first deceleration followed during the upcoming main-LINAC pass, and thus kinetic energy was recovered via storing it in the electric fields inside the cavities. After the first deceleration, the centroid momentum of the electrons was equal to the centroid momentum after first acceleration (23.66 MeV/ c ), hence the beam rigidity was identical to the one after the first main-LINAC pass. Therefore, the once-decelerated electrons were guided into the first recirculation beamline sharing the same design orbit with once-accelerated electrons (sharing model). The once-decelerated bunches had a time offset of about t RF /2 = 167 ps relative to the neighbouring once-accelerated bunches, which corresponds to a distance of about c × t RF /2 = 5 cm. The second deceleration and hence second energy recovery followed during the fourth main-LINAC pass. After having transferred most of their kinetic energy back to the electric fields inside the cavities, the electrons left the main LINAC with a centroid momentum equal to the injection centroid momentum (5 MeV/ c ). Due to the low beam rigidity, the splitter magnet forced the beam towards the low-energy dump (Figs. 1 and 2 ). Fig. 2: Comparison of operation modes. a , The CTA mode. The path of the injector beam (1), the first acceleration in the main LINAC (2), the first recirculation (3), the second acceleration in the main LINAC (4), the guiding into the second recirculation beamline and possible beam usage at the intended interaction point (5) and finally dumping at high energy (6). b , The TER mode. The first five steps are identical to the CTA mode. Instead of dumping at high energy, the beam is guided back to the main LINAC with an introduced phase shift of roughly 180° (7) so that the beam is decelerated for the first time in the main LINAC (8). Due to the now decreased beam rigidity, the beam is guided back into the first recirculation beamline, thus sharing the orbit with the once-accelerated beam (9). The beam is decelerated for a second time (10) and finally dumped at an energy equal to the injection energy (11). c , d , Artistic visualization of the situation in selected cells of the main-LINAC cavities at a fixed time. The direction of the longitudinal electric field is alternating from cell to cell because the cavities are operated in π-mode. c , Since the accelerator is operated in continuous-wave mode, once- (red) and two-fold (blue) accelerated bunches superimpose in the main LINAC in every second cell while operating in the CTA mode. In these accelerating cells, the electric field lines point in the opposite direction to the electrons’ direction of flight (beige). The decelerating cells with reversed electric field lines (orange) remain unoccupied. d , In the TER mode, once- (green) and two-fold (black) decelerated bunches superimpose additionally in the main LINAC. Due to the π-mode, accelerated and decelerated bunches are located in adjacent cells. Full size image Table 1 Working point to operate the S-DALINAC in two-fold ERL mode Full size table The accelerator is operated in continuous-wave mode, and therefore single and two-fold acceleration and single and two-fold deceleration occurred simultaneously (but for different electrons and in different cavity cells). Since the cavities are operated in π-mode, every second cavity cell contained single- and two-fold accelerated electrons and the neighbouring cavity cells contained single- and two-fold decelerated electrons (Fig. 2 ). The previous consumed energy for acceleration was later on recovered during deceleration. Due to the continuous-wave mode and π-mode operation, the energy recovered within a certain cell during the deceleration of electrons was already recycled after about t RF /2 = 167 ps when subsequent electrons sojourned in the very same cell for acceleration. Only about another t RF /2 later, completely different electrons arrived in the very same cell to recover kinetic energy during deceleration, which restarted the cycle. To evaluate the achieved energy recycling efficiency, we measured the beam power consumption in both the CTA and TER mode. In addition, the beam power consumption was measured after single acceleration and single deceleration, providing the single-deceleration efficiency and transmission information. The CTA and the TER mode were realized as described above. The single-acceleration mode was realized via blocking the once-accelerated beam in the first recirculation beamline. To investigate the single-deceleration mode, the beam was deflected slightly using a dipole magnet in the second recirculation beamline, so that the once-decelerated beam was stopped at a screen with a hole in the downstream first recirculation beamline. This enabled an undisturbed pass of the non-deflected once-accelerated beam through the hole. The beam power consumption was measured with a newly implemented RF-power measurement system 30 (see Methods for details and Fig. 3 for results). For a long-time measurement with an initial beam current of 2.3 μA, the beam loading (which is the beam power consumption), P b , in the TER mode was 13.8 ± 1.1 W compared with 86.3 ± 0.3 W in the CTA mode. The energy recycling efficiency is given by η = ( P b,CTA − P b,TER )/ P b,CTA , and for the mentioned case η = 84.0 ± 1.2% applies. To investigate the efficiency as a function of the initial current, the latter was ramped from 0.2 to above 7 μA (Fig. 3b ). The beam loading for the single-acceleration mode and the single-deceleration mode was almost identical. That is, in the single-deceleration mode, the energy required to accelerate for the second time was recovered during the first deceleration and recycled by accelerating subsequent electrons. Furthermore, no transmission losses occurred during the first deceleration or upstream. Fig. 3: Measured beam power consumption per mode. The measurements show the beam power consumption (beam loading) in the main LINAC for four different modes: two-fold deceleration (TER mode), single deceleration (dec.), two-fold acceleration (CTA mode) and single acceleration (acc.). To record the data, the individual modes were realized by changing the orbits using steering magnets and Faraday cups or screens to block the beam at different locations, respectively. While changing a mode, the beam was blocked at the injector Faraday cup. Visualized error bars represent the s.d. of the 1 s bins (see Methods for details on the post-processing). a , Stable long-time operation with an initial beam current of 2.3 μA. The beam loading (blue solid) decreases to zero while changing the mode as there is no beam in the main LINAC. During these mode changes, the beam current is measurable at the injector Faraday cup (black dashed). b , Beam loading for the four modes mentioned (red, orange, blue and green) and the resulting energy recycling efficiency (black) dependent on the initial beam current measured with the injector RF monitor. Here, a mode was kept while ramping the initial beam current, so that the beam current had to be ramped four times in total. Source data Full size image Due to the use of the sharing model and the corresponding lack of degrees of freedom, it is a challenge to find a transverse confinement for the superimposed beams in the first recirculation beamline. We managed to guide the centroid of the once-decelerated beam on its design orbit but did not find a solution to keep the entire beam within acceptance and therefore lost a part of the beam in the last recirculation. That section could be identified as the location of first particle loss based on the beam loading measurements during single deceleration as well as from the observations using beam loss monitors and screens with holes during tuning. As a result, not all electrons could restore their energy during the second deceleration, which limited the efficiency in our proof of principle. After the last deceleration, the beam was completely lost while guiding to the low-energy dump, preventing a current measurement at the final destination. An increased initial current led to larger transverse emittances, bunch length and momentum spread downstream of the injector LINAC, and thus the relative part of those electrons that were no longer within the acceptance increased, which is why the efficiency was decreasing as a function of the initial current. However, the efficiency dropped more strongly than expected on the basis of simulations, which indicates that the increase of the aforementioned quantities was not the only cause of efficiency change. More detailed quantitative measurements of the beam properties as a function of beam intensity in the multi-turn energy recovery mode will be the subject of further investigation. However, if particle losses can be avoided, even higher efficiencies closer to the target of 100% could be achievable. One option for avoiding particle losses could be the realization of the individual model, which makes it easier to find a transverse confinement due to additional degrees of freedom. That model also provides further advantages such as more extensive control of the longitudinal phase space via individual longitudinal dispersion per recirculation or a more comfortable compensation of energy losses due to synchrotron radiation relevant for future high-energy multi-turn ERLs. However, the individual model is more expensive to set up than the sharing model due to the need for additional beamlines. The RF power consumption for a given beam current has been reduced substantially in our experiment, which in turn allows the beam current to be increased many times over conventional operation while supplying the same amount of external RF power. Our results together with further developments on multi-turn ERLs 31 , 32 , 33 provide a promising basis for future high-power beams that also support sustainability aspects. Methods Accelerator and electron beam We carried out this experiment at the S-DALINAC 34 at Institut für Kernphysik, Technische Universität Darmstadt. The beam was generated via a 250 kV electrostatic thermionic gun, followed by a normal-conducting chopper cavity generating a bunched beam with a frequency of 3 GHz. The chopped beam passed a normal-conducting TM 010 cavity introducing velocity bunching, before the longitudinally compressed bunches entered the injector LINAC. The injector LINAC consists of a 6-cell and two 20-cell superconducting cavities, which accelerated the beam to a centroid momentum of 5 MeV/ c . The cavities of the injector LINAC were set up so that a momentum spread of (6.5 ± 0.7) × 10 −4 and a bunch length of 0.36 ± 0.04 ps resulted for a tuning beam current of about 1 nA. Downstream, the beam was guided as described above. The main LINAC consists of eight 20-cell superconducting cavities. The splitter and merger magnets specify the design orbits and the fixed ratios of the corresponding bending radii, which in turn specify the target momenta, depending on the initial momentum. After beam tuning, the current of the bunched beam was changed during the experiment between 0.2 μA and more than 7 μA. Fluorescence and optical transition radiation screens, Faraday cups, wire scanners, RF monitors and beam loss monitors were used for beam diagnostics. In those sections where superimposed beams occurred, screens with and without holes were used. By inducing a temporary displacement, all superimposed beams could be observed individually. More details on measured beam quantities are given in Extended Data Table 2 . Beam dynamics simulations For multi-turn energy recovery operation in the cost-efficient model, where accelerated and decelerated beams share the same recirculation beamlines, the setup must be precisely determined in advance due to a lack of degrees of freedom 27 , 28 . Searching for a suitable setup requires the consideration of the following points: The centroid momenta of the electron bunches have to be equal to the corresponding target momenta after each main-LINAC pass to ensure flying on the correct orbit defined by the splitter magnet. Furthermore, it is important that the momentum spreads are small in the entire accelerator to keep the beam within the accelerator’s acceptance 35 . Additionally, a particularly small momentum spread at the location where an experiment could take place (in the second recirculation beamline, after second acceleration) is desirable. Therefore, a sophisticated manipulation of the longitudinal phase space is necessary. The highest overall efficiency is achieved when each cavity recovers exactly the energy that it supplies. This forces a symmetrical structure, which in turn limits the number of degrees of freedom and therefore the possibility of influencing the longitudinal phase space, ergo the momentum spread. In this proof-of-principle demonstration, the nuance of symmetry was omitted and we focused on achieving the target momenta and small momentum spreads, whereby a high efficiency was still achieved. The problem was solved numerically using the simplex-based optimization algorithm of the software ‘elegant’ 36 . The codes are available from a TUdatalib repository 37 . First, the problem $${\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\min} {\sum \limits_{i = 1}^4}\; {f\left( {{\bar p_i}\left( {{A_1}, \ldots ,{A_8},{\phi _1}, \ldots ,{\phi _8},{L_{\mathrm{F}}},L_{\mathrm{S}}} \right),p_{0,i},T} \right)} \\{\mathrm{subject}}\,{\rm{to}} \left( {{A_1}, \ldots ,{A_8},{\phi _1}, \ldots ,{\phi _8},L_{\mathrm{F}},L_{\mathrm{S}}} \right) \in M \end{array}}$$ was solved, where A 1 to A 8 are the peak on-axis longitudinal electric fields inside the main LINAC’s cavities, ϕ 1 to ϕ 8 are the phases of these electric fields and L F and L S are the path length adjustments in the first and second recirculation beamline. These degrees of freedom can be freely selected within their individual technical limits, here indicated by the bounded set M , and are listed in Extended Data Table 1 . The index \(i \in \{ 1,2,3,4\}\) represents the main-LINAC pass. The variable \(\bar p_i\) is the centroid momentum after main-LINAC pass i , and p 0 ,i is the target momentum after main-LINAC pass i (Extended Data Table 3 ). The penalty function f is defined via $$f\left( {\bar p_i,p_{0,i},T} \right) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0,} & {|\bar p_i - p_{0,i}| \le T} \\ {\left( {\frac{{|\bar p_i - p_{0,i}| - T}}{T}} \right)^2,} & {|\bar p_i - p_{0,i}| > T} \end{array}} \right.,$$ where T is a threshold parameter to specify the precision of the centroid momenta (Extended Data Table 3 ). The problem was written compactly above using the summation symbol Σ; it should be emphasized that \(\bar p_1\) does not depend on L F and L S , and \(\bar p_2\) does not depend on L S . Second, the first-order longitudinal dispersions of the injector arc, R 56,I , of the first recirculation beamline, R 56,F , and of the second recirculation beamline, R 56,S , were varied to find the optimum momentum spread. Technical limits for the R 56 values are listed in Extended Data Table 1 . The simulations were performed with a two-dimensional Gaussian beam profile truncated after three s.d. with a momentum spread of σ δ = 6.5 × 10 −4 and a bunch length of σ t = 0.36 ps. All iterations of the algorithm were initially performed with a bunch consisting of 100 electrons to keep the simulation time short. The resulting variables were verified with a bunch consisting of 5,000 electrons and finally used for the experiment setup. The simulated behaviour of the longitudinal quantities \(\bar p\) , σ δ and σ t along the accelerator is presented in Extended Data Fig. 2 . The simulated longitudinal phase space is visualized in Extended Data Fig. 1 . Setup of degrees of freedom For the setup of the amplitudes and phases of the alternating electric fields inside the cavities of the main LINAC, the second cavity up to the last cavity were switched off and detuned to prevent resonances with the electron beam. Then, the on-crest acceleration for the first cavity during the first main-LINAC pass leading to the value specified in Table 1 was sought, before the off-crest phase was set. After that, the second cavity was switched on and tuned to the correct frequency, before proceeding in the same way as for the first cavity. This was repeated step by step until all eight cavities were set up. In this way, the main LINAC was set up only during the first pass, whereby the setting was valid for all passes and thus phase slippage was compensated as intended. The implementation of the R 56 values was based on simulated quadrupole settings using the software ‘elegant’ 36 . To ensure that the correct values for all R 56 had been set up, time-of-flight measurements 38 , 39 were carried out, measuring the RF monitors’ phases as a function of varied centroid momenta. The setup of the path length adjustment systems took place via monitoring of beam loading and optical feedback. RF-power stability and measurement The digital RF control system of the S-DALINAC stabilizes the amplitudes, A , and phases, ϕ , of the alternating electric fields inside the superconducting cavities 40 . During stable long-time multi-turn ERL operation with an initial current of 2.3 μA, (relative) r.m.s. values of σ A / A 0 < 5 × 10 −4 and σ ϕ < 0.3° were achieved for the cavities of the main LINAC, respectively. The setting of the amplitude is limited by the RF control only. Determination of the on-crest phases was done via monitoring of beam loading and optical feedback, and could be determined with a precision of |Δ ϕ | ≤ 0.5°. The RF forward power, P f , and reverse power, P r , were measured via the in-house designed power measurement system 30 . The beam loading, P b , was then determined by P b = P f − P r − ( P 0 + P t ), where P 0 is the dissipated power and P t is the transmitted power. The power consumptions P 0 + P t were determined in the absence of the beam, while it was blocked at the injector Faraday cup. Data post-processing The forward and reverse power were measured individually. These parameters were monitored via an ‘Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System’-based data acquisition system 41 . In the post-processing, the data had to be resampled to determine the beam loading, P b . The data visualized in Fig. 3 were downsampled into 1 s bins. The energy recycling efficiency shown in Fig. 3b was determined by calculating η = ( P b,CTA − P b,TER )/ P b,CTA from the linear splines for the CTA and TER data and applying a Savitzky–Golay filter 42 to counteract the artificial noise. Data availability The experimental data presented in this manuscript are available from a TUdatalib repository 43 . Source data are provided with this paper. Code availability The codes that support the findings of this study are available from a TUdatalib repository 37 .
abstract: Conventional electron linear accelerators are essential research tools but limited in providing high beam currents. Energy recovery technology enables high beam currents with reasonable and sustainable power supply requirements by recycling the electrons’ kinetic energy. Independently, higher beam energies can be achieved if electrons are accelerated multiple times in a linear accelerator. The combination of both techniques results in a multi-turn energy recovery linear accelerator, which is capable of providing high beam power. Here we report the demonstration of efficient energy recycling in multi-turn operation where we saved up to 87% of the consumed beam power in the main linear accelerator of the superconducting Darmstadt electron linear accelerator (S-DALINAC). In this setting, the cumulative phase slippage effect, caused by the different speeds of the electrons per main linear accelerator pass and the resulting different interactions with the alternating electric field, cannot be neglected and was compensated. Our proof-of-principle demonstration shows how multi-turn energy recovery linear accelerators can outperform conventional machines due to the potential for considerable power saving while providing higher beam power.
news_body: Particle accelerators are devices that use electromagnetic fields to speed up particles and collide them together or against a specific target. These devices are widely used by physicists to study particles, the forces that drive them and interactions between them. The larger and most advanced particle accelerators in the world consume huge amounts of energy. In a paper published in Nature Physics, researchers at Technische Universität Darmstadt recently introduced a new measurement at their particle accelerator that can retain a high beam power while consuming significantly less energy. "Particle accelerators are available in compact designs, such as those used for X-ray machines, and as large-scale facilities that are used for fundamental research," Manuel Dutine, one of the researchers who carried out the study, told Phys.org. "The latter in particular consume an enormous amount of energy. This raises the question whether the added value justifies the realization of large research-accelerator facilities, especially from an economic and sustainable point of view." Future large-scale particle physics research will require accelerators with an increasingly high beam power. The key objective of the work by Dutine and his colleagues was to create an accelerator that could help to meet these needs while consuming less energy, using a technology known as energy recovery. "The concept of energy recovery in particle accelerators is not new but under continuous development," Dutine explained. "To achieve higher beam energies at reduced resource needs, the energy recovery concept should be combined with multi-turn acceleration, which was the purpose of our research." Individual steps of the multi-turn energy recovery mode. The electron beam is generated, accelerated to maximum energy and could then be used for an experiment (1)-(5). In a conventional mode, the beam would be dumped at high energy after it has been used for an experiment (6), where the energy stored in the electron beam would be wasted. In the energy recovery mode, the electron beam is instead decelerated (7)-(10) and then dumped at low energy (11). During deceleration, energy is recovered in the cavities of the main linear accelerator. The recovered energy is then recycled by accelerating subsequent electron bunches. By passing the main accelerator twice (multi-turn), higher beam energies can be achieved. Credit: F. Schliessmann et al./Nature Physics. Credit: Schliessmann et al. Accelerators accelerate beams, which are essentially a large set of particles, and this process unavoidably consumes energy. A high energy accelerated beam could collide with a resting target, another beam of particles or even laser light, potentially resulting in desired reactions that can then be studied. Reactions of interest, however, are very rare, and most particles in a beam end up not interacting with anything during an experiment. "In a conventional accelerator, the remaining beam is dumped at high energy and the energy is wasted," Dutine said. "In an ERL (energy recovery linac), the beam's energy is recovered by decelerating the bunches. The energy is temporarily stored in the microwave fields in the accelerating structures and can afterwards be used to accelerate the next upcoming low-energy bunches." The ERL accelerator developed by Dutine and his colleagues has so far achieved very promising results. In initial tests, the team measured its energy recovery and recycling (i.e., the use of recovered energy for accelerating subsequent particle bunches) while accelerating an electron beam. "By measuring energy savings, we demonstrated that the multi-turn energy recovery concept works," Dutine said. "We measured a recovery of up to 87% of consumed beam power in the main accelerator. This power saving is financially relevant for beams with Gigawatt power or may help to reduce the footprint of energy consumption for large-scale facilities." The new multi-turn energy recovery accelerator created by this team of researchers could help to carry out physics studies more efficiently and sustainably, consuming less power while still achieving high beam energies. In the future, its unique design could inform the development of other accelerators with even lower fabrication costs and energy consumption, thus further promoting sustainable research. Dutine and his colleagues are now planning to upgrade their particle accelerator and also build a new one. "The potential for energy saving is even larger for facilities with increased beam current, beam energies and number of turns," Dutine added. "Here we are limited by the properties of our existing facility. We have started discussions with scientific partners, funding agencies, and our ministry to expand this research to a next-generation facility, in which we want to include our current findings, establish the energy recycling in a particle accelerator at larger scale, and thereby convince potential users from international science centers or industry to consider energy-recovering for their particle-accelerator needs."
