Full size image Here, we detail a modular protocol to obtain and characterize 3D artificial muscles by using hiPSCs from healthy donors or from patients with muscular dystrophies; the muscles can be mono- (muscle only), bi- (muscle + motor neurons), tri- (muscle + endothelial cells + pericytes) or tetra-lineage (muscle + endothelial cells + pericytes + motor neurons). We also provide a stepwise guide on how to image and quantify nuclear shapes in bioengineered muscles, which captures the nuclear phenotypes in models of skeletal muscle laminopathies (Fig. 4 ). We then highlight proof-of-principle examples of applications of this platform for testing gene therapy vectors or assessing cell migration in vitro, as well as for generating and implanting 3D muscles for in vivo studies (Fig. 5 ). Comparison with other methods There are currently (i.e., at the time of writing and reviewing this paper) three main alternative, fully hiPSC-based platforms for advanced skeletal muscle modeling in a 3D, organoid-like setup 32 , 33 , 34 . Rao et al. generated inducible myogenic progenitor cells from healthy donor human embryonic stem cells and induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) capable of differentiating into bundles of skeletal myofibers by modulating Wnt signaling, followed by overexpression of the myogenic factor Pax7 32 . Although this method enables assessment of functional parameters in bioengineered constructs, to our knowledge it has not been validated with a transgene-free, small molecule–based method of hiPSC differentiation capable of recapitulating developmental myogenesis. In addition, it has not been extended to multilineage hiPSC differentiation and has not used hiPSCs from genetic muscle or neuromuscular diseases. Most available systems for studying and modeling neuromuscular junctions (NMJs) and diseases have been developed by using murine cultures (examples include Cvetkovic et al. 15 and Machado et al. 42 ). However, Osaki et al. combined motor neuron spheroids differentiated from hiPSCs of a patient affected by amyotrophic lateral sclerosis with 3D hiPSC-derived skeletal muscle fiber bundles in an optogenetically controllable microfluidic device 33 . Similarly to Rao et al., chemically or electrically induced muscle force contraction followed by Ca 2+ transients were assessed 32 . More recently, a similar platform has been used by Paredes-Redondo et al. to model neuromuscular defects in Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) 43 . Both systems require a specialized microfluidic setup and have not assessed the effect mediated by the addition of key cell types such as vascular cells in muscle constructs; moreover, this setup has not been shown to be amenable for implantation in vivo. Recently, the autoimmune neuromuscular disorder myasthenia gravis was modeled by using hiPSC-derived organoids showing functional NMJs 34 . hiPSC-derived neuromesodermal progenitors generated 3D aggregates originating spinal cord neurons, skeletal muscle and Schwann cells capable of self-organizing to form functional NMJs. This model relies on self-assembly of the cell populations and lacks external cues to enable systematic parallel alignment of myofibers. Thus, although these platforms have their strengths (e.g., being amenable for functional assessments, using a microfluidic device, organoid-like self-organization), most have not been validated to model muscular dystrophies or lack the organization of muscle structure and the higher order of complexity from the presence of other supporting and muscle interstitial cells. Limitations of the platform The platform that we describe enables modeling of phenotypic features of native muscle tissue. However, it currently exhibits suboptimal maturation, similarly to the vast majority of other hiPSC-derived 3D cultures. Nonetheless, we show here that this limitation does not affect the potential of the platform to model severe muscle disorders with known congenital phenotypes. Further engineering the muscle constructs to include additional supporting cells (such as fibroblasts, fibro-adipogenic progenitors and/or macrophages) 3 and refining their organization to prolong time in culture will probably enhance maturation. In addition, more in-depth assessment of the PAX7 + population is also required to define their identity within the 3D muscle constructs (e.g., fetal myoblasts versus satellite-like stem cells). Importantly, NMJs 44 form in our model 31 but have not yet been functionally assessed. Using bi-lineage 3D constructs containing optogenetically engineered motor neurons to induce muscle contraction could provide a strategy for testing NMJ function and enhancing maturation in future work. From a translational perspective, the size of 3D muscle constructs can be scaled down into microtissues to enhance suitability for screening programs 45 , 46 or scaled up with in vitro ‘vascularization’ strategies 47 . However, although the key matrix of our scaffold (fibrin) is biocompatible, readily available in clinical-grade formulations and amenable for autologous use 48 , minor components such as Matrigel require replacement with defined materials to facilitate the scaling up of production for preclinical applications. Applications This 3D artificial muscle platform can be used to model features of normal or diseased human skeletal muscle, such as myofiber formation, alignment and growth, interaction with supporting cell types (e.g., vascular niche and NMJs; Fig. 3a ) and establishment of a muscle stem cell pool. Importantly, with this platform we pioneered hiPSC-based 3D modeling of muscular dystrophy 30 , 31 , unraveling key cellular phenotypic hallmarks of severe muscle disorders caused by defective nuclear envelope proteins. Specifically, 3D muscles outperformed conventional 2D cell monolayers when used to model skeletal muscle laminopathies, where they better reproduced the abnormally shaped nuclei associated with the underlying mutation in the nuclear envelope gene LMNA . Using this strategy, we defined nuclear major axis length in three dimensions as an objective phenotypic readout for screening programs (Fig. 4 ). Nuclear dysmorphic features correlate with the severity of clinical presentation in a mutation-specific fashion, with the most common and severe LMNA R249W mutation 49 having the most deformed and elongated nuclei (Fig. 4f ). Notably, this has been recently independently validated in an alternative 3D platform by using LMNA -mutant myoblasts 50 . Phenotypic readouts obtained from our platform go beyond morphological parameters. We also measure key downstream functional skeletal muscle outcomes such as contractility and calcium dynamics (Fig. 3c and Supplementary Videos 1 and 2 ). These, and other readouts, can be used in therapy development pipelines, based on small molecules or advanced products such as gene therapy vectors (see examples in Fig. 5a,b ). The multilineage nature of our 3D constructs (Fig. 3a ) may enable testing of the cell specificity, toxicity and efficacy of drugs (new or repurposed) and advanced therapies (including viral vectors and cell therapies; Fig. 5a–c and Supplementary Video 3 ) in a humanized isogenic platform in vitro. This opens new avenues in personalized medicine beyond disease modeling, including drug and therapy testing, toxicity studies and in vivo implantation for tissue replacement. Here, we provide examples of further applications of this platform for in vitro therapy testing and in vivo studies. Specifically, we show that hiPSC-derived 3D muscles can be used to monitor expression in live human tissues in vitro and in real time of (i) a nonintegrating, nonviral vector used in preclinical gene and cell therapy studies for DMD (DYS-HAC: a human artificial chromosome containing and expressing the full dystrophin gene locus) 51 (Fig. 5a ) and (ii) adeno associated viral vector (AAV) serotypes (AAV9) used in neuromuscular gene therapy clinical trials 52 (Fig. 5b ). Notably, the latter example highlighted dose-response correlation to different transgene doses and set the foundation for further work investigating the cell and lineage specificity of different AAV serotypes for precision medicine. Moreover, we also provide proof of principle for use of this 3D platform for evaluating advanced therapies beyond viral vectors and show that it can be used to assess engraftment and migration of myogenic cells 53 within isogenic 3D muscles (both derived from the same hiPSC source), providing a unique strategy to assess myogenic cell transplantation in a quasi vivo environment (Fig. 5c ) 54 . This 3D organoid-like platform has a strong impact for animal welfare in research, because it significantly refines, reduces and replaces animal use in preclinical research. The 3D, patient-specific, multicellular and isogenic nature of the artificial muscles reduces the number of animals required for in vivo work to validate a restricted number of disease pathogenesis or therapy/toxicity testing experiments. Of note, we have performed biocompatibility studies and shown engraftment of hiPSC-derived 3D muscles (both mono- and multi-lineage) in immunodeficient mice subjected to acute volumetric muscle injury or loss, where they are vascularized and integrated with the host tissue (Fig. 5c–h ) 31 , providing a basis for tissue-replacement strategies using this approach (Fig. 5d–h ), as well as for in vivo testing of therapeutics selected from in vitro work. We foresee this platform innovating and accelerating science and technology by (i) studying human myogenesis to understand early disease phenotypes not captured by biopsy-derived skeletal myoblasts, (ii) including further cellular components from less-studied but critical niches and supporting cell types such as fibro-adipogenic progenitors and myotendinous junctions and (iii) harnessing the potential of emerging microfabrication and microfluidic techniques to generate advanced muscle-on-chip models for precision medicine. Experimental design Overview of the procedure This protocol is modular in design, which allows for multiple variations and versatile applications. Its three main stages are as follows. The first step is monolayer expansion of progenitor cells to be used in making engineered muscles (Step 1). Here, we report using up to four different cell lineages (including the option of two different methods of progenitor cell derivation for two lineages): myofibers (Step 1A and B), motoneurons (Step 1C and D), endothelial cells and pericytes (Step 1E). The second step is generating and culturing the 3D artificial muscle constructs (Step 2). The choice of which progenitors to expand is based on the type of construct needed (e.g., with or without vascular cells or innervation (Fig. 1b )). The third step is the use of engineered muscles in downstream applications (Steps 3–17 and Boxes 1 – 4 ; Figs. 1 , 4 and 5 ). Notably, we foresee that a number of alternative or new methods to derive myogenic and nonmyogenic cell lineages can also be easily adapted to our protocol to engineer muscle, which could then be assessed as reported here, including using functional contractility and calcium-imaging assays 55 . Box 1 Light-sheet microscopy method Timing 5–18 h The steps listed are the same as recommended by the manufacturer, with minor changes to better fit 3D artificial skeletal muscles. Please note that due to tissue thickness, opacity and abundance of extracellular matrix, depth of imaging is often limited to a just a few layers of myofibres. The same sample can be imaged by both light-sheet and confocal microscopy. It is advisable to not excessively manipulate the artificial muscle, to preserve its structure, and to perform the following steps quickly to avoid photobleaching of the fluorophores. Procedure 1 Prepare the light-sheet fluorescence microscope by setting up the objective (5×), the filter cubes and perfusion chamber (filled with PBS to the top of the chamber windows) and place them onto the chamber mount. Make sure that the relevant filter cubes are installed. 2 Assemble the syringe into the microscope’s sample holder disk with a syringe adapter ring. 3 After immunolabeling (steps 4–9 below), using forceps, place the construct on a 10-cm dish. Ensure that there is no excess of PBS but that the construct is not completely dry. 4 Dip the tip of a needle into a droplet of Superglue and attach to the furthest end of the muscle construct, having a couple of millimeters of overlap between the needle and construct so that both are in tandem. Critical step This step must be performed rapidly because Superglue sets quickly. 5 Leave to set for 30 s and then attach the needle to the syringe. 6 Slowly mount the sample holder disk onto the microscope stage and into the upper opening. 7 Using the Zen software ‘locate sample’ tool, find and center the specimen along the three axes. 8 In the acquisition tab, set up the light path by defining the necessary tracks. 9 Activate the multi-view checkbox in the multidimensional input field and using the now-available z-stack tool window, select the upper and lower limit for imaging by using the ‘set first’ and ‘set last’ buttons, the interval (recommended: 1 μm) and the number of rotations (recommended: 3). 10 Start imaging of the constructs by selecting ‘start experiment’. 11 Once done, remove the holder disk from the stage and the needle from the syringe. 12 Place the sample gently onto a 10-cm dish and hold the needle at 30° and then quickly slide a blade along the needle to detach the Superglued muscle construct from the needle. 13 Using forceps, place the construct back into a PBS-filled conical tube and keep away from light at 4 °C. Pause point Samples can be stored in the dark at 4 °C for ≤1 month before the fluorophore signal begins to degrade. 14 The acquired image is exported in CZI file extension and imported in Arivis Vision4D software. 15 Reconstruct the 3D muscle and export images and videos. Show more Box 2 Molecular characterization Procedure (A) Gene expression analysis Timing 8–18 h (i) Wash constructs three times with PBS, then remove hydrogels from the PDMS posts as described in Step 4 and transfer to Eppendorf tubes containing 500 μl of Trizol per sample. Pause point If RNA extraction is not performed immediately, dry constructs can be stored in vials or tubes at −80 °C for ≤6 months. (ii) Homogenize for ~30 s on ice by using the Ultra-Turrax homogenizer (power level: 5; 20,500 rpm) and then incubate at RT for 5 min. (iii) Add 100 μl of chloroform per sample and shake intermittently for 3 min at RT. (iv) Centrifuge at ≥12,000 g for 15 min at 4 °C. (v) Transfer the aqueous phase containing the RNA without touching the interphase to a new Eppendorf tube. (vi) Add 250 μl of isopropanol to the aqueous phase. Incubate for 10 min and centrifuge at 12,000 g for 10 min at 4 °C. (vii) Discard the supernatant, add 1 ml of chilled 70% ethanol (vol/vol), vortex and centrifuge at 7,000 g for 5 min at 4 °C. (viii) Discard the supernatant, air-dry the RNA pellet for a few minutes at RT, resuspend it in 20 μl of nuclease-free water and measure the RNA yield and purity by using a Nanodrop. (ix) Use 1 μg of RNA to retro-transcribe to cDNA with the ImProm-II reverse transcription system kit. (x) Perform a PCR for a housekeeping gene (e.g., GADPH or TBP ) to evaluate the efficacy of the retro-transcription reaction. (xi) Perform qRT–PCR to assess gene expression (e.g., for myogenic factors) with the GoTaq qPCR master mix kit by following the manufacturer’s directions and using a Bio-Rad CFX96 machine. (B) Western blot Timing 2–3 h (i) Add ice-cold lysis buffer to 3D muscle constructs (200 μl per sample) or cell pellets (50 μl per sample) if single cell–type characterization is required before generating the constructs. (ii) Homogenize the samples for 30 s by using the Ultra-Turrax homogenizer (power level: 5; 20,500 rpm) on ice. (iii) Incubate the samples on ice for 30 min, mixing thoroughly every 5–10 min, and then centrifuge at 12,000 g for 10 min at 4 °C. (iv) Collect the supernatant (containing proteins) for downstream analysis (e.g., protein quantification or western blot assay). Pause point Protein suspensions can be stored at −80 °C indefinitely. (v) Prepare a standard curve by using BSA (8 mg/ml to 0.5 mg/ml) and quantify the samples by using the colorimetric reaction DC protein assay and the iMark microplate reader. (vi) Heat samples at 98 °C for 5 min in reducing loading Laemmli buffer. If samples are stored at −80 °C, thaw on ice before use. (vii) Load a molecular weight ladder and 30 μg of protein per sample on the 4–15% (wt/vol) precast gel and run at 100 V for 2–3 h. (viii) Transfer the proteins onto the PVDF membrane by using a semi-dry transfer system (iBlot gel transfer system) at 20 V for 7 min. (ix) Stain the membrane by using Ponceau S to confirm correct protein transfer. (x) Rinse the membrane three times by using TBST buffer and then block by using milk-TBST blocking buffer for 60 min at RT. (xi) Incubate the PVDF membrane in milk-TBST blocking buffer containing primary antibodies of choice at the respective concentration on a shaker overnight at 4 °C. Wash three times by using TBST buffer for 10 min each at room temperature. (xii) Incubate the PVDF membrane in milk-TBST blocking buffer containing HRP-conjugated secondary antibody at the respective concentration for 1 h at RT on a shaker. Then, wash three times by using TBST buffer for 10 min each at RT. (xiii) To visualize, develop the signal by incubating the membrane with ECL reagents, according to the manufacturer’s instructions, and image by using the chemiluminescence detector (ChemiDoc). Show more Box 3 Functional characterization Procedure (A) Contractility assay Timing 15 min (i) Assemble the imaging setup by placing a plate holder under the lamp and inverted Basler camera. Connect the camera to the computer. (ii) On the computer, start the Pylon software and select the Basler ace camera. (iii) Select ‘Windows’ and then ‘Recording setting’ and set the acquisition speed to 25 fps (records a frame every 40 ms) and the recording time to 20 s and select the desired destination folder where the data file will be stored. (iv) Prepare the electric stimulator and connect it to the autoclaved pacing electrodes. (v) Add 500 μl of differentiation medium preheated at 37 °C to each well to reach a total volume of 2 ml per well. (vi) Place on the plate-holder and remove the lid. Position the light above the skeletal muscle construct and position the camera under it. (vii) Gently mount the carbon electrodes on the well containing the muscle construct. Dip the two electrodes into the medium. Use Blu Tack to fix the electrodes to the plate. Troubleshooting Critical step Avoid touching the constructs with the electrodes or the electrodes touching each other. (viii) Switch on the electrical stimulator. Critical step To acquire background signal without stimulation, make sure that the pulse is off at this stage; otherwise, the muscle will be stimulated. (ix) On the Pylon software, select the camera icon and record the first video of the hydrogel without an electrical stimulus; this is the baseline. (x) Set up the desired voltage and frequency on the electrical stimulator to achieve the required current. Start with a stimulation of 2 Hz and 5 V. Increase the voltage or frequency if the aim is to further challenge the muscle. (xi) Select the new destination folder, turn the pulse switch to the on state and start recording the video immediately. Alternatively, start recording before turning on the stimulation to obtain the baseline and contraction from the same video. (xii) Turn off the pulse switch and the stimulator. (xiii) Gently remove the electrodes, fix the constructs with 4% (wt/vol) PFA for 3 h to reconstruct the nuclei in 3D or 8 h to perform normal confocal imaging and continue with Steps 4–9 for immunofluorescence. Alternatively, proceed directly to Box 2 for mRNA expression analysis or for western blotting. Constructs to be used for mRNA expression analysis and western blotting can be snap-frozen in a conical microtube in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80 °C. Analysis (xiv) To analyze the video (e.g., Supplementary Video 1 ), install the MUSCLEMOTION plugin on ImageJ. (xv) Drag the folder with the pictures onto the ImageJ toolbar and select ‘Use virtual stack’ to mount the video. (xvi) Save the entire video. Troubleshooting (xvii) Click on the MUSCLEMOTION icon in the ImageJ tool bar. In the ‘analysis parameters wizard’, insert the frame rate of the recording (25 fps in step iii), and the speed to analyze the video should be set to 1 frame. Select ‘yes, but keep it simple’ to decrease the noise in the output and detecting the reference frame and ‘No’ for analysis of the transient time. Refer to the MUSCLEMOTION user manual for further information regarding the plugin and settings. (xviii) Select the video file from its stored location in the computer for automatic processing by ImageJ-MUSCLEMOTION, which analyzes pixel changes due to motion in the video and translates it to signal intensity of contraction (for more details, see ref. 68 ). (B) Whole-construct calcium transients Timing 2–5 h (i) Incubate artificial muscles anchored to the PDMS post racks in Fluo-4 loading buffer for 30 min at 37 °C. (ii) Wash the constructs with DMEM for 5 min. (iii) Transfer the constructs and posts on glass-bottom dishes. Critical step For high-quality imaging, make sure that the constructs adhere to the bottom of the wells. If the construct does not touch the bottom of the plate, fill the gap with medium. (iv) Set the long-term time-lapse wide-field system Nikon microscope to fluorescence excitation of 488 nm with a 10× lens. Set image acquisition every 0.333 s for a total of 90 s. Critical step Ensure that the CO 2 and temperature settings in the chamber are 37 °C with 5% CO 2 . (v) Place the plate on the stage and gently mount the carbon electrodes on the well containing the muscle construct as described in Box 3 a(iv and vii). (vi) Start the time-lapse acquisition. The first 10 s are acquired as baseline; after setting up the desired voltage and frequency (as described above for the contractility assay), switch on the electrical stimulator. (vii) At the end of recording, the constructs can be fixed or used for RNA or protein extraction. Analysis (viii) Export the image series and open in Fiji/ImageJ. (ix) Select ‘Image’ and select the ‘Stacks’ option. Select ‘Plot Z-axis profile’. Save the data as a .xls file. (C) Single-cell calcium transients Timing 2–5 h (i) With the 3D artificial muscles retained on the PDMS post racks, incubate in Fluo-4 loading buffer for 30 min at 37 °C. (ii) Wash the constructs by using recording buffer. (iii) Take the 3D artificial muscles out of the medium and carefully disassemble them from the PDMS posts by using fine-tip forceps, place them in a 10-cm dish and submerge the samples in recording buffer. Critical step Remove 3D artificial muscles very gently and place carefully to avoid snapping them. For the best imaging quality, make sure that most of the construct body sticks to the bottom of the plate. (iv) Set the Zeiss 880 confocal light-sheet microscope to fluorescence excitation of 488 nm and emission collected at >530 nm and prepare to acquire as a time series at 33 fps. Critical step Make sure that the CO 2 and temperature settings in the chamber are 37 °C with 5% CO 2 . (v) Place the plate under the stage, choose the field with the most aligned myotubes and perform imaging by using a 10× objective. (vi) Add caffeine to the recoding buffer at a final concentration of 10 mM and immediately mark the time and captured frame on the microscope software. (vii) Repeat step v when the fluorescence excitation subsides, for as many times as necessary. (viii) Fix the constructs in 4% (wt/vol) PFA at 4 °C for 3 h to reconstruct the nuclei in 3D or 8 h to perform regular confocal imaging and continue with Steps 4–9 for immunofluorescence. Alternatively, proceed to Box 2 for mRNA expression analysis and for western blotting. Constructs to be used for mRNA expression analysis and western blotting can be snap-frozen in a conical microtube in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80 °C. Analysis (ix) Export the time series from the microscope and open it in Fiji software (also known as ImageJ). (x) Using the ‘Lasso’ tool, select areas (each representing a myotube and covering a maximum of 10% of its area to avoid interfering signals from other cells). (xi) From the ‘Analyze’ menu, choose ‘Set measurements’ and select ‘Mean gray value’. (xii) From the ‘Image’ menu, select ‘Stacks’, ‘Plot z axis profile’ and save as a .xls file. Show more Box 4 Disease modeling: a stepwise guide to study nuclear morphology and model laminopathy-associated nuclear abnormalities by using 3D artificial muscles Timing 3–15 h Procedure A characteristic pathological hallmark of laminopathies is nuclear shape abnormality. This aspect can be modeled by using the 3D artificial muscle platform described in this protocol and reconstructing the nuclei in three dimensions 2 . For both 3D artificial muscles and 2D classic monolayer cultures, the imaging and the 3D nuclear reconstruction were performed in the same way to facilitate comparison. For more details, see also Supplementary Protocol 1 . 1 Import the raw (LIF) files obtained in Step 17 into Imaris 8.4.1 by selecting ‘Image’ in the upper menu (the images will automatically appear in the ‘Arena’ window). Images can also be arranged in folders named ‘Assays’ or ‘Groups’ also found in the upper menu (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Steps 1–2). 2 Select the image to reconstruct, which will appear under the ‘Surpass’ window in a ‘3D view’. To reconstruct the nuclei, select ‘Add new surfaces’ on the left-hand menu. The ‘Surface’ window will show in the panel below. If the ‘Display Adjustment”’window does not appear automatically, it can be opened by selecting Edit → Show Display Adjustment . This function will allow the operator to tick and untick the channels to be visualized (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Steps 3–5). Troubleshooting 3 Imaris software: choose ‘Default’ as a Favourite Creation Parameter and proceed by selecting the blue arrow at the bottom of the panel (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Step 4). Pause point The operator can also generate and store Creation Parameters that can be applied to any number of grouped images. 4 Source channel: select the channel to be reconstructed (e.g., the red channel indicating lamin A/C), tick the box ‘Smooth’ and choose the ‘Surface Detail’ value that better sets up the smoothness of the resulting area (e.g., 0.200–0.300 µm). Tick the ‘Absolute Intensity’ box as the parameter on which to base the thresholding and proceed by selecting the blue arrow at the bottom of the panel (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Step 5). 5 Threshold: set the threshold to detect objects. This is based on the intensity of the immunofluorescence in the analyzed image. The lower the value, the more objects are detected and reconstructed within the image. By ticking the box ‘Enable’, it is possible to split the touching objects. Click on the blue arrow at the bottom of the panel (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Step 6). Critical step It is advisable to set the threshold at around a value that allows the detection of low-intensity objects while preserving the natural size of the objects with higher fluorescence intensity. 6 Classify surfaces: this function allows the removal of objects that can interfere with the final results and analysis. To achieve this, several filters can be selected (e.g., number of voxels, area and volume). Use this function to remove small unspecific and partially reconstructed objects that are not relevant. Click on the green arrow on the bottom of the panel to execute the creation steps and obtain the final reconstruction with and without myofibers (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Steps 7–9). Critical step Ensure that most unspecific objects are removed. To achieve this goal, untick or tick the ‚Volume‘ box on the upper panel to better understand what is the specific and the unspecific signal detected. 7 Major axis length analysis: upon 3D reconstruction of the nuclei, select ‘Add new measurement point’ in the left panel menu where the ‘Add surfaces’ tool is (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Step 10). 8 Choose ‘Settings’ in the panel below to set the point or line shape, size and labels. Select ‘Pairs’ for ‘Line mode’ and tick ‘Distance’ for ‘Line Labels’ (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Step 11). 9 Select ‘Edit’ and choose ‘Surface of object’ as the intersection site when adding the points. This action is performed by shift-clicking the left mouse button to add the point on to the desired object. Observe the image and use the ‘Navigate’ function on the top right corner menu under the item ‘Pointer’ to assess which myonucleus to measure (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Step 12). Critical step Use the ‘Display adjustment’ tool to assess whether a nucleus is located within or outside a myotube. 10 Choose ‘Select’ from the top right menu to pinpoint one extremity of the myonucleus by shift-clicking the mouse left button. After selecting ‘Navigate’ and turning the image, add the second extremity to the same nucleus. The distance between the two points will display automatically. To appreciate the final result from the top, select ‘Reset’ (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Steps 13–15). Critical Make sure that the extremities are positioned in the most appropriate way by selecting Surface → Color → Transparency . This function will allow a better visualization of the line throughout the objects. 11 After measuring the desired objects in the image, select ‘Statistics’, click on the ‘Detailed’ tab, ‘Specific Values’ and export the Excel files, including all the nuclear long axis values, to the desired directory. The resulting spreadsheet will include all relevant information such as the type of object measured (e.g., surface), the outputted value, the unit of measurement and the object ID to enable tracking of analyzed items. Examples of possible additional measures other than the major axis length include nuclear surface area and volume (Supplementary Protocol 1 , Step 16). Show more Preparation of cell lineages (Step 1) This section gives a brief overview of the steps required to prepare the cell types required to generate a mono- or multi-lineage 3D muscle (with further details regarding derivation in their associated publications). Standard practice is to first confirm the ability of the scaffold and biomaterial to support formation of aligned multinucleated skeletal myofibers, for example, by using primary or immortalized adult human myoblasts, which are usually easier to source (even commercially) than hiPSC-derived myogenic cells (Fig. 2a,b ). Next, we strongly advise quality-controlling the hiPSC starting populations for their identity, genomic stability, pluripotency and lack of mycoplasma contamination (Fig. 2b ) before differentiation into specific derivatives. The derivatives should also be quality-controlled as per the original publications in which those specific protocols are detailed 31 , 38 , 39 , 40 , 41 (Fig. 2c ). Medium containing serum (such as FBS) might give variable outcomes depending on the specific lot of serum, and therefore we recommend batch-testing and stocking from lots or batches that give optimal results. Skeletal myogenic cells (Step 1A and B) hiPSCs are a versatile source of skeletal myogenic progenitor cells for disease modeling, drug screening and tissue-replacement studies, because of their controllable and extensive proliferation and differentiation capacity, minimal ethical concerns and noninvasive sampling 56 . Several protocols are available to generate hiPSC-derived skeletal myogenic cells 57 . Initial studies based on controlled expression of myogenic regulators to obtain transplantable myogenic cells from hiPSCs 58 , 59 , 60 were followed by transgene-based protocols to direct hiPSC differentiation into skeletal muscle 37 , 61 , 62 , 63 . State-of-the-art strategies now use genomic integration–free, small molecule–based protocols to generate myogenic cells by mimicking embryonic myogenesis 38 , 64 , 65 , 66 . To derive skeletal muscle progenitors from hiPSCs, two approaches were used: a transgene-based and a transgene-free method. These two key modalities of iPSC differentiation into myogenic cells each have advantages and disadvantages; for example, transgene-based strategies have a consistent yield but provide limited insights into early myogenesis, whereas transgene-free approaches recapitulate developmental myogenesis with a higher fidelity but are often more expensive and prone to generate cells also from other lineages when not purified. Our transgene-based method relies on transiently expressing the myogenesis regulator MyoD in hiPSC-derived mesodermal progenitors to generate hiPSC-derived inducible myogenic cells (HIDEMs) 37 . Cells should be thawed 24 h in advance and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 3–5% O 2 . To recapitulate embryonic myogenesis with a transgene-free, small molecule–based method, we used a commercially available kit to enhance standardization 38 (Genea Biocells, now Myocea) in deriving a skeletal myoblast-like population termed ‘iPSC-derived myogenic progenitors’. Regardless of the differentiation method used, before use, it is essential to test the myogenic capacity of the generated hiPSC derivatives by using a late differentiation marker such as the key sarcomeric component myosin heavy chain (MyHC), after a minimum of 5 d of differentiation 37 . hiPSC-derived motor neurons (Step 1C and D) Derivation of hiPSC-derived neural progenitors to generate spinal motor neurons for integration into the 3D muscle construct can also be performed by using two methods. The respective neural progenitors have been used in different topographical arrangements with respect to myofibers. The first option is based upon seeding hiPSC-derived neurospheres 40 onto a polymerized artificial muscle, where they attach and innervate the construct, mimicking native innervation (i.e., motor neurons’ cell bodies outside skeletal muscles), albeit limited to the outermost layers of the 3D muscles. The second method 41 is based on co-seeding single hiPSC-derived neural progenitor cells (NPCs) together with the other cell types within the artificial muscle, which increases the number of innervated muscle fibers (but results in a heterotopic location of the motor neurons’ nuclei juxtaposed to the myofibers). Both methods are extensively detailed in their respective publications 40 , 41 , which contain important quality control steps, including confirming the identity of the neural progenitors (e.g., by using markers such as OLIG2 and differentiated motor neurons (such as neurofilament protein SMI32 and choline acetyltransferase). ECs and pericyte-like cells (PCs) (Step E) ECs and PCs have been derived by using another established protocol: that from Orlova et al. 39 . Key deviations from this protocol include cell purification by FACS. Here, we start from Step 15 in the aforementioned protocol and list the steps immediately before use of the cells in making tri- or tetra-lineage artificial skeletal muscles. FACS enrichment was used to purify CD31 + ECs from CD31 − PCs, instead of the magnetic bead purification method of the original paper 39 . We also outline lentiviral transduction of PCs with a GFP reporter to facilitate visualization in 3D muscles. A lentiviral GFP transgene is suggested here, but alternative labeling strategies are likely to be equally efficacious. Generation of hiPSC-derived 3D artificial skeletal muscles (Step 2) To support 3D assembly of hiPSC derivatives for skeletal muscle bioengineering, we adapted a platform previously developed for cardiac tissue engineering 67 (EHT Technologies). The 3D muscle construct measures approximately 8–10 mm in length and 2 mm in width. 3D constructs generated by using exclusively hiPSC myogenic derivatives are referred to as ‘single-lineage (artificial) muscles’ (Step 2A and B). Constructs containing myogenic progenitor cells and one or more cell lineages, such as neural and/or endothelial and/or pericyte cells, are referred to as ‘bi-’, ‘tri-’ or ‘tetra-’ depending on the number of lineages included or more generally as ‘multilineage (artificial) skeletal muscles’. Here, we detail the generation of bi-lineage artificial skeletal muscles from hiPSC-derived myogenic cells and motoneuron progenitors (Step 2C and D); these could be relevant for modeling physiological muscle innervation or disorders of the NMJ. For tri-lineage muscles, we provide an example using inducible myogenic cells, ECs and PCs (Step 2E); this could be relevant for modeling interactions between blood vessels and myofibers in health and disease. For tetra-lineage muscle, we use myogenic cells, ECs, PCs and motoneuron progenitors (Step 2F and G), integrating several cell types with primary or secondary dysfunction in muscle and neuromuscular disorders. For bi-, tri- and tetra-lineage constructs, we describe procedures using myogenic cells made through our transgene-based approach, although transgene-free approaches could also be used upon optimization of culture media. Our protocol has a modular design, allowing multiple combinations depending on experimental design and desired outcomes, and will probably be amenable to addition of further cell types important in muscle pathologies, such as fibrogenic cells. Different media combinations are detailed in the Materials and Reagents sections. Single-lineage muscles last up to 10 d on average in culture, whereas multilineage constructs have a longer stability: up to 24 d tested, but likely longer if required. We have successfully generated hiPSC-derived artificial skeletal muscles of different cellular complexity from multiple (>10) healthy donor and dystrophic hiPSCs (e.g., from Duchenne, LMNA -related and limb girdle 2D muscular dystrophies), and we do not foresee any special or complex considerations for adapting the procedure for other muscular dystrophies. Characterization of hiPSC-derived 3D artificial skeletal muscles (Step 3) Artificial skeletal muscles need to be characterized before proceeding to downstream applications. We categorize these experiments into three different tiers (Fig. 3 ). Tier 1 includes essential morphological characterization experiments to be performed with every artificial skeletal muscle to confirm the successful generation of single- or multilineage constructs. Tier 2 (desirable) experiments include additional imaging (such as electron or light-sheet microscopy), gene and protein expression analyses and assessment of a stem or progenitor pool (e.g., by immunolabeling and quantifying the number of PAX7 + cells; Fig. 3b ). Tier 3 (advanced) experiments are usually restricted to specific applications of the 3D muscle constructs and include contractility and functional assays (e.g., calcium dynamics) as well as implantation and biocompatibility experiments (Fig. 3c and Supplementary Videos 3 and 4 ). Morphological characterization (Steps 4–17) Immunolabeling using lineage-specific markers is required to confirm the specific hiPSC derivatives included in the artificial skeletal muscles (Fig. 3a ). We recommend using MyHC or Titin to identify myofibers, SMI32 for motor neurons and CD31 for ECs. To facilitate detection of PCs, we suggest staining for SM22 (an actin-binding protein of the calponin family) after purification to confirm their identity, followed by labeling with a fluorescent reporter before incorporation in the hydrogel (e.g., with a phosphoglycerate kinase (PGK)-GFP lentiviral vector). Light-sheet microscopy (Box 1 ) Light-sheet microscopy can be conducted to study the 3D morphology of artificial skeletal muscles, their cell distribution and localization of proteins in different areas of the construct (Fig. 3b and Supplementary Video 4 ). Expression analysis (Box 2 ) For mRNA expression analysis and western blotting, conventional methods of RNA and protein extraction from tissues are used (Fig. 3b ). To preserve morphology for immunofluorescence, we recommend fixing muscle constructs in 4% (wt/vol) paraformaldehyde (PFA) for 8 h (or, 1% (wt/vol) PFA overnight) before removing them from the polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) posts. Staining samples immediately after PFA fixation is recommended, although fixed artificial muscles can be kept at 4 °C in PBS for ≤1 month with good outcomes. We recommend reducing 4% (wt/vol) PFA fixation to 3 h for nuclear immunolabeling and 3D reconstruction. Incubation times of antibodies in permeabilization buffer are longer than normally used for monolayer cultures, to enable better antibody penetration. Artificial muscles are then mounted on slides with a concave depression and imaged by using confocal microscopy (Fig. 3a ). 3D muscle can also be paraffin-embedded and sectioned for immunohistochemistry 31 , although this is beyond the scope of this protocol. Functional analysis (Box 3 ) Finally, assessment of muscle contraction in vitro and implantation of the constructs in vivo in mice enable the study of more complex phenomena and readouts, such as maturation and function of sarcomeres, force production, fragility of myofibers and vascularization or innervation of the 3D muscles from the host (in this protocol, we limit assessment of contractility to in vitro assays, because functional in vivo testing requires more specialized expertise and equipment and infrastructure).To test for functional myofibers within the artificial muscles, we administered caffeine to induce calcium transients via sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium release and visualized them by using Fluo-4 AM, which binds to released calcium, exhibiting fluorescence that was recorded by using a confocal microscope (Fig. 3c ). Disease modeling (Box 4 ) We validated the use of our 3D artificial skeletal muscle system to model key cellular hallmarks of laminopathies such as abnormal nuclear morphology. We have also shown that our 3D constructs more accurately recapitulate those nuclear phenotypic readouts compared to monolayer cultures and discovered that nuclear elongation is a prominent and measurable phenotypic outcome of myonuclei for possible in vitro therapy testing 29 . To use the phenotype of myonuclei in disease modeling, 3D hiPSC-derived artificial skeletal muscles from laminopathy patients should be immunolabeled for LAMIN A/C and a sarcomeric protein (e.g., TITIN). Samples should then be imaged by using confocal microscopy, and the myonuclei should be three-dimensionally reconstructed and measured. For optimal imaging, we recommend acquiring z-planes every 0.5 µm. Image stacks are then imported into Imaris to generate 3D reconstructions of myonuclei (Fig. 4a–e and Supplementary Videos 5 and 6 ). Reconstruction is performed by setting the threshold to detect objects in the field and selecting the surfaces to exclude undesired items from rendering (please refer to Supplementary Protocol 1 for a stepwise guide on 3D nuclear reconstruction). Here, we describe measurement of the major axis length, as an example of an objective outcome measure (Fig. 4f ). These 3D reconstructions can also be used to explore other features of nuclear morphology (e.g., sphericity, area and volume) and positioning (e.g., distance between nuclei). Expertise needed to implement the protocol Human cell culture experience is necessary to implement this protocol, preferably with human pluripotent stem cells and with myogenic cells; experience with basic molecular biology techniques, immunolabeling and confocal imaging is also necessary. No advanced bioengineering expertise is required, given that the molds and scaffolds are commercially available and easy to use. Alternatively, users with 3D printing expertise can produce the spacers and PDMS scaffolds in house as recently described 55 . The protocol also relies on the use of a fluorescent cell sorter, which is usually outsourced to a specialist facility. Some possible downstream applications need specific expertise in viral vector handling and micro-surgical techniques, requiring specialist training, dedicated risk assessments and standard operating procedures according to institutional guidelines. Divide the solution into aliquots and store at −80 °C for ≤6 months. (Z)-4-OH tamoxifen Dissolve the powder in ethanol at a stock concentration of 25 mM and store at −20 °C for ≤1 month. Before use, thaw an aliquot, dilute to 250 μM with PBS and use diluted 1:250 in medium. ROCK inhibitor Y27362 Resuspend the powder at a final concentration of 1 mg/ml. Divide the solution into aliquots and store at −20 °C for ≤6 months. Use diluted to a 1:300 ratio in medium. Matrigel and Geltrex Thaw the stock solution on ice at 4 °C and once liquid, keep on ice and divide the solution into aliquots by using cold tips and cold tubes. Store at −20 °C according to the product’s expiration date. Thrombin Thaw the stock solution of thrombin (500 IU/ml) overnight at 4 °C. Transfer the content to a Falcon tube and make 50 aliquots. Dilute stock solution 1:10 with sterile PBS, make 7.2-μl aliquots and store at –20 °C for ≤3 months; one thrombin aliquot per hydrogel is required (see Supplementary Table 1 for details), which gives a final concentration of 3 IU/ml. Fibrinogen Thaw the stock solution of fibrinogen (3.5 mg/ml) overnight at 4 °C. Transfer to a Falcon tube, make 50-μl aliquots and store at –20 °C for ≤6 months. Do not reuse fibrinogen aliquots. Agarose 2% (wt/vol) Dissolve 2 g of UltraPure agarose in 100 ml of sterile PBS and autoclave. The 2% (wt/vol) agarose can be stored at 4 °C for several months. Aprotinin Resuspend the powder in sterile water to obtain a 33-mg/ml suspension. Aliquots can be kept at –20 °C for a maximum of 6 months, and they can be reused up to three times. Use diluted to a 1:1,000 ratio in medium. PM Resuspend 5 mg of PM in 960 μl of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to obtain a 10 mM stock solution. Make 20-μl aliquots and store at –20 °C for ≤3 months. Because DMSO is light sensitive, do not expose to light. Heparin Dissolve 5 mg of heparin in 5 ml of sterile PBS and prepare 20-μl aliquots. The latter can be stored at –80 °C for a maximum of 6 months. RA Resuspend the powder in DMSO to obtain a 3-mg/ml (10 μM) suspension. Make 20-μl aliquots and store at –20 °C for ≤12 months. Because DMSO is light sensitive, do not expose to light. Recording buffer Prepare a 10 mM glucose, 150 mM NaCl, 4.25 mM KCl, 1.25 mM NaH 2 PO 4 , 4 mM NaHCO 3 , 1.2 mM CaCl 2 , 1.2 mM MgCl 2 and 10 mM HEPES solution and adjust pH to 7.4. Fluo-4 loading buffer Prepare a recording buffer solution and add Fluo-4AM at 5 µM and Pluronic 0.002% (wt/vol). Heat-inactivated FBS Put the required volume of FBS in a 2-ml vial or tube and incubate on a preheated heating plate for 30 min at 56 °C. Heat-inactivated FBS can be used fresh or refrozen at –20 °C and used until the product expiration date. Hexadimethrine bromide (polybrene) Prepare a stock solution of 8 mg/ml by dissolving the required amount of powder in sterile water. Store the solution protected from light at 4 °C for ≤1 year. Use it at a 1:1,000 dilution in medium when transducing cells. Reagents for western blotting Lysis buffer Prepare a 50 mM Tris HCl pH 7.4, 1% (vol/vol) Triton X-100, 1 mM EDTA pH 8 and 150 mM NaCl in double distilled water solution. Then, add fresh PMSF protease inhibitor (1 mM) and a protease inhibitor cocktail (1×). Store lysis buffer at 4 °C for ≤1 week. TBS-Tween (TBST) buffer Prepare a 20 mM Tris pH 7.5 solution with 150 mM NaCl and 0.1% (vol/vol) Tween 20. Store TBST buffer at 4 °C for ≤1 week. Milk-TBST blocking buffer Mix TBST buffer with 5% (wt/vol) nonfat dry milk solution (Cell Signaling, cat. no. 999S). Prepare milk-TBST blocking buffer fresh. Composition Volume (ml) Final concentration MilliQ water 250 ml – NaCl (5 M) 150 ml 1.5 M Tris HCl (1 M) pH 7.5–7.8 100 ml 200 mM 4% (wt/vol) PFA solution Dissolve 4 g of PFA in 100 ml of PBS. Stir the solution on the hot plate and allow it to warm up to 60 °C. Adjust pH to 7.4 by adding 1 N NaOH dropwise and then wait until the solution is completely clear. Adjust the final volume with double-distilled water. Divide the solution into aliquots and store them at −20 °C for ≤6 months. Once thawed, working aliquots can be stored at 4 °C for 1 week. Caution PFA is extremely toxic and must be handled with personal protective equipment in a chemical cabinet. 2% (wt/vol) PFA solution Dilute a required volume of 4% (wt/vol) PFA solution with PBS at a ratio of 1:1 (i.e., equal volume of both). Divide the solution into aliquots and store them at −20 °C for ≤6 months. Once thawed, the working aliquots can be stored at 4 °C for a week.
