Use this reagent diluted to 1:250 and 1:125 ratios for 2D and 3D cultures, respectively. hiPSC-derived inducible myogenic cells (HIDEMs) For transgene-based myogenic differentiation of hiPSCs, follow steps 1–45 of our published protocol 37 to produce frozen stocks of HIDEMs. Cells can be stored for the long term in liquid nitrogen (e.g., >5 years). Characterization of skeletal muscle cells is also detailed in Steps 46–93 of the same protocol. hiPSC-derived myogenic progenitors Transgene-free, small molecule–mediated hiPSC differentiation into skeletal myogenic progenitor cells is performed by using a published and commercially available method 38 . In brief, passage hiPSCs by using Accutase, seed the single-cell suspension at 2.5 × 10 3 cells/cm 2 on Matrigel-coated dishes and culture in induction medium for 10 d at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 3–5% O 2 . Upon completion of this first differentiation phase, incubate the cells at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 3–5% O 2 with myoblast medium for 8 d to obtain expandable hiPSC-derived myogenic progenitors. These differentiated myogenic progenitors can be stored in a liquid nitrogen storage tank for long-term storage. Further insights into basic characterization and testing of the differentiation capacity of the hiPSC myogenic derivatives before differentiation in three dimensions are detailed in ref. 38 . Neurospheres For complete derivation of neurospheres from hiPSCs, refer to steps 3–22 of ref. 40 . The neurospheres generated can be stored for the long term in a liquid nitrogen storage tank for several years. Characterization of the cell identity and differentiation potential of the neural progenitors according to the published protocol 40 is required before use in 3D muscle cultures. NPCs As an alternative source of neuronal cells, we differentiated hiPSCs into NPCs by following a procedure described by Hall et al. 41 . This is a monolayer differentiation protocol of hiPSCs to neuroepithelium and then to NPCs. Unlike neurospheres, the NPCs obtained here can be processed as single cells while generating the 3D hydrogels. NPCs can be stored in liquid nitrogen until further use. Characterization of the cell identity and differentiation potential of the NPCs was done according to the published protocol 41 and is required before use in 3D muscle cultures. Equipment setup Equipment setup for monolayer cultures Matrigel-coated plates Thaw an aliquot of Matrigel on ice and once liquefied, dilute to a 1:100 ratio in ice-cold DMEM with a cold pipette tip. Mix thoroughly and add 1 ml of diluted Matrigel per well of a six-well plate. Incubate the mixture at 37 °C for 30 min and then remove residual Matrigel without scraping the bottom of the well for immediate use. For more details, see ref. 37 . Geltrex-coated plates Thaw an aliquot of Geltrex on ice and once liquefied, dilute to a 1:100 ratio in ice-cold DMEM/F-12 with a cold pipette tip. Mix thoroughly and add 1 ml of diluted Geltrex per well of a six-well plate. Incubate the mixture at 37 °C for 60 min and then remove Geltrex without scraping the bottom of the well for immediate use. For more details, see ref. 41 . Gelatin-coated plates Add 1.5 ml of EmbryoMax 0.1% (wt/vol) gelatin solution to each well of a six-well plate and then incubate at RT for 30 min. Discard the solution and immediately add cells resuspended in medium. Equipment setup for generating 3D artificial muscles Teflon spacers and PDMS post rack Sterilize by soaking in a large beaker filled with Milli-Q water and microwaving until the water boils. Put the beaker on a heating plate set at 100 °C for 2 h. Dry the items, place them in a container wrapped in aluminum foil and autoclave. Items must be sterilized before every use. 2% (wt/vol) agarose molds Microwave the sterile 2% (wt/vol) agarose described in Reagent setup for ≥3 min and until a completely fluid content without clumps is achieved. Keep the cap but slightly loosen it to avoid pressure build-up while microwaving. Transfer the melted 2% (wt/vol) agarose bottle into the laminar hood and quickly pipette 1.5 ml of the 2% (wt/vol) agarose into each well of a 24-well plate. Place the Teflon spacers into the wells, making sure that they are centrally positioned and let them stand undisturbed for 1 h to allow the 2% (wt/vol) agarose to solidify. Thereafter, remove the Teflon spacers to reveal the 2% (wt/vol) agarose molds. Procedure Expansion of hiPSC-derived myogenic, neuronal and blood vascular cells Timing 2–6 d 1 Prepare the cell types required to generate a mono- or multi-lineage 3D muscle. For the differentiation of hiPSCs into skeletal muscle cells, follow option A for a transgene-based method and option B for a transgene-free method. For the derivation of motor neurons, follow option C for a neurosphere-based method and option D for an NPC-based method. For the derivation of vascular ECs and PCs from hiPSCs, follow option E. (A) Skeletal muscle cells: transgene-based method Timing 2–3 d (i) Thaw HIDEMs (see Reagent setup) at 37 °C until frozen clumps are no longer visible (up to 1 min). Transfer the cells to a 15-ml Falcon tube containing HIDEM proliferation medium (in a ratio of 5 ml of HIDEM medium per 1 ml of freezing medium) and spin at 300 g for 5 min (unless specified, the temperature during cell centrifugation is not crucial). (ii) Discard the supernatant, resuspend the cell pellet in HIDEM proliferation medium, plate 4–5 × 10 3 cells/cm 2 onto an uncoated surface and incubate the cells at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 3–5% O 2 . (iii) After 24 h, replace the HIDEM proliferation medium. Continue culturing cells until ~80% confluent before using for artificial skeletal muscle generation. Pause point HIDEMs can be expanded onto uncoated surfaces by using HIDEM proliferation medium and cryopreserved by using HIDEM freezing medium 37 . (B) Skeletal muscle cells: transgene-free method Timing 2–3 d (i) Thaw hiPSC-derived myogenic progenitors (see Reagent setup) at 37 °C for ≤2 min. Transfer the cells to a 15-ml Falcon tube containing DMEM resuspension medium and spin at 300 g for 5 min at RT. (ii) Discard the supernatant, resuspend the cell pellet in myoblast medium (Myocea), plate 4–5 × 10 3 cells/cm 2 onto an uncoated surface and incubate the cells at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 3–5% O 2 . (iii) After 24 h, replace the myoblast medium (Myocea). Continue culturing until 70% confluent before use for artificial skeletal muscle generation. Critical step hiPSC-derived myogenic progenitors take 2–3 d to recover from freezing. If the culture is too confluent or cells appear flattened and large (i.e., senescing), proceed with splitting the cells either in the same flask or dish (in situ splitting) or into additional flasks or dishes to facilitate recovery and expansion. Pause point hiPSC-derived myogenic progenitors can be expanded onto standard uncoated tissue culture plastic grade surfaces by using myoblast medium (Myocea) and cryopreserved by using HIDEM freezing medium 37 , 38 . (C) Motor neurons: neurosphere-based method Timing 4–6 d (i) Thaw neurospheres (see Reagent setup) at 37 °C for ≤2 min. Transfer to a 15-ml conical tube containing prewarmed CDM and spin at 250 g for 2 min. (ii) Discard the supernatant, resuspend the cell pellet in 10–12 ml of fresh neurosphere proliferation medium, seed the cells in a 10-cm dish and incubate at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 3–5% O 2 . (iii) After 24 h, replace neurosphere proliferation medium. Continue culturing neurospheres by changing the proliferation medium every other day. This cell population can then be used in bi- or tetra-lineage muscles as detailed in Step 2, C and F. (D) Motor neurons: NPCs (monolayer) Timing 2–6 d (i) Coat a 3.5-cm dish with 1% (vol/vol) GFR Matrigel or Geltrex at 37 °C for 30 or 60 min, respectively. (ii) Thaw NPCs (see Reagent setup) at 37 °C for ≤2 min. Transfer the cells to a 15-ml Falcon tube containing NPC proliferation medium with ROCKi (1:300) and spin at 300 g for 5 min. (iii) Discard the supernatant, gently resuspend the cell pellet in fresh NPC proliferation medium with 1:300 ROCKi and transfer the cells to the coated wells. Seed ≥2 × 10 5 NPCs/cm 2 and incubate at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 3–5% O 2 . (iv) After 24 h, change NPC proliferation medium. Continue culturing NPCs by changing NPC proliferation medium every other day. This cell population can then be used in bi- or tetra-lineage muscles as detailed in Step 2, D and G. (E) Vascular ECs and PCs Timing 4–6 d (i) Follow the Orlova et al. protocol 39 up to Step 15 and then incubate with Trypsin-EDTA for 4 min at 37 °C for detaching the ECs. (ii) Neutralize by resuspending in 20–30 ml of FACS buffer. (iii) Hydrate the 40-μm-pore-size cell strainer with 2–3 ml of FACS buffer and immediately pass the cells through it. (iv) Count the cells and keep aside 2 × 10 5 cells resuspended in FACS buffer on ice for the unstained fraction to be used in calibrating the cell sorter. (v) Spin down the rest of the cells at 300 g for 5 min. Discard the supernatant and incubate the cell pellet with CD31 (mouse anti-human CD31 antibody, TP1/15-FITC) 1:150 for 1 h at 4 °C. (vi) Resuspend the cells in 10 ml of FACS sorting buffer, filter through a cell strainer and collect the cell suspension in a 15-ml tube. (vii) Spin the cells at 300 g for 5 min, discard the supernatant and collect the cell pellet for FACS. (viii) Prepare one tube with 2 ml of EGM-2 medium to collect the CD31 − fraction (PCs) and one tube with 2 ml of EC-SFM proliferation medium to collect the CD31 + fraction (ECs). (ix) Prepare 0.1% (wt/vol) gelatin-coated flasks (1 ml per well of a six-well plate) to plate the ECs and PCs and once the coating is complete, seed the sorted cells into the flasks in their respective medium. These cell populations can be used for bi-lineage, tri-lineage or tetra-lineage constructs; only the latter two are reported in this protocol and detailed in Step 2, E, F and G. (x) Lentiviral transduction of PCs . To lentivirally transduce PCs with a PGK-GFP transgene, plate 1 × 10 5 PCs in a 35-mm dish in EGM-2 medium. (xi) When the cells are fully attached to the culture dish, thaw the PGK-GFP lentiviral vector on ice and calculate the volume necessary to transduce cells with a multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 1, 5 and 10. The following formula can be used for this purpose: microliters of virus needed = number of cells to infect × desired MOI/titer of virus (in milliliters) × 1,000. (xii) Dilute the PGK-GFP lentiviral vector in 1 ml of EGM-2 medium supplemented with 1 μl of polybrene. (xiii) Remove the medium from the 35-mm dish of PGK-GFP PCs and replace it with the 1 ml of viral suspension. Incubate the cells for 12 h at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 3–5% O 2 . (xiv) Remove the medium and wash the cells twice with PBS. Subsequently, add fresh EGM-2 medium and incubate the cells at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 3–5% O 2 . This cell population can be used to generate bi-lineage, tri-lineage and tetra-lineage constructs; however, in the protocol, we outline the procedure to obtain the latter two (Step 2, E, F and G). Making hiPSC-derived artificial skeletal muscles Timing 9–26 d 2 Here, we provide procedures for producing artificial/bioengineered muscles. For single-lineage artificial muscle by a transgene-based method, follow option A. For single-lineage artificial muscle by a transgene-free method, follow option B. For bi-lineage artificial muscle containing skeletal myofibers and motor neurons generated by using neurospheres deposited on the outer layer of the construct, follow option C. For bi-lineage artificial muscle containing skeletal myofibers and motor neurons generated by using NPCs mixed with myogenic cells, follow option D. For tri-lineage artificial muscle containing myofibers and blood vascular ECs and PCs, follow option E. For tetra-lineage artificial muscle containing myofibers, motor neurons and vascular cells by using neurospheres, follow option F. For tetra-lineage artificial muscle containing myofibers, motor neurons and vascular cells by using NPCs, follow option G. (A) Single-lineage artificial muscle by a transgene-based method Timing 9–11 d Critical step At least 24 h before beginning the protocol, ensure that the Teflon spacers and PDMS post racks are cleaned and autoclaved and that an adequate amount of 2% (wt/vol) agarose, thrombin, fibrinogen, Matrigel and aprotinin aliquots are available (Supplementary Table 1 , Reagent setup and Equipment setup). (i) Expand HIDEMs (from Step 1A(iii)) to 80% confluence before making hydrogels. Because 10 6 cells per hydrogel are required, we suggest expanding HIDEMs in T175 flasks until the area is fully covered. (ii) After expansion, discard the medium and replace with fresh HIDEM proliferation medium supplemented with ROCKi (1:300). Incubate for 2 h at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 . (iii) Place thrombin and Matrigel aliquots on ice to thaw. Fibrinogen aliquots can be kept at RT. Label one 1.5-ml Eppendorf tube per condition or cell line and place on ice. (iv) Prepare an appropriate amount of HIDEM proliferation medium prepared with heat-inactivated FBS (1%) and resuspend the cells to be embedded in hydrogels. The volume needed depends on the number of hydrogels. For a total of 20 constructs, we suggest preparing 5 ml. (v) After 2 h of incubation with HIDEM proliferation medium and 1:300 ROCKi, discard the medium, wash once with PBS and cover the cells with trypsin-EDTA. Incubate for ≤5 min to detach the cells. (vi) Neutralize trypsin-EDTA by using HIDEM proliferation medium (in a ratio of 4 ml of medium per 1 ml of trypsin) and count the cells. (vii) Centrifuge the required number (1 × 10 6 HIDEMs per hydrogel) of cells at 300 g for 5 min. Discard the supernatant and keep the cell pellet on ice. (viii) Add 1.5 ml of agarose to each well of a 24-well plate that will house a 3D artificial muscle, place the spacers into the wells and allow to cool. Critical step Ensure that the spacers are placed correctly at the middle of the wells, to avoid spillage of cells and biomaterial mixture at a later stage. (ix) Add a minimal volume of HIDEM proliferation medium prepared with heat-inactivated FBS to resuspend the cell pellet. We suggest adding 50–100 µl of medium depending on the number of hydrogels (see Supplementary Table 1 for details). (x) Aspirate the total cell suspension by using a P200 pipette, transfer the contents to a 1.5- or 2-ml tube or vial and note the cell suspension volume. If a high number of hydrogels are generated, then this step can be repeated with a larger volume of cell suspension from the tube, but make a note of the volume of cell suspension transferred to the Eppendorf tube. Critical step Be accurate when assessing the volume and ensure that there are no bubbles in the tip when aspirating the cell suspension. (xi) Add Matrigel to the cell suspension, mix gently by pipetting four to five times and note the added Matrigel volume (see Supplementary Table 1 for details). (xii) For each condition or cell line, calculate the final master mix volume that will be in the Eppendorf tube (see Supplementary Table 1 for details). Critical step The final master mix should consist of the number of 3D muscle constructs needed plus one extra construct. Assume that the volume of one construct will be lost. (xiii) Sum the volume of cell suspension measured in Step 2A(x), the volume of Matrigel measured in Step 2A(xi) and the volumes of fibrinogen and thrombin to be added later (as determined in Supplementary Table 1 ). Subtract this value from the total volume of the master mix and add this volume of HIDEM proliferation medium with inactivated serum to the Eppendorf tube. (xiv) Take the required volume of fibrinogen by using a P200 pipette (see Supplementary Table 1 for details), quickly dispense it and immediately resuspend the cell mixture with a P1000 pipette by pipetting up and down ≥10 times to avoid fibrinogen forming clumps. Critical step Thaw fibrinogen at 37 °C for ≥3 min. This will ensure that the fibrinogen is less viscous for more accurate pipetting. Critical step Prepare both P200 and P1000 pipettes with tips attached before Step 2A(xiv), to be as quick as possible to prevent fibrinogen clumps forming, leading to defective constructs. Critical step When releasing the fibrinogen into the Eppendorf tube, do not touch the cell suspension with the pipette tip; otherwise, a clump could form, blocking the tip. Troubleshooting (xv) Remove the spacers from agarose and place the PDMS post rack into the molds. Critical step Make sure that both PDMS posts are placed at the center of the molds before proceeding with the next steps. (xvi) Gently mix the content of the Eppendorf tube by using a pipette. For each hydrogel, take 113 µl of the mixture and add to a single thrombin aliquot (7.2 µl). Quickly mix by pipetting up and down one or two times and transfer the mixture into the agarose well between the PDMS posts. Critical step Make sure that the mixture is quickly mixed with thrombin for a maximum of two times to prevent formation of fibrin in the tube or tip. Make sure that no bubbles are formed. Critical step Do not push the pipette fully, because bubbles might be released into the mixture, which might result in potential premature snapping of the muscle constructs. (xvii) Cover the plate and place inside the incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 for 2 h. (xviii) Meanwhile, prepare HIDEM proliferation medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000) to prevent fibrin degradation. Add 1.5 ml per well of a new 24-well plate. (xix) Add 1 ml of DMEM per well on top of the hydrogels and incubate at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 for 10 min. This facilitates dislodging of the hydrogels from the 2% (wt/vol) agarose molds. (xx) Transfer the PDMS post racks with hydrogels to the 24-well plate containing the prewarmed medium prepared in Step 2A(xviii). Incubate the constructs at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 for 48 h. (xxi) Change the medium with HIDEM proliferation medium supplemented with 1 µM 4-OH tamoxifen and aprotinin (1:1,000) (first tamoxifen pulse). Incubate the constructs at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 for 24 h. (xxii) Change the medium with HIDEM differentiation medium supplemented with 1 µM 4-OH tamoxifen and aprotinin (1:1,000) (second tamoxifen pulse). Incubate the constructs at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 . (xxiii) Keep the hydrogels in culture until day 9 and change the medium every other day with HIDEM differentiation medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000). Troubleshooting (B) Single-lineage artificial muscle by transgene-free method Timing 9–11 d Critical At least 24 h before starting the protocol, make sure that the Teflon spacers and PDMS post racks are cleaned and autoclaved and that an adequate amount of 2% (wt/vol) agarose, thrombin, fibrinogen, Matrigel and aprotinin aliquots are available (see the Calculation Sheet in Supplementary Information, Reagent setup and Equipment setup). (i) Expand the hiPSC-derived myogenic progenitors (from Step 1B(iii)) to 75% confluence before making hydrogels. Because 10 6 cells per hydrogel are required, we suggest expanding the myogenic progenitors in T175 flasks until they reach ~80% confluence. (ii) After expansion, discard the medium. Add fresh medium containing ROCKi at a dilution of 1:300 to the hiPSC-derived myogenic progenitors and incubate for 2 h at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 . (iii) Place thrombin and Matrigel aliquots on ice to thaw. Fibrinogen aliquots can be kept at RT. Label one 1.5-ml Eppendorf tube per condition or cell line and place them on ice. (iv) Prepare an appropriate amount of heat-inactivated myoblast medium (Myocea) to resuspend the cells to be embedded in hydrogels at a later stage. Inactivation can be achieved by incubating myoblast medium (Myocea) in a 1.5-ml Eppendorf tube at 56 °C for 30 min. The volume needed depends on the number of hydrogels to be made and can be calculated on the basis of Supplementary Table 1 . Critical step Becauses myoblast medium (Myocea) contains horse serum, it needs to be inactivated to prevent possible interference with polymerizing factors, which would lead to an impaired hydrogel structure. (v) Detach cells by adding 2 ml of trypsin in each T175 flask and incubating at 37 °C with 5% CO2 and 3–5% O2 for ≤5 min. (vi) Neutralize the trypsin-EDTA with DMEM resuspension medium (in a ratio of 4 ml of medium per 1 ml of trypsin) and count the cells. (vii) Follow Step 2A(vii–xvii) to generate the transgene-free artificial muscles. (viii) Prepare myoblast medium (Myocea) and aprotinin (1:1,000). Add 1.5 ml per well of a new 24-well plate. (ix) To help dislodge the hydrogels, follow Step 2A(xix). Then, transfer the PDMS post racks with hydrogels to the 24-well plate containing prewarmed medium prepared in Step 2B(viii) and incubate at 37 °C with 5% CO2 and 18% O2 for 48 h. (x) After 2 d, switch to Myocea fusion medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000) to begin terminal differentiation. Alternatively, to generate constructs containing also a pool of PAX7+ cells, use early-passage hiPSC-derived myogenic progenitors (cells at day 2 of the Myocea myoblast stage) and transfer hydrogels to prewarmed myoblast medium (Myocea) supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000). (xi) Keep the hydrogels in culture for ≥7 d, changing the medium every other day with Myocea fusion medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000). Alternatively, to generate constructs containing also some PAX7+ cells: Change myoblast medium (Myocea) with aprotinin (1:1,000) every other day for the subsequent 5 d. On day 6, switch to Myocea fusion medium and aprotinin (1:1,000) to begin terminal differentiation. (xii) Keep the hydrogels in culture until day 14, adding fresh Myocea fusion medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000) every other day. Troubleshooting (C) hiPSC-derived bi-lineage muscle containing skeletal myofibers and motor neurons by using neurospheres Timing 16 d (i) Generate the desired number of single-lineage artificial muscles as described in Step 2A(i–xx). (ii) For each hydrogel, prepare a mixture of 113 μl of MegaCell DMEM and 14 μl of thrombin. Keep on ice. (iii) Hold the PDMS post in your hand and deposit a 5 μl drop of fibrinogen on the artificial muscle. Critical step Warm up fibrinogen by hand or in a bead bath for 3 min to reduce its viscosity to pipette more accurately. (iv) Collect three to six neurospheres as described in Step 1C(iii) by using a 20-μl pipette and add them into the fibrinogen drop. Critical step When collecting the neurospheres, aspirate as little neurosphere proliferation medium as possible to avoid negative interactions with fibrinogen. (v) Immediately add 7 μl of the MegaCell DMEM and thrombin solution (from Step 2C(ii)) on top of the neurospheres and fibrinogen mixture. Wait 3 min to allow formation of fibrin. (vi) Transfer the hydrogels to a 24-well plate containing 1.5 ml of prewarmed neurosphere bi-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with 1 μM 4-OH tamoxifen, 5 μg/ml heparin and aprotinin (1:1,000) per well (first tamoxifen pulse) and place in the incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 . (vii) After 24 h, perform a full medium change with fresh neurosphere bi-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with 1 μM 4-OH tamoxifen and aprotinin (1:1,000) (second tamoxifen pulse). (viii) Change the neurosphere bi-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000) every other day until day 6. (ix) From day 6, change the medium with neurosphere bi-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with 1 μM PM and aprotinin (1:1,000) every other day until day 14. Troubleshooting (D) Bi-lineage artificial muscle by using NPCs (as single cells, not in neurospheres) Timing 16 d (i) Thaw and culture the HIDEMs and hiPSC-derived NPCs as described in Step 1A(i–iii) and 1D(i–iv), respectively, until they reach 90–100% confluence. (ii) Prepare reagents for making the bi-lineage complex artificial muscles, as described in Step 2A(iii and iv). (iii) Pretreat both NPCs and HIDEMs with respective media containing ROCKi in them (1:300; as described in Step 2A(ii)) for 2 h before making the bi-lineage complex muscle constructs. (iv) Collect 7 × 10 5 HIDEMs by following Step 2A(v–vii). (v) Incubate NPCs with 0.5 mM EDTA (diluted in sterile water) at 37 °C for 5 min. Then, remove EDTA solution and add 1 ml of N2B27 medium per dish. (vi) Detach the cells from the bottom of the dish by gently pipetting the N2B27 medium up and down. When the cells have detached, collect them in a 15-ml Falcon tube and count the NPCs. (vii) Collect 3 × 10 5 NPCs. Prepare a mix of the two cell types in a ratio of 70% HIDEMs and 30% NPCs and spin the cell mixture at 300 g for 5 min. (viii) Discard the supernatant and add a minimal volume of NPC bi-lineage proliferation medium containing heat-inactivated FBS to resuspend the cell mixture. We suggest adding 50–100 µl of heat-inactivated NPC bi-lineage proliferation medium depending on the number of hydrogels (see Supplementary Table 1 for details). (ix) Note the cell mixture volume, then aspirate the contents by using a P200 pipette and transfer to the respective Eppendorf tubes. (x) Follow Step 2A(xi–xvii) to generate the bi-lineage constructs by using NPC bi-lineage proliferation medium with heat-inactivated FBS. (xi) Transfer the posts with the hydrogels into 24-well plates already containing prewarmed NPC bi-lineage proliferation medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000) and incubate the hydrogels at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 for 48 h. (xii) Change the medium with NPC bi-lineage proliferation medium supplemented with 1 µM 4-OH tamoxifen, 0.1 µM y-secretase inhibitor and aprotinin (1:1,000) (first tamoxifen pulse). Incubate the hydrogels at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 for 24 h. (xiii) Change the medium with NPC bi-lineage differentiation medium with 1 µM 4-OH tamoxifen, 0.1 µM y-secretase inhibitor and aprotinin (1:1,000) (second tamoxifen pulse). Incubate the constructs at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 . (xiv) Keep the hydrogels in culture, performing daily changes of NPC bi-lineage differentiation medium containing 0.1 µM y-secretase inhibitor and aprotinin (1:1,000). (xv) On day 5, change the medium by using NPC bi-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with 0.1 nM agrin and aprotinin (1:1,000). Repeat on days 6 and 7, by using NPC bi-lineage differentiation medium with 0.5 nM and 1 nM agrin, respectively. (xvi) From day 8, change the medium with NPC bi-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000) and 1 nM agrin daily until day 14 (or beyond this date for longer cultures). Troubleshooting (E) hiPSC-derived tri-lineage artificial muscle containing myofibers and blood vascular ECs and PCs Timing 19 d (i) Culture HIDEMs (Step 1A(i–iii)), ECs (Step 1E(ix)) and PCs (Step 1E(xiv)) in T175 flasks containing HIDEM proliferation, EC-SFM and EGM-2 media, respectively, until they reach the desired confluence (see Step 1). (ii) Prepare reagents for making the tri-lineage complex artificial muscles, as described in Step 2A(iii and iv). (iii) Pretreat ECs, PCs and HIDEMs with ROCKi (1:300) for 2 h before making the tri-lineage complex muscle constructs (as described in Step 2A(ii)) by using the respective proliferation media. (iv) Obtain the required cell numbers of HIDEMs, ECs and PCs as described in Step 2A(v–vii) by using their respective culture media for each cell type for inactivation of trypsin. Prepare a mix of 70% HIDEMs (7 × 10 5 ) and 30% vascular cells (6% ECs and 24% PCs (6 × 10 4 ECs and 2.4 × 10 5 PCs)) per hydrogel. (v) Spin cells at 300 g for 5 min, discard the supernatant and resuspend in an appropriate volume of tri-lineage proliferation medium with heat-inactivated FBS. (vi) Follow Step 2A(xi–xvii) to generate the tri-lineage constructs by using tri-lineage proliferation medium with heat-inactivated FBS. (vii) Transfer the PDMS post rack with hydrogels into prewarmed tri-lineage proliferation medium containing aprotinin (1:1,000) and incubate the hydrogels at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 for 48 h. (viii) Change the medium with tri-lineage proliferation medium supplemented with 1 μM 4-OH tamoxifen and aprotinin (1:1,000) (first tamoxifen pulse). (ix) After 24 h, change the medium with tri-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with 1 μM 4-OH tamoxifen and aprotinin (1:1,000) (second tamoxifen pulse). (x) Keep the hydrogels in culture until day 17, performing a full medium change with tri-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000) every other day. Troubleshooting (F) Tetra-lineage artificial muscle containing myofibers, motor neurons and vascular cells by using neurospheres Timing 26 d (i) Generate a tri-lineage construct by following Step 2E(i–vi). (ii) After 1 h of polymerization, pipette three to six neurospheres (as described in Step 3C(iii and iv)) on top of each hydrogel by using 10 μl of fibrinogen. (iii) Transfer the PDMS post racks with hydrogels into prewarmed neurosphere tetra-lineage proliferation medium containing aprotinin (1:1,000) and keep at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 for 48 h. (iv) Change the medium with neurosphere tetra-lineage proliferation medium supplemented with 1 μM 4-OH tamoxifen and aprotinin (1:1,000) (first tamoxifen pulse). (v) After 24 h, change the medium with neurosphere tetra-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with 1 μM 4-OH tamoxifen and aprotinin (1:1,000) (second tamoxifen pulse). (vi) Perform medium changes every 2 d with neurosphere tetra-lineage differentiation medium and aprotinin (1:1,000) until day 7. (vii) From day 8, perform full medium changes with fresh neurosphere tetra-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with 1 μM PM and aprotinin (1:1,000) every other day. Troubleshooting (G) Tetra-lineage artificial muscle containing myofibers, vascular cells and motor neurons by using NPCs Timing 26 d (i) Culture HIDEMs (Step 1A(i–iii)), ECs (Step 1E(ix)) and PCs (Step 1E(xiv)) in T175 flasks containing HIDEM proliferation, EC-SFM and EGM-2 media, respectively, until they reach the appropriate confluence. (ii) Culture NPCs as described in Step 1D(i–iv) until the full confluence of at least one six-well plate is reached. (iii) Prepare reagents for making the tetra-lineage artificial muscles, as described in Step 2A(i–iii). (iv) Pretreat ECs, PCs, HIDEMs and NPCs with ROCKi (1:300) for 2 h before making the tetra-lineage constructs (as described in Step 2A(ii)) by using the respective proliferation media. (v) Obtain the required numbers of HIDEMs, ECs and PCs by following Step 2A(v–vii). Make sure to use the appropriate medium for each cell type for inactivation of trypsin. (vi) Count the number of NPCs. (vii) Mix the four cell types in the required ratio and spin the cell mixture at 300 g for 5 min. A mix of 70% myogenic cells (7 × 10 5 ), 15% vascular cells (10% ECs and 5% PCs (1 × 10 5 ECs and 5 × 10 4 PCs)) and 15% NPCs (1.5 × 10 5 ) is required per hydrogel. (viii) Discard the supernatant and resuspend the cells in a minimal volume of NPC tetra-lineage proliferation medium containing heat-inactivated FBS. We suggest adding 50–100 µl of heat-inactivated NPC tetra-proliferation medium depending on the number of hydrogels (see the Calculation Sheet in Supplementary Information for details). (ix) Follow Step 2A(xi–xvii) to generate hydrogels by using NPC tetra-lineage proliferation medium with heat-inactivated FBS. (x) Transfer the PDMS post racks with hydrogels into prewarmed NPC tetra-lineage proliferation medium containing aprotinin (1:1,000) 2 h after embedding the cells into fibrin hydrogels and incubate at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 and 18% O 2 for 48 h. (xi) Change the medium with NPC tetra-proliferation medium supplemented with 1 μM 4-OH tamoxifen and aprotinin (1:1,000) (first tamoxifen pulse). (xii) After 24 h, change the medium with NPC tetra-lineage differentiation medium supplemented with 1 μM 4-OH tamoxifen and aprotinin (1:1,000). (xiii) Keep the gels in culture until day 24 and replace NPC tetra-differentiation medium supplemented with aprotinin (1:1,000) every other day. Troubleshooting 3 Upon completion of differentiation, proceed to functional assays. For essential (Tier 1) characterization, fix the gels in 4% (wt/vol) PFA at 4 °C for 8 h, continue with Steps 4–9 to perform immunofluorescence, followed by Steps 10–19 for regular confocal microscopy. Alternatively, Tier 2 or 3 functional analyses can be performed: For light-sheet imaging, fix and proceed with immunofluorescence (Steps 4–9) as for regular confocal imaging and then proceed to Box 1 . For mRNA expression analysis and western blotting, proceed to Box 2 . For electrical stimulation contraction and for calcium transients, proceed to Box 3 . For constructs prepared with hiPSCs bearing mutations to be used for disease modeling experiments, fix the gels in 4% (wt/vol) PFA at 4 °C for 3 h, continue with Steps 4–10 to perform immunofluorescence (suggestions for markers are detailed in Experimental design), followed by Steps 11–20 for regular confocal microscopy. Then, proceed to Box 4 for quantification of nuclear shape. Morphological characterization of 3D artificial muscle Immunofluorescence Timing 2 d Critical Immunohistochemistry can also be performed on sectioned constructs to further characterize and image tissue architecture. For further details, see ref. 31 . 4 Remove the fixed hydrogels from the PDMS posts by using tweezers and transfer to a conical tube containing the blocking buffer. Pause point Artificial muscles can also be stored in PBS at 4 °C for ≤1 week. Critical step Carefully run the tweezers along one post and remove one end of the construct. Follow the same procedure for the other end of the construct. Do not touch the central portion of the hydrogels with tweezers, because this could damage the construct. 5 Incubate fixed artificial muscles in blocking buffer for 6 h at 4 °C to block nonspecific binding. Troubleshooting 6 Remove the blocking buffer and incubate with the primary antibody or antibodies in permeabilization buffer at 4 °C overnight. 7 Remove the primary antibody and wash the samples six times for 60 min each in TBS 1× at RT on a plate shaker. 8 Incubate the samples with the secondary antibody or antibodies and Hoechst in permeabilization buffer at 4 °C overnight on a plate shaker. 9 Remove the secondary antibody and wash samples six times for 60 min each in TBS 1× at RT on a plate shaker. Critical step Antibodies conjugated with fluorophores are light sensitive; thus, Step 9 and subsequent steps must be performed while protecting the samples from light (e.g., by covering the samples with aluminum foil). Confocal microscopy Timing 1–5 h 10 For confocal imaging, mount the immunolabeled hydrogels on microscope glass slides with a concave depression by using two or three drops of fluorescence mounting medium. 11 Gently place a coverslip on top of the sample and let it dry for 20 min. Critical step Make sure that the constructs are positioned in the middle of the concave depression and are covered with fluorescence mounting medium, while avoiding bubbles. Pause point For long-term storage of 3D muscle on slides, we recommend sealing the coverslip to the glass slide by applying nail polish to the sides of the coverslip and leaving to dry for 20 min at RT. The sealed slides can be stored at 4 °C and should be used for imaging within 3–6 months. 12 Place the slide onto the stage of the confocal microscope and use the following settings: 63× objective; scanning parameters: 1,024 × 1,024, speed of 600, zoom factor 1.0, frame average of 3 and z-stack spacing of 1.5 µm. For 3D reconstruction of nuclei, use the same settings but with a zoom factor of 1.5 and z-stack spacing of 0.5 µm. 13 Because the 63× objective is an immersion objective, dispense two drops of immersion oil on top of the coverslip before imaging. 14 Lower the objective until it contacts the immersion oil. Use the ocular to further lower the objective until the sample is in focus. 15 Select the field to image and set the start and end of the Z position, moving the objective up and down across the sample. This will represent the portion of artificial muscle over which the z-stack will be collected. We advise choosing the start and end of the Z position on the basis of the fluorescent channel of major interest (e.g., lamin A/C to reconstruct myonuclei). 16 For each fluorescent channel, use the LUT tool to adjust the gain and the offset to improve signal intensity and background noise, respectively. 17 Initiate imaging. Upon completion of this process, use the Intensity Max Projection tool to combine stacks to generate a single image to be exported as a TIFF file. For 3D nuclear reconstruction, export the files in LIF format and proceed to Box 4 . Troubleshooting Troubleshooting advice can be found in Table 1 . Table 1 Troubleshooting table Full size table Timing Step 1A(i–iii), culturing of transgene-derived skeletal myogenic cells: 2–3 d Step 1B(i–iii), culturing of small molecule–derived skeletal myogenic cells: 2–3 d Step 1C(i–iii), culturing of neurospheres: 4–6 d Step 1D(i–iv), culturing of monolayer motor neurons: 2–6 d Step 1E(i–xiv), derivation of ECs and PCs: 4–6 d Step 2A(i–xxiii), making single-lineage artificial muscle by a transgene-based method: 9–11 d Step 2B(i–xii), making single-lineage artificial muscle by a transgene-free method: 9–11 d Step 2C(i–ix), making bi-lineage artificial muscle with neurospheres: 16 d Step 2D(i–xvi), making bi-lineage artificial muscle with NPCs: 16 d Step 2E(i–x), making tri-lineage artificial skeletal muscle: 19 d Step 2F(i–vii), making tetra-lineage artificial skeletal muscle with neuroshperes: 26 d Step 2G(i–xiii), making tetra-lineage artificial skeletal muscle with NPCs: 26 d Steps 4–9, immunofluorescence characterization of 3D artificial muscle: 2 d Steps 10–17, confocal imaging of 3D artificial muscle: 1–5 h Box 1, steps 1–15, light-sheet imaging of 3D artificial muscle: 5–18 h Box 2A(i–xi), gene expression analysis of 3D artificial muscle: 8–18 h Box 2B(i–xiii), western blotting analysis of protein production in 3D artificial muscle: 2–3 h Box 3A(i–xviii), contractility assay: 15 min Box 3B(i–ix), whole-construct calcium transients: 2–5 h Box 3C(i–xii), single-cell calcium transients: 2–5 h Box 4, steps 1–11, modeling laminopathy-related abnormalities: 3–15 h (depending on the number of samples to be analyzed) Anticipated results In this protocol, we detail the modular generation of human 3D artificial muscle constructs fully derived from pluripotent stem cells. We focused on hiPSCs as the preferential source of cells, given the limited ethical constraints and noninvasiveness of the original cell population sampling, although similar results could be obtained by using human embryonic stem cells 31 or more conventional cell sources such as human primary or immortalized skeletal myoblasts 11 , 31 . We provide a stepwise guide to generate an array of 3D bioengineered muscles with incremental multicellular and multi-lineage complexity: from bundles of myofibers generated by hiPSC-derived myogenic progenitors (single-lineage muscles) to complex isogenic neuromuscular constructs containing vascular networks derived by using hiPSC-derived myogenic, neural and vascular progenitors (bi-, tri- and tetra-lineage muscles). Moreover, the presence of PAX7 + cells (albeit less controllable than other cell types; Fig. 3c ) increases the number of simultaneous isogenic cell lineages to five, potentially enabling studies of complex cell interactions in the human muscle stem cell niche in a quasi-vivo setup. Derivation of cell lineages from hiPSCs relies on validated protocols. For skeletal myogenic cells, two different methods have been used, a transgene-based method 37 and a transgene-free method 38 , although we do not foresee major hurdles in using alternative methods 61 , 62 , 63 , 64 , 65 , 66 . We recommend using transgene-based hiPSC myogenic differentiation if the specific research question or application does not require the faithful recapitulation of myogenic specification events (e.g., studies of phenotype in myotubes rather than in muscle stem or progenitor cells). In addition, we recommend using transgene-based methods if complex multi-lineage constructs need to be developed, as cell culture requirements for nonmyogenic cells (e.g., neural or vascular lineages) might interfere with small molecule–based myogenic differentiation of hiPSCs. Once hiPSC derivatives have been characterized (details can be found in the respective protocols 37 , 38 , 39 , 40 , 41 ), 3D constructs are generated by using a platform originally developed for cardiac tissue engineering and subsequently validated by our group for skeletal muscle engineering 11 , 31 . After polymerization, the fibrin-Matrigel hydrogels appear soft and translucent (Fig. 2a ). Over the following days, changes in the hydrogel texture occur, and it becomes more opaque and thinner and shorter as the cells remodel the matrix. Constructs should be handled with care at any stage of differentiation. For maximal preservation of tissue architecture (e.g., myofibril alignment), fixing the hydrogels in situ (i.e., without removing them from the PDMS post racks) is recommended. Depending on the specific research question or application, 3D constructs can be analyzed at different time points with lineage specific markers detected by using one or multiple techniques as detailed in Fig. 3 . We recommend assessment after a minimum of 7 d in differentiating conditions to enable formation of sarcomeric structures in myofibers and organization of the neuromuscular and vascular cells. Longer time points (e.g., >12 d) are required to detect contraction upon stimulation. Contractility data can be converted into force values by using the formula and method detailed in Osaki et al. 33 . We have validated our platform by using a library of >10 healthy donor and patient-specific hiPSCs from different muscle diseases (e.g., Duchenne, limb-girdle 2D and LMNA-related muscular dystrophies). This method is also remarkably versatile in terms of applications: from tissue replacement 31 (Fig. 5c–h ) to disease modeling (Fig. 4 ) and therapy screening (Fig. 5 ). Using this platform, we successfully modeled the dysmorphic myonuclear phenotype of skeletal muscle laminopathies, unraveling novel objective readouts (i.e., nuclear elongation) previously undetected in conventional monolayer cultures and currently being used to screen potential drug candidates in our laboratory. Regardless of the genotype, rendering of confocal images will clearly show that nuclei in artificial muscles appear 3D compared to flattened nuclear morphology seen in monolayer cultures. Artificial muscles can also be used to screen advanced therapies such as gene therapy vectors and cell therapies 54 . Here, we also provide proof-of-principle evidence of their use to monitor expression of a nonviral vector introduced in myogenic cells before their combination with biomaterials in 3D (i.e., hiPSC-derived myogenic cells containing a human artificial chromosome 59 ; Fig. 5a ), as well as of viral vectors directly delivered to the already assembled 3D muscles (Fig. 5b ). We focused on AAVs as they are currently the most used and promising neuromuscular gene therapy vectors (e.g., onasemnogene abeparvovec for spinal muscular atrophy), using a simple fluorescent-based readout to show expression dynamics of different vector concentrations upon transduction of muscle constructs. Production of the fluorescent transgene product can be monitored at the whole-muscle level by using fluorescent stereomicroscopy and then confirmed with higher-resolution microscopy in live cells or upon fixation of the tissue by using specific antibodies (Fig. 5b ; anti-GFP positive (green) myotubes were counter-stained with MyHC (red), and we could detect double-positive myotubes with varying degrees of GFP intensity, as seen by the presence of yellow-colored structures). Depending on the AAV serotype and MOI, transgene expression in 3D muscles can be observed as early as 3 d after transduction. Another personalized advanced therapy that can be assessed and optimized with this platform is muscle cell therapy: we provide here proof-of-principle assessment of migration of myogenic cells 53 within isogenic 3D muscles (with both derived from the same hiPSC source), providing a unique strategy to assess myogenic cell transplantation in a quasi vivo environment to optimize future skeletal muscle–directed cell therapies (Fig. 5c ) 54 . Finally, transgene expression can also be detected with similar modalities upon ex vivo explant of mouse muscles implanted with artificial muscles (Fig. 5d–h ) 31 . Of note, muscle function experiments in immunodeficient mice subjected to volumetric muscle injury suggested better integration and rescue of function by tri-lineage artificial muscles (myofibers + ECs + PCs) than more conventional single-lineage hydrogels 31 . abstract: Skeletal muscle is a complex tissue composed of multinucleated myofibers responsible for force generation that are supported by multiple cell types. Many severe and lethal disorders affect skeletal muscle; therefore, engineering models to reproduce such cellular complexity and function are instrumental for investigating muscle pathophysiology and developing therapies. Here, we detail the modular 3D bioengineering of multilineage skeletal muscles from human induced pluripotent stem cells, which are first differentiated into myogenic, neural and vascular progenitor cells and then combined within 3D hydrogels under tension to generate an aligned myofiber scaffold containing vascular networks and motor neurons. 3D bioengineered muscles recapitulate morphological and functional features of human skeletal muscle, including establishment of a pool of cells expressing muscle stem cell markers. Importantly, bioengineered muscles provide a high-fidelity platform to study muscle pathology, such as emergence of dysmorphic nuclei in muscular dystrophies caused by mutant lamins. The protocol is easy to follow for operators with cell culture experience and takes between 9 and 30 d, depending on the number of cell lineages in the construct. We also provide examples of applications of this advanced platform for testing gene and cell therapies in vitro, as well as for in vivo studies, providing proof of principle of its potential as a tool to develop next-generation neuromuscular or musculoskeletal therapies.
news_body: Head of the Crick's Stem Cells and Neuromuscular Regeneration Lab, Francesco Saverio Tedesco, is also a doctor at Great Ormond Street Hospital, where he specializes in neurological and neuromuscular diseases of childhood, like muscular dystrophy. In his lab, his team are working on brand new ways to generate muscle tissue, that will help researchers understand diseases where muscles deteriorate, but could also be the first steps towards future muscle regeneration therapies. The team have published their latest methods for growing skeletal muscle tissue from stem cells, in Nature Protocols, and we spoke to Saverio about their progress. What are the challenges of growing muscle tissue? We've entered into a new era of regenerative medicine. The advanced tools at our disposal today mean that it's now possible to grow incredibly complex structures in the lab. But there are still challenges. It's a time and resource-intensive process and the conditions have to be just right. How have your team approached muscle generation? We have taken a 3D bioengineering approach. We start with human induced pluripotent stem cells, which are capable of becoming any cell type in the body. We then grew these into the different cell types that make up skeletal muscle—myogenic (muscle) cells, neurons, and vascular cells for the growth of blood vessels. We combined these within 3D hydrogels under tension to create a scaffold in which the muscle would grow. The end result is a small piece of skeletal muscle tissue, about 1cm in size. And the interesting thing about this method is that we can create lab-grown muscles that are reflective of specific patients. The induced pluripotent stem cells can be grown from a patient's own skin cells and the resulting muscle structure will reflect features of the muscle disease they have. Contractions of hiPSC-derived 3D skeletal muscle constructs. Credit: The Francis Crick Institute How long does it take to grow muscle tissue? The length of time varies depending on the complexity and size of the end structure. If the muscle-making cells are readily available, the mini muscles can be produced in about two weeks. But it could take up to 3-4 months if you start from scratch, i.e. if one has to generate also the induced pluripotent stem cells. Creating and differentiating the induced pluripotent stem cells take around two months and then it takes a further month to grow them together in the hydrogel to create the tissue. It's not a quick process, but now we have a bank of frozen stem cells that we can create muscle from in just a few weeks. How are you planning to use the lab-grown muscles? Because we can grow muscle from specific patients, we can use these structures to better understand the biology of different conditions characterized by muscle deterioration. We can also use the lab-grown muscle to test potential new treatments. In our Nature Protocols paper, we showed that the structures are responsive to inactive viruses used for new gene therapies in a dose-dependent manner. We also showed that mini muscles could be used to study how stem cells behave when delivered to muscle tissue, providing a tool to optimize possible future cell transplantation strategies for muscle diseases. How could muscle generation be used in future treatment? I think a clear use for artificial muscles will be in finding and testing new treatments for diseases like muscular dystrophy. The muscles could also be grown from individual patients to help personalize their care by selecting treatments that are most likely to work based on how well the artificial muscle responds. But one day we might also be able to grow and transplant sections of muscle. In our paper we also showed that the artificial muscle can be engrafted and implanted in mice as a form of tissue replacement therapy.

**Breakthrough in Muscle Research: Scientists Create 3D Bioengineered Muscles from Stem Cells**

In a groundbreaking development, researchers have announced the successful creation of three-dimensional human skeletal muscle tissues using advanced bioengineering techniques. These lab-grown muscles are engineered from human induced pluripotent stem cells, offering a promising new model for understanding and treating severe muscle disorders.

Skeletal muscles, known for their role in movement and force generation, are more complex than they might seem. Composed of multinucleated fibers, they rely on the support of various cell types. Disorders affecting these muscles can be debilitating, making advances in their study and treatment vital. The new 3D engineered muscles can closely mimic the intricate cellular makeup and functionality of real human muscles, providing scientists with a more accurate platform for research.

To create these bioengineered muscles, scientists first differentiated stem cells into various precursors: myogenic (muscle-related), neural (nerve-related), and vascular (blood vessel-related). These elements were then combined within a three-dimensional hydrogel framework. This setup creates a scaffold that mirrors the alignment and structure of natural muscle fibers, complete with integrated vascular networks and motor neurons.

Remarkably, these lab-grown muscles exhibit several key characteristics of human skeletal muscle, including the presence of cells expressing muscle stem cell markers. This resemblance not only provides a new avenue to explore muscle function but also offers a vital platform for studying muscle diseases. For instance, researchers can now investigate conditions like muscular dystrophies, characterized by distorted muscle cell nuclei, in more detail.

The development process of these muscles is straightforward for those with experience in cell culture, taking anywhere from nine to thirty days depending on the complexity required. The researchers also demonstrated the potential applications of this technology: it can serve as a testing ground for new gene and cell therapies, both in vitro (outside a living organism) and in living organisms, paving the way for next-generation treatments for neuromuscular and musculoskeletal conditions.

This breakthrough represents a significant step forward in muscle research, with the potential to transform how scientists understand and treat muscle-related diseases. It offers hope for more effective therapies and a deeper understanding of the complexities of muscle biology.

paper_body: Abstract Membrane technology is a low-footprint and highly efficient industrial separation process. While more stable membranes could substantially contribute to modernizing the chemical industry, sustainability must be seen holistically. Polymer sources, solvents, and recycling strategies that adhere to the strategic concepts of the circular economy should be considered at the membrane design stage. Recycling plastic waste into separation membranes can help remediate the environmental impact of the current plastic pollution. Polyolefins are the most manufactured and used polymer family, and their high chemical resistance and low price are attractive for membrane preparation. However, their limited solubility in mainly non-renewable solvents at high temperatures restricts their processability and recycling. In this work, we present the use of polypropylene (PP) and low-density polyethylene (LDPE) as the source of membrane preparation by their dissolution in two bio-based and renewable solvents (α-pinene and D -limonene). The thermal properties and phase separation behavior were studied and phase diagrams were obtained. Liquid–liquid phase separation and spherulitic morphology were observed for the three studied systems. PP membranes were obtained by a thermally induced phase separation (TIPS) process employing α-pinene as a solvent, and food packaging plastic waste or commercially available PP pellets in the polymer dope solution. The obtained membranes were tested for water-in-oil emulsion separations. The influence of the polymer content and the quenching media on the morphology, mechanical and thermal properties, and water contact angle was investigated. PP membranes were fabricated with 20–30 wt% polymer contents using water at 4 °C and 20 °C as quenching media. The contact angles were higher than 150° under oil enabled efficient water-in-toluene emulsion separation, where approx. 95% water rejection and an average of 99.97% toluene purity were achieved. 1. Introduction Membrane technology plays an essential role in sustainable industrial practices due to its low environmental footprint during operation compared to conventional thermal separation methods. 1,2 In addition, membranes exhibit remarkable performance in applications such as air and water purification, biomedical devices, energy production, food processing, chemical separations, and others. 2–5 However, when focusing on the membrane fabrication stage, the overall membrane's sustainability can be further improved. 6–9 Polymeric membranes lead the market over ceramic ones due to their low cost, processability, scalability, and versatility. 4 They are mainly prepared by phase separation processes triggered by changes in the thermodynamic conditions. A homogeneous polymer casting solution separates into a polymer-lean phase (incipient pores) and a polymer-rich phase, following a spinodal decomposition or a nucleation and growth mechanism. The polymer-rich phase gels and solidifies, kinetically trapping the porous structure. 10,11 Three of the most applied techniques are thermally induced phase separation (TIPS), non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS), and vapor induced phase separation (VIPS), in which the phase separation is triggered by a change in temperature, solvent and non-solvent exchange in the liquid state, and exposure to a non-solvent in the gas state, respectively. 12,13 To date, the membrane fabrication industry uses mainly non-renewable and toxic solvents, such as N -methylpyrrolidone (NMP), N , N -dimethylacetamide (DMAc), and N , N -dimethylformamide (DMF), and fossil-based polymers. 14–16 Such solvents and polymers have been estimated to contribute to global warming, human health threats, and fossil resource scarcity. 8 In response, strategies following the principles of circular economy and green chemistry are being increasingly studied over the last few years for their implementation in membrane fabrication processes. Two examples are the utilization of greener solvents and alternative polymer sources. 6,7,16–19 Greener solvents in membrane preparation such as methyl lactate, ethyl lactate, Cyrene™, PolarClean®, natural organic carbonates, ionic liquids, vegetable oil, and others, have shown good compatibility with different polymers. 15,20–27 For example, Rasool et al. 21 fabricated asymmetric cellulose acetate (CA) nanofiltration membranes via NIPS using bio-derived methyl lactate and 2-methyl tetrahydrofuran as a solvent and co-solvent, respectively. The optimized membranes showed a Rose Bengal (1018 g mol −1 ) rejection above 99% in water, making them promising candidates for applications in the nanofiltration range. Wang et al. 15 used the synthetic non-toxic PolarClean® for CA dissolution to fabricate nanofiltration membranes and to dissolve polysulfone (PSF) and polyethersulfone (PES) for preparing ultrafiltration membranes via the NIPS process. Marino et al. 20 reported the use of Cyrene™, a bio-solvent derived from nonfood cellulosic sources, for PES and poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) membrane preparation by NIPS obtaining pore sizes around 500 nm and pure water permeance around 550 L m −2 h −1 bar −1 . Ismail et al. 28 reported the fabrication of PVDF membranes employing ethylene carbonate (EC) as a solvent during a combined NIPS and TIPS process, which exhibited an outstanding performance for water desalination by membrane distillation, with more than 99.97% salt rejection factor. These encouraging works serve as an example of the high potential of green solvents for replacing traditional non-renewable ones while maintaining a competitive performance to be applied in diverse applications. Nevertheless, the use of green solvents for the dissolution of chemically stable polymers is still challenging. This is the case for polyolefins such as polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP), commonly dissolved in fossil-derived hydrocarbons ( e.g. p -xylene, dodecane, decalin, and paraffin oils) at temperatures ranging from 140 to 250 °C. Beside their processing limitations, PP and PE significantly contribute to plastic pollution, considering that they account for nearly half of the plastics produced worldwide for their use in applications such as food packaging, bottle containers, automotive parts, toys, and houseware products (182 million metric tons produced in 2021) 29,30 and that only around 8.8% of the plastics are recycled. 31,32 A 2017 report by the Ellen Macarthur Foundation estimated that plastic packaging materials are devalued by 95% after one short use, which translates to economic losses of USD 80–120 billion annually. 33 Thus, efforts to directly transform plastic waste into value-added products, such as polymeric membranes, is a step forward toward more sustainable industrial practices, specifically in the membrane industry. 6,34,35 Several works have reported the successful utilization of plastic waste for membrane fabrication. Pulido et al. 36 fabricated polyethylene terephthalate (PET) ultrafiltration membranes from plastic waste by NIPS, showing good performance even at temperatures up to 100 °C and high resistance towards harsh solvents. Lai et al. 37 reported the use of polystyrene (PS) waste as a material source for fabricating waterproof CO 2 separation membranes through hot-pressing. Keles and Uysal 38 optimized the fabrication of low-density polyethylene (LDPE) and acrylic fiber membranes by electrospinning using shrink film packaging waste as the polymer source for wastewater treatment applications. These examples demonstrate the technical viability of using plastic waste as feedstock for membrane fabrication and the potential for environmental remediation. In this work, we contribute to sustainability in polyolefin membrane fabrication through two approaches. First, by utilizing renewable bio-based solvents for PP and LDPE dissolution, and second, by implementing the direct recycling of plastic waste as a polymer source in the membrane fabrication process. The selected bio-based solvents were α-pinene and d -limonene, belonging to the terpene family, the largest class of natural products (produced by all plants), and have a long trajectory in human healthcare, with a FDA (U.S. Food and Drug Administration) approval for their use in food and cosmetics. 39–42 They have been proposed as sustainable alternatives to fossil-derived solvents such as n -hexane for the extraction of valuable compounds, such as carotenoids, oil from seeds and microalgae, lipids, and aromas. 43–45 d -Limonene is produced from citrus fruits peels, a feedstock widely available from the juice industry waste, while α-pinene is derived from gum turpentine, an essential oil distilled from the pine gum harvested from living pine trees or obtained as a side product of the pulp mill industry (Fig. S1 † ). 46,47 Their similar Hansen Solubility Parameters (HSP), compared to the petroleum-based p -xylene used in polyolefin dissolution, offers a great potential for their use as alternative greener solvents in PP and LDPE dissolution (Table S2 † ). Despite being renewable and widely available, their feasibility for polyolefin dissolution and membrane fabrication has not been studied to date, to the best of our knowledge. Here we proposed the use of α-pinene and d -limonene for polyolefin dissolution and membrane fabrication. The phase diagrams were obtained from the solubility and phase separation mechanism of commercially available PP and LDPE in the bio-based solvents. Then, the effect of polymer content and quenching media on the PP membrane's morphology, mechanical and thermal properties, and water contact angle were addressed. The resulting membranes were tested in water-in-oil emulsion separation as an example of the application of hydrophobic PP membranes. Commercially available PP and plastic waste from PP food packages were utilized as the polymer source for membrane fabrication, and the changes in the membrane's properties and performance were investigated and compared. This study broadens the alternatives for polyolefin (PP and LDPE) dissolution and membrane fabrication with renewable bio-based solvents while revalorizing plastic waste, which can be of benefit for the polymers and membrane fields in the transition to a circular economy following the green chemistry principles. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Materials Low-density polyethylene (0.925 g mL −1 ) and isotactic polypropylene (0.9 g mL −1 , 250 000 g mol −1 ) pellets were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Polypropylene food containers were obtained from a local supermarket and used as the plastic waste source. (1 S )-(−)-α-Pinene was purchased from Merck Germany and d -limonene from MP Biomedicals. Heptane was purchased from Fisher Scientific, and toluene and n -hexane from VWR chemicals. A Span 80 non-ionic surfactant was obtained from Fluka Analytical. All materials were used as received without further purification. PP food containers were rinsed with acetone and used without further purification. 2.2. Membrane preparation Flat-sheet membranes were prepared via TIPS, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Commercially available PP pellets were used for the polymer dope solution at 15, 20, and 25 wt% content in α-pinene. The solutions were stirred at 130 °C for three hours in a silicon oil bath to ensure complete polymer dissolution. Then, the stirring was stopped for three hours for de-bubbling while maintaining the temperature at 130 °C. The obtained solution was cast with a casting knife with a 250 μm gap on a stainless-steel plate, which was at the same temperature as the dope solution. The ambient relative humidity and temperature were 70% and 20 °C. The film was then cooled by keeping the plate in air, or immersing the plate in water at 20 °C or 4 °C for five minutes to induce the phase separation. After the membrane was formed, the remaining solvent was removed by immersing the membrane in ethanol for 24 h with a change of ethanol 12 h after the initial immersion. Finally, the obtained membranes were dried at room temperature for at least 24 h before filtration tests. The same procedure was applied for membranes prepared using PP waste from food containers. Fig. 1 Schematic of polymeric membrane preparation via TIPS: preparation of the dope solution containing the polymer and the solvent, casting of the solution on a stainless steel plate, phase separation of the membrane by quenching media at a temperature below the crystallization temperature, and solvent extraction in ethanol. 2.3. Polymer solubility and phase diagrams The bio-based solvents used in this work were initially identified by screening solvents with Hansen Solubility Parameters (HSP) similar to those of the fossil-based p -xylene (traditional solvent for polyolefin dissolution), utilizing the HSPiP software (Table S3 † ). According to Hansen, 48 the affinity of solvent 1 and polymer 2 can be quantified by calculating the solubility parameter distance ( R a ) based on eqn (1) : R a 2 = 4( δ d2 − δ d1 ) 2 + ( δ p2 − δ p1 ) 2 + ( δ hb2 − δ hb1 ) 2 (1) where δ d , δ p , and δ hb are the dispersion, polar, and hydrogen bonding solubility parameters, respectively. The distance R a was calculated in comparison with PP and LDPE, and the position of the solvents in relation to the polymers was plotted in the HSP space considering an interaction radius of 5 MPa 1/2 according to Van Krevelen and Te Nijenhuis 49 (Fig. S1 † ). The solubility of LDPE and PP in the selected solvent was evaluated by gradually adding commercially available polymer pellets to the solvent at a temperature of 130 °C, and the dissolution limit was set to the concentration beyond which turbidity was observed in the system. The phase diagrams were obtained by two methods: (i) observations of the dope solutions with different polymer contents on a Leica DM4500 P optical microscope and (ii) DSC measurements on a DSC 250 – TA instruments. The solutions were prepared in the same way for both methods. For optical microscopy observations, a drop of the polyolefin dope solution at 130 °C was placed between two glass slips with a 0.1 mm thickness at the same temperature and rapidly transferred into a Linkam Hot Stage, pre-heated at 140 °C. The temperature was maintained for five minutes and then decreased to room temperature (20 °C) at a 10 °C min −1 rate. The phase separation was observed during the cooling process under standard and polarized light. The cloud point (CP) was taken as the temperature at which the nucleus formation was observed with a 10× amplification lens. DSC measurements were obtained by quenching the polyolefin dope solution with a specific composition in liquid nitrogen. A small piece of the solidified sample was then placed in a Tzero hermetic pan, heated to 140 °C and maintained for 20 min, cooled down to room temperature, kept for 10 min, and reheated to 140 °C to remove the thermal history of the polymer. Cooling–heating rates were 10 °C min −1 in all cases, and the exothermic peak temperature was taken as the crystallization temperature ( T c ). 2.4. Characterization methods The infrared spectra were obtained on a Nicolet iS10 instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific) from 600 to 3800 cm −1 with 32 scans. The molecular weight (MW) of the polymers was determined using an Agilent 1260 Infinity II High-Temperature Gel Permeation Chromatography (HT-GPC) Multi-Detector System equipment using 1,2,4-trichlorobenzene as a mobile phase. Thermal analysis was performed on a TGA Q50 instrument under an air atmosphere at a heating rate of 20 °C min −1 from 25 to 800 °C. DSC 250 and Tzero hermetic pans (TA instruments) were used for obtaining the crystallization and melting temperatures of polymers and PP membranes. In all cases, a heat–cool–heat cycle with an upper temperature of 230 °C was performed with a cooling–heating rate of 10 °C min −1 . SEM images were obtained on a Zeiss Merlin Electron Microscope. Samples for cross-section observations were prepared by breaking a piece of membrane in liquid nitrogen and placing it between two layers of conductive carbon tape. Silver paint was used on the borders of the membrane pieces to make contact between the carbon tape and the membrane. All samples were coated with a 3 nm iridium layer in a Quorum Q300RT sputter coater. Water contact angles were measured on an FM40 Easy Drop instrument (KRÜSS). 2 μl water drop was utilized for air and under-oil measurements. All reported values are the average from three measurements. Young's modulus, fracture strain, tensile strength, and toughness were obtained from strain vs. stress measurements on a Discovery DMA850 instrument. The stress ramp was set from 0.05 N to 18.00 N at a rate of 1.5 N min −1 . Young's modulus was calculated as the slope of the linear section of the strain vs. stress curve; fracture strain was taken as the maximum strain value before the sample broke; tensile strength as the stress at which breaking occurred; and toughness was the area under the strain vs. stress curve. All reported values are the average from three measurements. 2.5. Membrane performance 2.5.1. Pure hydrocarbon permeance Heptane, n -hexane, and toluene were used as model oils for testing pure hydrocarbon permeance. Tests were conducted using stainless steel dead-end filtration cells with 400 mL total volume pressurized with five bar of nitrogen. For each test, 250 mL of the hydrocarbon were loaded into the cell, and the permeate weight was recorded during the experiment. Permeance ( J ) for each membrane was calculated according to eqn (2) : (2) where V is the permeated volume of hydrocarbon, A is the effective area of the membrane (3.46 cm 2 ), t is time, and Δ P is the pressure applied during filtration (five bar). Permeance was calculated with data from at least eight hours of flux in the steady state. The densities and viscosities of all hydrocarbons used in permeance tests are shown in Table S1. † The reported values are the averages from two membranes prepared independently under the same conditions. 2.5.2. Water-in-toluene emulsion separation Water-in-toluene emulsions were prepared for testing the membrane's performance according to a procedure described elsewhere. 50 First, 114 mL of toluene and 0.5 g of Span 80 as a surfactant were stirred at 500 rpm for five minutes. Afterward, 1 mL of water was added. The mixture was stirred at 1000 rpm for six hours and then loaded into the filtration cell. Five bar was applied during the separation experiment, and the emulsion was maintained under stirring at 500 rpm to ensure emulsion homogeneity during the test. Water rejection, also denoted as separation efficiency ( R ), was calculated from the water concentration in the permeate ( C p ) and in the feed ( C f ) expressed in ppm as shown in eqn (3) : (3) C p and C f were measured two times in a Karl Fischer coulometric titrator (Mettler Toledo). Before each set of measurements, a 1000 ppm Hydranal water standard was measured, obtaining less than 1% error in all cases. Water drop size in the emulsions was determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) in a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern). 3. Results and discussion Polyolefins are used on a large scale for disposable items and constitute an extensive part of plastic waste worldwide. Upcycling this plastic waste into useful materials, such as separation membranes, is a step forward toward more sustainable industrial practices. Nevertheless, the high chemical stability of polyolefins represents a challenge in terms of their processability since most membrane preparation methods are based on a solution process. Polyolefins are soluble only in a few selected solvents at high temperatures, where most of them are based on non-renewable sources. Green natural solvent alternatives have been seldomly identified ( Tables 1 and 2 ). Table 1 Solvent comparison between d -limonene, α-pinene and other solvents commonly used for polyolefin solubilization Solvent Source Boiling point Solubility in water Viscosity Vapor pressure Price Bio/fossil °C mg L −1 mPa s at 25 °C mmHg at 25 °C $ per kg d -Limonene Bio 178 13.8 0.846 1.64 10 α-Pinene Bio 156 2.49 2.100 4.75 3 Benzene Fossil 80 1–5 0.654 76.00 0.8 Toluene Fossil 111 526 0.554 28.40 0.8 Chlorobenzene Fossil 132 0.5 0.806 12.00 0.8 Isooctane Fossil 99 2.2 0.473 49.30 2 p -Xylene Fossil 138 165 0.603 8.84 0.5 Trichloroethylene Fossil 87 1.28 0.545 69.00 0.5 Table 2 Solvent comparison between α-pinene and other solvents reported for PP membrane fabrication (PP contents between 5 wt% and 25 wt%) via TIPS Solvent Source Boiling point Solubility in water Viscosity Vapor pressure Price CP T c
